- Servo drives continuously log **vibration signatures, current spectra, thermal profiles, and encoder health metrics**.
- Edge-AI algorithms predict **bearing failure, encoder degradation, and belt wear** weeks in advance — Industry 4.0 alignment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function of an Armature-Controlled DC Servomotor

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most repeated 7-mark problem** in KTU ESE papers on CNC servos. Master every step.

### Step 1: Write the Electrical (Armature) Equation

Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature circuit containing resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, and the back-EMF source $E_b$:

$$
V_a(t) = R_a \, i_a(t) + L_a \, \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + E_b(t)
$$

### Step 2: Write the Back-EMF in Terms of Angular Velocity

The rotating armature in the magnetic field generates a voltage opposing the applied voltage (Lenz's law):

$$
E_b(t) = K_b \, \omega(t)
$$

### Step 3: Write the Mechanical (Newton's Second Law for Rotation) Equation

Sum of torques on the rotor equals inertia times angular acceleration plus viscous friction:

$$
T_m(t) = J \, \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \, \omega(t) + T_L(t)
$$

### Step 4: Express Motor Torque in Terms of Armature Current

For a DC servomotor, electromagnetic torque is proportional to armature current (constant field flux):

$$
T_m(t) = K_t \, i_a(t)
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Laplace Domain (Zero Initial Conditions)

The differential equations transform as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a(s) &= (R_a + L_a s) \, I_a(s) + K_b \, \Omega(s) \\
K_t \, I_a(s) &= (J s + B) \, \Omega(s) + T_L(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Solve the Mechanical Equation for $I_a(s)$

Rearranging the second equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_t \, I_a(s) &= (J s + B) \, \Omega(s) + T_L(s) \\
I_a(s) &= \frac{(J s + B) \, \Omega(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Substitute $I_a(s)$ into the Electrical Equation

$$
V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B) \, \Omega(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t} + K_b \, \Omega(s)
$$

### Step 8: Group Terms Containing $\Omega(s)$

$$
V_a(s) = \Omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}{K_t} \right] + \frac{(R_a + L_a s) \, T_L(s)}{K_t}
$$

### Step 9: Solve for the Velocity Transfer Function (with $T_L = 0$)

Setting $T_L(s) = 0$ for the un-loaded plant:

$$
\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}
$$

This is the **standard first-order velocity transfer function** of a DC servomotor.

### Step 10: Expand the Denominator and Identify the Electrical and Mechanical Time Constants

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= R_a J s + R_a B + L_a J s^2 + L_a B s + K_t K_b \\
&= L_a J s^2 + (R_a J + L_a B) s + (R_a B + K_t K_b)
\end{aligned}
$$

Define:
- **Electrical time constant:** $\tau_e = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$
- **Mechanical time constant:** $\tau_m = \dfrac{J R_a}{K_t K_b + R_a B} \approx \dfrac{J R_a}{K_t K_b}$ (when viscous friction is small)
- **Motor gain:** $K_m = \dfrac{K_t}{R_a B + K_t K_b} \approx \dfrac{1}{K_b}$ (no-load speed constant)

The compact form becomes:

$$
\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{(\tau_e s + 1)(\tau_m s + 1)}
$$

### Step 11: Integrate to Obtain Position Transfer Function

Since $\theta(t) = \int \omega(t) \, dt$, the Laplace property $\Theta(s) = \dfrac{\Omega(s)}{s}$ yields:

$$
\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s \, (\tau_e s + 1)(\tau_m s + 1)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor $K_t K_b$ in the denominator of the velocity transfer function. This term represents the **back-EMF damping** and is *essential* for stability — without it the motor would accelerate indefinitely.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Error of a Type-1 Servo with Proportional Control

A simplified CNC position loop is modeled as:

- **Plant:** $\dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ (neglecting $\tau_e$ for clarity)
- **Controller:** $K_p$ (proportional)
- **Feedback:** unity ($H(s) = 1$)

### Step 1: Open-Loop Transfer Function

$$
G_{OL}(s) = \frac{K_p K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}
$$

### Step 2: Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Standard Formula)

$$
G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K_p K_m / \tau_m}{s^2 + s/\tau_m + K_p K_m / \tau_m}
$$

### Step 3: Identify the System as Type-1

The factor $1/s$ in $G_{OL}(s)$ means the system is **Type 1** — one pure integrator in the forward path.

### Step 4: Compute the Static Error Constants

- Position constant $K_{pos} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G_{OL}(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K_p K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)} = K_p K_m$
- Velocity constant $K_{vel} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G_{OL}(s) = K_p K_m$
- Acceleration constant $K_{acc} = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G_{OL}(s) = 0$

### Step 5: Apply Final Value Theorem to Compute Steady-State Errors

For a **step input** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p K_m} = \frac{1}{1 + K_{pos}}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A Type-1 system with **finite** $K_p$ produces a *non-zero* steady-state position error to a step input. This is why CNC position loops **must include integral action** (PI control) to drive the error to exactly zero.

For a **ramp input** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$ (constant-velocity contouring):

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_{vel}} = \frac{1}{K_p K_m}
$$

This non-zero ramp error is the **contouring error** during a CNC linear interpolation move — reducing it requires increasing $K_{vel}$, which is the **velocity-loop gain**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Following Error in CNC Contouring

During a linear cut at feedrate $F$ (mm/s), the lag in the position loop is governed by:

### Step 1: For a Ramp Command, Position Error = Velocity / Gain

$$
e_f = \frac{F}{K_v}
$$

where $K_v$ is the **velocity error constant** (1/s). For a Type-1 system, $K_v = K_p K_m$.

### Step 2: Define the Servo Gain Quantitatively

If the closed-loop velocity-loop gain is $K_v$ (typical values 30 to 100 s⁻¹ for machine tools), then:

$$
e_f = \frac{F}{K_v} = \frac{F}{K_v}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example

Given $F = 100$ mm/s and $K_v = 50$ s⁻¹:

$$
e_f = \frac{100}{50} = 2\ \text{mm}
$$

This means the **cutting tool lags 2 mm behind the commanded path** — unacceptable for precision machining. Hence high-precision CNC machines use:
- **Feed-forward (FF) control** — adds the velocity command directly to the controller output, effectively making the system Type-2 for ramp inputs and reducing $e_f$ to near zero.
- **Cross-coupled control (CCC)** — synchronizes two axes to minimize contour deviation on circular/curved paths.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Servomotor Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners love this as a 7-mark "compare-and-contrast" question. Use this exact structure.

| Feature | DC Servo | AC Servo (PMSM) | Stepper Motor | Linear Servo |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Commutation | Brushes (wear) | Electronic (brushless) | Open-loop, no commutation | Electronic |
| Feedback required | Yes (encoder) | Yes (encoder/resolver) | **No** | Yes (linear scale) |
| Holding torque at standstill | High | High | High (no excitation decay) | High |
| Max speed | Medium | High | Low–Medium | High |
| Efficiency | 70–80% | 90–95% | 60–70% | 90–95% |
| Heat dissipation | Armature (poor) | Stator (excellent) | Stator (good) | Stator (good) |
| Cost | Medium | High (dominant) | Low | Very high |
| Use in CNC | Legacy machines | **Standard today** | Low-cost hobby/3D print | Premium machines |
| Position error recovery | Automatic (closed loop) | Automatic | Can lose steps (open loop) | Automatic |
| Power density | Medium | High | Low | Very high |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Inertia Reflected Through a Gear Train

A CNC axis typically has a motor coupled to a ballscrew through a gearbox of ratio $N = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{\omega_{load}}$.

### Step 1: Kinetic Energy Equivalence

The total kinetic energy of the system must be conserved when reflected to the motor shaft:

$$
\frac{1}{2} J_{eq} \omega_{motor}^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_{motor} \omega_{motor}^2 + \frac{1}{2} J_{load} \omega_{load}^2
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Gear-Ratio Relationship

Using $\omega_{load} = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{N}$:

$$
J_{eq} = J_{motor} + \frac{J_{load}}{N^2}
$$

> **Engineering Implication:** Selecting a gearbox with $N > 1$ (speed-reducer) *dramatically* reduces the inertia reflected from the load to the motor, allowing a smaller motor to accelerate the same load. However, the reflected load torque is **amplified** by $N$, so the motor must supply $N \times$ more torque.

This trade-off is captured in the **inertia-matching rule** for high-performance servos:

$$
0.5 \leq \frac{J_{load,\ reflected}}{J_{motor}} \leq 5
$$

If the ratio falls outside this range, the drive will be either unstable (too high reflected inertia) or unresponsive (too low).
