### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Auto-ID Definition:** Automated identification of physical parts using machine-readable technologies — eliminates manual data entry.
- **Five primary families:** Optical (barcode, OCR, vision), Electromagnetic (RFID), Magnetic (stripe, MICR), Contact (smart cards, iButton), Surface Marking (laser, peen).
- **1D Barcode:** Width-modulated parallel bars; line-of-sight required; $\sim 30$ chars; cheap.
- **2D Barcode / Data Matrix:** Matrix of cells; finder pattern (L-shape) enables orientation independence; up to 2,335 alphanumeric chars; **Reed-Solomon ECC** recovers 25% damage.
- **Direct Part Marking (DPM):** Laser-etched or dot-peened Data Matrix on metal; mandatory in automotive, aerospace, medical-device industries.
- **RFID Coupling Types:** Inductive (LF/HF — near-field, coil-to-coil) and Backscatter (UHF/microwave — far-field, radar principle).
- **Active vs. Passive Tags:** Active = battery-powered, long range ($\sim 100$ m), expensive, finite life. Passive = battery-less, harvested energy, short range, cheap, unlimited life.
- **Friis Equation (UHF range):** $d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{th}}}$ — governs far-field RFID read range.
- **Inductive Coupling Voltage:** $V_{tag} \propto \omega B A N_2$ — frequency and coil-turns are the main design levers for HF RFID.
- **Read-Range Performance Order:** Active RFID (longest) $>$ Passive UHF $>$ HF/NFC $>$ 2D Barcode $>$ 1D Barcode (shortest).
- **Industry 4.0 Positioning:** Auto-ID is a foundational enabler of the *digital twin* and *traceability* of every physical artifact in a smart factory.
- **Critical Standards to Remember:** ISO/IEC 15415 (barcode print quality grading), ISO/IEC 16022 (Data Matrix), ISO/IEC 18000 (RFID air-interface), AS9132 (aerospace DPM), FDA UDI (medical device tracking).
- **Always mention these three advantages of RFID over barcodes** in any comparison: (i) no line-of-sight, (ii) multiple-tag simultaneous reading (anti-collision), (iii) rewritable memory.
- **Always mention these two advantages of DPM Data Matrix over printed barcodes:** (i) permanence (cannot be peeled or torn), (ii) survives painting, plating, and heat treatment.
- **Conversion discipline:** In all numerical questions, convert dBm $\rightarrow$ W before substituting into Friis equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Guided Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Guided Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automatic Guided Vehicle (AGV)** is a portable, self-powered, computer-controlled material handling system used to move loads along defined guide paths within an industrial environment, most commonly a warehouse, manufacturing plant, or distribution center. The system operates without an onboard operator and relies on a combination of sensors, controllers, and predefined guidance mechanisms (magnetic tape, laser, inertial, or vision-based) to perform pick-up, transport, and drop-off tasks autonomously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "An AGV is a driverless material-handling vehicle that follows a marked path or uses onboard sensors for navigation, communicating with a central dispatcher for task assignment, traffic control, and battery management."

The primary subsystems of any AGV include:
- **Navigation & Guidance Subsystem** (path detection and localization)
- **Drive & Locomotion Subsystem** (motors, wheels, steering)
- **Payload Interface Subsystem** (forks, conveyors, lift decks)
- **Control & Communication Subsystem** (onboard PLC, RF/WiFi link)
- **Safety Subsystem** (bumpers, E-stops, proximity scanners)
- **Power Subsystem** (battery, charging interface)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a bus following a fixed route in a city. The bus has a designated path, stops at predefined stations, and follows a schedule — but no driver is required to steer. The bus "knows" its stops through trackside beacons and onboard maps. An AGV is essentially the **industrial equivalent of such a driverless bus**, except instead of carrying passengers, it carries raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), or finished goods between workstations.

Another helpful analogy is the **automated lawn-mower robot** that roams within an invisible boundary, detecting obstacles and returning to its charging dock — the same autonomy principle scaled up to handle **payloads ranging from 50 kg to 50,000 kg** in industrial settings.

## 1.3 Key Industry Metrics & Standard References

The following performance and design metrics are commonly used in AGV literature and are **high-yield for KTU exams**:

| Metric | Typical Industrial Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Payload Capacity** | **50 kg – 50,000 kg** | Defines application class |
| Travel Speed | **0.5 – 2.0 m/s** | Determines throughput |
| Positioning Accuracy | **± 10 mm** (typical) | Critical for assembly lines |
| Battery Voltage | **24 V / 48 V / 80 V DC** | Standard industrial tiers |
| Operating Temperature | **0 °C to 40 °C** | Ambient requirement |
| Battery Type | Lead-acid / Li-ion | Affects charge cycle time |
| Communication Protocol | Wi-Fi / RFID / Zigbee | Determines integration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AGVs Matter in Industry 4.0:** AGVs form the **backbone of flexible manufacturing systems (FMS)** and are a foundational technology of the "Smart Factory." They enable **24×7 operations**, **labor cost reduction**, **zero-accident environments**, and **seamless integration with WMS (Warehouse Management Systems)** and MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of AGVs

AGVs can be classified along multiple dimensions — by **guidance method**, **load handling mechanism**, or **navigation intelligence**.

### A. Classification by Guidance / Navigation Technology

1. **Wire-Guided AGV** — Buried energized wire in floor produces a magnetic field detected by onboard sensors. Mature, low-cost, but installation is invasive.
2. **Magnetic Tape-Guided AGV** — Magnetic tape applied on floor surface. Easier to modify paths than wire.
3. **Optical/Reflective Tape AGV** — Vehicle tracks a painted line using photoelectric sensors. Cheap but tape degrades.
4. **Laser-Guided AGV (LGV)** — Rotating laser scans retroreflective targets on walls; triangulation yields ±10 mm accuracy. Industry gold standard.
5. **Inertial Navigation AGV** — Uses gyroscopes and wheel encoders; magnet markers in floor correct drift.
6. **Vision-Guided AGV (VGA)** — Onboard cameras and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) algorithms.
7. **GPS/Outdoor AGV** — Used in container terminals, ports, and large outdoor yards.

### B. Classification by Load-Handling Mechanism

- **Tow AGV** — Pulls multiple trailers behind it.
- **Unit Load AGV** — Carries discrete pallets or bins.
- **Forklift AGV** — Equipped with automated mast and forks.
- **Automated Cart (AGC)** — Small, lightweight version for e-commerce.
- **Hybrid AGV / AMR** — Combines AGV discipline with AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) flexibility.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of AGV Operation

The core operational logic of an AGV system can be broken into **5 sequential layers**:

1. **Dispatch Layer (Top)** — A central fleet manager (host computer / WMS) assigns a job: pick a payload at *Location A* and deliver to *Location B* by time *T*.
2. **Path Planning Layer** — The AGV's onboard controller determines the **optimal route** considering traffic, blocked zones, and battery state.
3. **Guidance & Localization Layer** — Sensors continuously verify the AGV is on the planned path. Any deviation triggers a **steering correction** at every control cycle (typically **20 – 50 ms**).
4. **Motion Control Layer** — Speed and acceleration profiles are applied using **PID controllers** so that the AGV accelerates, cruises, and decelerates smoothly.
5. **Safety Layer (Bottom)** — Bumper sensors, LiDAR safety scanners, and emergency-stop circuits operate **independently of software**, directly cutting motor power when triggered.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common essay-style question asks students to "describe the working principle of an AGV." Examiners expect the answer to be structured top-down: **Dispatch → Path → Guidance → Motion → Safety**. Memorize this 5-layer model.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

The following formulas govern AGV performance, scheduling, and design — **these are the most-likely-asked equations in KTU ESE**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Travel Time | $T = \dfrac{D}{V}$ | $D$ = Distance (m), $V$ = Velocity (m/s) |
| 2 | Acceleration Distance | $d_a = \dfrac{V^2}{2a}$ | $V$ = Final velocity, $a$ = Acceleration (m/s²) |
| 3 | Stopping Distance | $d_s = \dfrac{V^2}{2\mu g}$ | $\mu$ = Friction coeff., $g$ = 9.81 m/s² |
| 4 | Cycle Time | $T_c = T_{travel} + T_{load} + T_{unload} + T_{idle}$ | All in seconds |
| 5 | Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{jobs}}{T_{total}}$ | Jobs per hour |
| 6 | Minimum AGV Count (Banerjee's bound) | $N_{min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{R \cdot T_c}{3600} \right\rceil$ | $R$ = Jobs/hr demand, ceiling operator |
| 7 | Battery Run-Time | $t_b = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{load}}$ | $C$ = Capacity, $P$ = Power draw (W) |
| 8 | Traction Force Required | $F_t = m \cdot a + \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$ | $m$ = Mass, $\mu_r$ = Rolling resistance |
| 9 | Turning Radius (Ackermann) | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan \delta}$ | $L$ = Wheelbase, $\delta$ = Steer angle |
| 10 | Pallet-Lift Energy | $W = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | $h$ = Lift height (m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tip for Solving Problems:** Always convert units to **SI base units (m, s, kg, m/s²)** before plugging into equations. KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks for unit inconsistency.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Sector:** AGVs deliver subassemblies (engines, dashboards) to the assembly line with **± 10 mm precision**, allowing robotic arms to dock directly without human intervention.
- **Warehousing & E-Commerce:** Amazon's Kiva/Hercules robots reduce order-pick time by **40–60 %**.
- **Hospitals:** Pneumatic-tube and AGV hybrids transport linens, meals, and pharmaceuticals.
- **Ports & Container Terminals:** GPS-guided AGVs move 40-ft ISO containers at speeds up to **6 m/s**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Number of AGVs to Meet Production Demand

This is a classic KTU problem (Banerjee & George, 1985 — formula well-established). The derivation below shows **every algebraic step**, suitable for a 7-mark derivation question.

**Given:**
- Production demand $R$ = number of jobs (deliveries) required per hour
- Cycle time of each AGV $T_c$ = total time for one complete delivery (in seconds)

**To find:** Minimum integer number of AGVs $N_{min}$ required to meet demand.

**Step 1 — Define the throughput of a single AGV.**

One AGV completes $\dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ jobs per hour, since $T_c$ is in seconds and one hour has 3600 seconds.

$$\text{Throughput}_{single} = \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad \text{(jobs/hour)}$$

**Step 2 — Define total throughput required.**

The system must complete at least $R$ jobs per hour. With $N$ identical AGVs operating in parallel, total throughput is $N$ times the single-unit throughput.

$$\text{Throughput}_{total} = N \cdot \frac{3600}{T_c}$$

**Step 3 — Impose the feasibility constraint.**

For demand to be met:

$$N \cdot \frac{3600}{T_c} \;\geq\; R$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$.**

$$N \;\geq\; \frac{R \cdot T_c}{3600}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the ceiling operator.**

Since $N$ must be a whole number (you cannot deploy half an AGV), we take the smallest integer greater than or equal to the right-hand side:

$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{R \cdot T_c}{3600} \right\rceil$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A plant needs 30 deliveries/hour. Each AGV cycle is 240 s. Then $N_{min} = \lceil (30 \times 240) / 3600 \rceil = \lceil 2.0 \rceil = \mathbf{2}$ AGVs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Braking Distance for a Safety-Compliant AGV

A KTU 2024 question often asks: *"An AGV moves at 1.5 m/s. Coefficient of friction is 0.3. Find the minimum stopping distance."*

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law on the AGV during braking.**

The only horizontal force is friction (assuming pure rolling):

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Set the friction force equal to the mass times deceleration.**

$$m \cdot a = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = \mu \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Use the kinematic equation of motion (final velocity squared).**

With initial velocity $V$ and final velocity $0$:

$$0^2 = V^2 - 2 \cdot a \cdot d_s$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for stopping distance $d_s$.**

$$d_s = \frac{V^2}{2 a} = \frac{V^2}{2 \mu g}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numerical values.** With $V = 1.5$ m/s, $\mu = 0.3$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$d_s = \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{2.25}{5.886} = 0.382 \;\text{m}$$

**Therefore, the minimum safety stopping distance is approximately 382 mm.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** KTU examiners and AGV safety standards (ANSI/ITSDF B56.5) require a **safety margin of at least 1.5×** this calculated value, i.e., the physical bumper should engage at ≥ 0.6 m from any obstacle.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Traction Force & Motor Power Sizing

To size the drive motor, the designer must compute the **peak traction force** the motor must deliver.

**Step 1 — Identify all horizontal forces opposing motion.**

- Inertial force: $F_i = m \cdot a$
- Rolling resistance: $F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$

**Step 2 — Sum the forces.**

$$F_t = F_i + F_{rr} = m \cdot a + \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the mass.**

$$F_t = m \left( a + \mu_r \cdot g \right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert traction force to motor power using $P = F \cdot V$.**

$$P_{motor} = F_t \cdot V = m \cdot V \left( a + \mu_r \cdot g \right)$$

**Step 5 — Add a safety margin and efficiency factor.** A typical industrial design includes a 25 % safety factor and assumes 85 % drivetrain efficiency:

$$P_{design} = \frac{1.25 \cdot P_{motor}}{\eta_{drivetrain}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Check:** An AGV with $m = 500$ kg, $a = 0.5$ m/s², $\mu_r = 0.02$, $V = 1.0$ m/s, $\eta = 0.85$ gives $P_{design} \approx 943$ W. Designers would select a **1 kW BLDC motor** — a standard industrial size.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Guidance Method

| Guidance Method | Accuracy | Installation Cost | Path Flexibility | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wire Guidance | ± 25 mm | Very High (invasive) | Very Low | High |
| Magnetic Tape | ± 25 mm | Low | Medium | High |
| Optical Tape | ± 10 mm | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Laser Guidance (LGV) | ± 10 mm | High | High | **Very High** |
| Inertial + Magnets | ± 25 mm | Medium | High | Medium |
| Vision / SLAM | ± 30 mm | Very High | Very High | Rising (2024+) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** The question papers now emphasize **LGV and AMR (Vision/SLAM)** because these represent the current industrial state-of-the-art. Memorize the ± accuracy figures and typical applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AGV

The diagram below maps the **interactions between all major subsystems** of an AGV — its structure mirrors the way KTU examiner diagrams are drawn in model answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    centralCtrl[Central Control Unit - PLC] --> navSys[Navigation System]
    centralCtrl --> driveSys[Drive and Motion System]
    centralCtrl --> commSys[Communication System]
    centralCtrl --> payloadSys[Payload Handling System]
    navSys --> laserScanner[Laser Scanner Module]
    navSys --> imuSensor[Inertial Measurement Unit]
    navSys --> wheelEncoder[Wheel Encoder]
    navSys --> rfidReader[RFID Floor Reader]
    driveSys --> motorDriver[Motor Driver]
    driveSys --> wheelAssembly[Wheel Assembly Unit]
    driveSys --> steerActuator[Steering Actuator]
    commSys --> wifiModule[WiFi Radio Module]
    commSys --> hostLink[Host Computer Link]
    payloadSys --> liftMechanism[Lift or Conveyor Mechanism]
    payloadSys --> forkAssembly[Fork or Pallet Mechanism]
    powerSys[Power Supply Subsystem] --> battPack[Battery Pack]
    powerSys --> chargePort[Charging Port]
    battPack --> centralCtrl
    battPack --> driveSys
    battPack --> payloadSys
    safetySys[Safety Subsystem] --> bumperSensor[Front and Rear Bumpers]
    safetySys --> proxScanner[Proximity Safety Scanner]
    safetySys --> eStopBtn[Emergency Stop Button]
    safetySys --> motorDriver
```

## 4.2 Navigation & Dispatch Flow (Operational Sequence)

This flowchart shows **how an AGV processes a job from dispatch to delivery** — a high-yield diagram for KTU descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Host Computer Dispatches Job]) --> jobAck[Onboard Controller Receives Task]
    jobAck --> pathPlan[Path Planning Algorithm Activated]
    pathPlan --> trafficCheck{Traffic Zone Clear?}
    trafficCheck -- Yes --> navActive[Guidance Sensors Activate]
    trafficCheck -- No --> waitState[Enter Wait Queue]
    waitState --> trafficCheck
    navActive --> motionCtrl[Motion Controller Activated]
    motionCtrl --> monitor[Continuous Sensor Monitoring Loop]
    monitor --> obstCheck{Obstacle Detected?}
    obstCheck -- Yes --> eBrake[Apply Emergency Brake]
    eBrake --> monitor
    obstCheck -- No --> destReach[Destination Reached]
    destReach --> loadOp[Perform Load or Unload Operation]
    loadOp --> battCheck{Battery Low?}
    battCheck -- Yes --> goCharge[Navigate to Charging Dock]
    battCheck -- No --> jobAck
    goCharge --> resumeOp[Resume Operation After Full Charge]
    resumeOp --> jobAck
```

## 4.3 Guidance Method Comparison (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    agv[AGV Guidance Methods] --> contactBased[Contact Based]
    agv --> nonContactBased[Non Contact Based]
    contactBased --> wireGuid[Wire Guidance]
    contactBased --> magTape[Magnetic Tape]
    contactBased --> optTape[Optical Tape]
    nonContactBased --> laserGuid[Laser Guidance LGV]
    nonContactBased --> inertial[Inertial Navigation]
    nonContactBased --> visionGuid[Vision SLAM Based]
    nonContactBased --> gpsGuid[GPS Based Outdoor]
    laserGuid --> highAcc[High Accuracy plus 10 mm]
    visionGuid --> flexRoute[Flexible Route Adaptation]
    gpsGuid --> outdoorUse[Outdoor Yard Applications]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of an AGV," examiners expect at least **six labeled blocks**: Power, Control, Navigation, Drive, Communication, and Safety. Missing the **Safety subsystem** is the #1 reason students lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Automatic Guided Vehicle" and list any four of its primary subsystems.**

**Model Answer:**

An Automatic Guided Vehicle (AGV) is a self-powered, computer-controlled material handling vehicle that moves autonomously along defined paths in an industrial environment to transport goods between workstations, without an onboard human operator.

The four primary subsystems are:
1. **Navigation and Guidance Subsystem** — sensors that detect and follow the predefined path.
2. **Drive and Locomotion Subsystem** — motors, wheels, and steering mechanisms.
3. **Control and Communication Subsystem** — onboard PLC and RF/WiFi link to host.
4. **Power Subsystem** — battery pack and charging interface.

*(A complete answer may also include Payload Interface and Safety Subsystems.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between AGV and AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot).**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | AGV | AMR |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Fixed (wire/tape/laser) | Dynamic (SLAM-based) |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Infrastructure | Requires guides | Map-based, no guides |
| Accuracy | ± 10 mm | ± 30 – 50 mm |
| Cost | Lower initial | Higher initial, lower install |
| Best For | High-volume repetitive tasks | Dynamic, mixed-SKU environments |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule:** Part B Module 2 questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must attempt **one full question** from the choice.

---

### QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **Laser-Guided Vehicle (LGV)** with a neat block diagram. Mention its accuracy, working range, and two industrial applications.

**Model Solution:**

A Laser-Guided Vehicle uses a **rotating laser scanner mounted on top of the AGV** that emits laser pulses. These pulses reflect off **retroreflective targets** (passive corner-cube reflectors) fixed at known positions on the walls or columns of the facility.

**Working Principle (4-step process):**
1. The laser head rotates at high speed (e.g., 25 Hz) and emits a horizontal fan-beam laser.
2. The onboard photo-detector receives the reflected beam from each target.
3. Using the **angle of arrival** of the reflected beam and the **known coordinates of the target**, the AGV's onboard computer applies **triangulation** to compute the AGV's exact $(x, y)$ position and heading $(\theta)$.
4. The controller compares the computed pose to the desired path and issues steering commands at every control cycle (typically 20–50 ms).

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**
Laser Scanner → Triangulation Processor → Path Comparator → Steering Motor → Wheels (with feedback loop via wheel encoders).

**Specifications:**
- **Accuracy:** ± 10 mm positional, ± 0.1° angular.
- **Working Range:** Up to **200 m radius** from the target cluster.
- **Update Rate:** 25–50 Hz.

**Industrial Applications:**
1. Automotive assembly line parts delivery.
2. High-bay warehouse pallet handling.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing cell requires **45 deliveries per hour**. Each AGV takes a cycle time of **300 seconds** to complete one delivery (travel + load + unload). Calculate the **minimum number of AGVs** required.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Demand rate $R = 45$ jobs/hour
- Cycle time $T_c = 300$ s

**Step 1 — Apply Banerjee's formula:**

$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{R \cdot T_c}{3600} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{45 \cdot 300}{3600} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{13500}{3600} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.75 \rceil$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ceiling operator:**

$$N_{min} = 4 \text{ AGVs}$$

**Answer: A minimum of 4 AGVs is required to meet the production demand.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to apply the **ceiling operator** and write "3.75 AGVs" — a numerical answer is mandatory because you cannot have a fractional vehicle. **Always round UP to the next whole integer.**

---

### QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **major subsystems of an Automatic Guided Vehicle system**. Explain the function of the **central control station** and the **vehicle management software**.

**Model Solution:**

An AGV system consists of two main tiers — the **vehicle tier** and the **station tier**.

**Vehicle Tier (onboard):**
- Navigation System (laser, magnetic, inertial, or vision sensors)
- Drive System (motor, gearbox, wheels, brakes)
- Payload Interface (lift, conveyor, or forks)
- Communication Module (Wi-Fi, RFID)
- Safety System (bumpers, scanners, E-stop)
- Power System (battery, BMS)

**Station Tier (offboard / fixed):**
- Central Control Station (host computer)
- Vehicle Management Software (VMS)
- Charging Stations
- Traffic-control beacons

**Functions of the Central Control Station:**
1. **Job Dispatching** — Assigns pick-up and delivery tasks to AGVs based on real-time availability.
2. **Traffic Management** — Prevents collisions by managing intersection priorities and blocked-zone allocation.
3. **Battery Management** — Routes low-battery AGVs to charging docks and brings standby AGVs online.
4. **Performance Logging** — Records cycle times, downtime, and exception events for KPI tracking.

**Functions of Vehicle Management Software (VMS):**
- Real-time fleet visualization on a graphical map.
- Path optimization algorithms (Dijkstra/A* for shortest path).
- Integration with WMS / MES via APIs.
- Alarm and exception handling.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An AGV moving at **2 m/s** needs to stop before a wall. If the coefficient of friction between the wheel and floor is **0.25**, calculate the **minimum stopping distance**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Velocity $V = 2$ m/s
- Friction coefficient $\mu = 0.25$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Compute the deceleration:**

$$a = \mu \cdot g = 0.25 \times 9.81 = 2.4525 \;\text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the kinematic equation:**

$$d_s = \frac{V^2}{2a}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$d_s = \frac{(2)^2}{2 \times 2.4525} = \frac{4}{4.905}$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$d_s \approx 0.815 \;\text{m}$$

**Answer: The minimum theoretical stopping distance is approximately 0.82 m. Considering the 1.5× safety factor mandated by ANSI/ITSDF B56.5, the AGV safety system must be triggered at least 1.22 m before the obstacle.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is to **confuse the friction coefficient with the rolling-resistance coefficient**. Braking distance uses $\mu$ (sliding/kinetic friction), while traction force calculation uses $\mu_r$ (rolling resistance, typically 0.01 – 0.03 for rubber tires on concrete). Mixing them up costs full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this section for the night before your KTU ESE — it is a one-page revision summary of the entire topic.**

- **AGV Definition:** Self-powered, computer-controlled, driverless material-handling vehicle. *(Always mention "driverless" — losing this word costs a mark.)*
- **Six Subsystems:** Navigation, Drive, Payload, Communication, Safety, Power.
- **Five-Layer Operation Model:** Dispatch → Path → Guidance → Motion → Safety.
- **Most-Asked Guidance Types:** Wire, Magnetic Tape, Laser (LGV), Inertial, Vision (SLAM).
- **LGV Accuracy:** ± 10 mm — **memorize this number**.
- **Banerjee's Minimum AGV Formula:** $N_{min} = \lceil R \cdot T_c / 3600 \rceil$ — **always use the ceiling operator**.
- **Stopping Distance Formula:** $d_s = V^2 / (2 \mu g)$ — **always show the unit conversion step**.
- **Traction Force Formula:** $F_t = m(a + \mu_r g)$ — distinguish between $\mu$ (braking) and $\mu_r$ (rolling).
- **Industry-Relevant Standards:** ANSI/ITSDF B56.5, ISO 3691-4.
- **AMR vs AGV:** AGV = fixed path; AMR = SLAM-based, dynamic.
- **Key Industrial Users:** Amazon (Kiva/Hercules), Toyota, BMW assembly lines, Maersk ports.
- **Payload Range to Mention:** **50 kg – 50,000 kg**.
- **Communication Protocols:** Wi-Fi (802.11 a/b/g/n/ac), RFID (active/passive), Zigbee.
- **Safety Rule:** Always include the 1.5× safety margin on calculated stopping distance.
- **Latest Trend (2024+):** Vision-guided AMRs replacing tape-guided AGVs in greenfield warehouses.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not confuse **"AGV" with "AS/RS"** — AS/RS is fixed racking with cranes, AGV is mobile on the floor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Systems for Automated Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Systems for Automated Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Automation** and the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a **Sensor System for Automated Inspection** is defined as an integrated arrangement of transducers, signal-conditioning circuits, data-acquisition hardware, and decision algorithms that collectively perform non-contact or contact-based measurement, evaluation, and verification of manufactured components, assemblies, or processes **without continuous human intervention**.

The system converts physical phenomena (dimensional, optical, thermal, electromagnetic, or chemical) into quantifiable electrical signals, which are then processed to determine conformance against pre-defined engineering tolerances and quality benchmarks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Automated inspection is the *backbone of Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)* and is directly mapped to **CO2**: *Apply sensor and vision systems for quality control in automated production environments.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology Standardized to KTU / ISO 9001 Vocabulary

| Term | KTU / ISO Definition |
|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Degree of closeness of a measurement to the true value of the measurand. |
| **Precision** | Degree of repeatability (random scatter) under unchanged conditions. |
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change in the input that produces a perceptible output change. |
| **Sensitivity** | Ratio of change in output to the corresponding change in input ($\Delta y / \Delta x$). |
| **Repeatability** | Ability to produce the same output for the same input over multiple trials. |
| **Hysteresis** | Maximum difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions. |
| **Linearity** | Constancy of the slope of the calibration curve across the operating range. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Full-Body Check-up**
>
> Imagine a patient walking into a modern diagnostic centre. The doctor does **not** rely on a single instrument. Instead, a *suite of sensors* is deployed:
>
> - **X-Ray machine** → checks bone structure (analogous to **vision/structural sensors**)
> - **Thermometer** → checks body temperature (analogous to **thermal sensors**)
> - **ECG pads** → check electrical activity of the heart (analogous to **eddy-current / electromagnetic sensors**)
> - **Blood pressure cuff** → checks fluid pressure (analogous to **pressure / force sensors**)
> - **Stethoscope** → listens to acoustic signatures (analogous to **acoustic emission / ultrasonic sensors**)
>
> The **automated inspection system in a factory** works exactly the same way: a *multi-modal sensor suite* gathers complementary information about a part, and a central processor (the "doctor's brain") fuses the data to issue a Pass/Reject decision. The fundamental shift is that the doctor's subjective judgement is replaced by **algorithmic, deterministic, and traceable evaluation** — which is the very essence of Industry 4.0 quality assurance.

## 1.4 Why Automated Inspection? — The Industrial Justification

1. **Zero-defect manufacturing (Six Sigma / ZDM):** Human inspectors have a typical effectiveness of only **80%–85%**; automated systems routinely exceed **99.9%**.
2. **Sub-second cycle times:** Modern inline sensors can complete inspections in **< 50 ms**, enabling 100% inspection at full production throughput.
3. **Quantitative data:** Automated systems produce **digitally stored, trendable** data — a prerequisite for Statistical Process Control (SPC).
4. **Hazardous environments:** Sensors can operate in **high-temperature, radioactive, or explosive atmospheres** where humans cannot.
5. **Microscale measurements:** Sub-micron tolerances (e.g., semiconductor wafers) are physically impossible to verify by eye.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Sensor Systems

Automated inspection sensors are best classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — By Physical Principle of Operation

1. **Optical / Vision Sensors**
   - 2-D CCD/CMOS cameras, line-scan cameras, smart cameras
   - Operating principle: photoconductivity / photovoltaic effect
   - Applications: surface defect detection, dimensional gauging, OCR, colour verification

2. **Laser-Based Sensors**
   - Laser triangulation, laser scanners (LIDAR), laser interferometers
   - Operating principle: coherent light + triangulation or interferometry
   - Applications: profile measurement, displacement to ±0.1 µm, 3-D scanning

3. **Inductive / Eddy-Current Sensors**
   - Operating principle: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction
   - Applications: crack detection in conductive materials, position sensing, material sorting

4. **Capacitive Sensors**
   - Operating principle: change in capacitance $C = \varepsilon A / d$
   - Applications: thickness of non-metals, liquid-level, run-out of rotating shafts

5. **Ultrasonic Sensors**
   - Operating principle: Time-of-Flight (TOF) of acoustic waves ($v = \sqrt{E/\rho}$)
   - Applications: bond-line inspection, thickness gauging, weld inspection

6. **X-Ray / Computed Tomography (CT) Sensors**
   - Operating principle: differential absorption (Beer–Lambert law)
   - Applications: internal defect detection (voids, inclusions), BGA solder inspection

7. **Force / Tactile / Piezoelectric Sensors**
   - Operating principle: piezoelectric effect $F = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot A^{-1}$
   - Applications: assembly verification, force-controlled robotic inspection

8. **Chemical / Gas Sensors**
   - Operating principle: chemi-resistive, electrochemical, or spectroscopic reaction
   - Applications: leak detection, contamination, freshness monitoring in food packaging

### Axis B — By Mode of Contact

- **Contact sensors:** touch probes (CMMs), pneumatic gauging
- **Non-contact sensors:** vision, laser, eddy-current, capacitive, ultrasonic

## 2.2 Operating Principle — A Generic Sensor Model

Every sensor system can be represented by the canonical signal-flow chain:

$$
\text{Physical Stimulus} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transducer Element} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Signal Conditioner} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ADC} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Processor}
$$

The transducer's static behaviour is modelled by the **linear first-order equation**:

$$
y = K \cdot x + b
$$

where $y$ is the output (V, mA, or digital count), $K$ is the **sensitivity** (V/unit), $x$ is the input measurand, and $b$ is the **offset / bias**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these equations. They cover **> 90 % of numerical/derivation questions** asked in KTU Module-2 ESE papers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ | V/unit or counts/unit | Slope of calibration line |
| 2 | Resolution | $R = \text{FSR} / 2^{n}$ | engineering unit | For an $n$-bit ADC |
| 3 | Full-Scale Range | $\text{FSR} = y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ | unit | Determined by sensor range |
| 4 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ | dB | Higher = better |
| 5 | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}(y_{\max} / y_{\min,\text{detect}})$ | dB | |
| 6 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Farad | Used in capacitive sensors |
| 7 | Strain-gauge relation | $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | $G_f$ = gauge factor (~2 for metal) |
| 8 | Ultrasonic depth | $d = v \cdot t / 2$ | m | Factor 2 = round-trip |
| 9 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Coulomb | $d_{33}$ = charge coefficient |
| 10 | Beer–Lambert absorption | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | W/m² | X-ray / optical absorption |
| 11 | Laser triangulation distance | $x = f \cdot L / d_{i}$ | m | $f$ = focal length, $L$ = base, $d_i$ = image shift |
| 12 | Eddy-current depth of penetration | $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho / (\mu \omega)}$ | m | Skin depth |
| 13 | Thermocouple Seebeck EMF | $V = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S(T)\,dT$ | V | $S$ = Seebeck coefficient |
| 14 | Measurement uncertainty (RSS) | $u_c = \sqrt{\sum u_i^2}$ | unit | Root-sum-square |
| 15 | % Accuracy (full-scale) | $\%\text{FS} = (\Delta_{\max} / \text{FSR}) \times 100$ | % | |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** 100 % weld inspection using phased-array ultrasonic + eddy-current arrays on every car body.
- **Semiconductor industry:** Optical + X-ray inspection at sub-micron tolerances for wafer and chip packaging.
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Machine-vision verification of blister-pack tablets and barcode traceability.
- **Aerospace industry:** Computed Tomography (CT) of turbine blades to detect internal porosity.
- **Food & beverage:** Multi-spectral vision for grading fruits by ripeness, foreign-body detection via X-ray.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are framed as **worked analytical problems** that demonstrate the exact manipulation style KTU examiners reward in **14-mark questions**. Each transition is justified in words and algebraically expanded.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensor Resolution from ADC Bit Width

### Problem Statement
A digital laser triangulation sensor has an analog front-end with a Full-Scale Range (FSR) of **10.000 mm** and is interfaced to a **16-bit unipolar ADC**. Compute:
(a) the theoretical resolution $R$,
(b) the smallest detectable displacement,
(c) the resolution if the ADC is upgraded to 24-bit.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Recall the canonical resolution equation.**

The Least Significant Bit (LSB) of an ideal $n$-bit converter is the smallest resolvable increment:

$$
R = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^{n}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute FSR = 10 mm, $n = 16$.**

$$
R = \frac{10}{2^{16}} \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the denominator.**

$$
2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 4: Compute the ratio.**

$$
R = \frac{10}{65{,}536} \;\text{mm} = 1.5259 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to micrometres (multiply by 1000).**

$$
R = 0.15259 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 6: Part (c) — repeat for $n = 24$.**

$$
2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216
$$

$$
R_{24} = \frac{10}{2^{24}}\;\text{mm} = 5.96 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{mm} = 0.000596\;\mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 7: Ratio of resolutions.**

$$
\frac{R_{16}}{R_{24}} = \frac{2^{24}}{2^{16}} = 2^{8} = 256
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{R_{16\text{-bit}} = 0.153\;\mu\text{m}, \quad R_{24\text{-bit}} = 0.000596\;\mu\text{m}, \quad \text{Improvement factor} = 256}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Upgrading the ADC yields a 256-fold improvement in resolution — sufficient for inspecting wafer-level features.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitive Sensor Sensitivity to Gap Change

### Problem Statement
A parallel-plate capacitive sensor is used to measure shaft run-out. Nominal gap $d_0 = 1.0\;\text{mm}$, plate area $A = 100\;\text{mm}^2$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air). Find:
(a) nominal capacitance,
(b) sensitivity $K = dC/dd$,
(c) % change in $C$ for a $\Delta d = 5\;\mu\text{m}$ shaft displacement.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Write the governing equation for a parallel-plate capacitor.**

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}$.

**Step 2: Convert SI units (CRITICAL — KTU students frequently lose marks here).**

$$
A = 100\;\text{mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
d_0 = 1.0\;\text{mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Compute nominal capacitance.**

$$
C_0 = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(1 \times 10^{-4})}{1 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
C_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-13}\;\text{F} = 0.8854\;\text{pF}
$$

**Step 4: Derive sensitivity by differentiating $C$ w.r.t. $d$.**

$$
C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot d^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^{2}} = -\frac{C_0}{d_0}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation.**

$$
K = -\frac{8.854 \times 10^{-13}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = -8.854 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{F/m} = -0.8854\;\text{pF/mm}
$$

**Step 6: Compute $\Delta C$ for $\Delta d = 5\;\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}$.**

$$
\Delta C = K \cdot \Delta d = (-0.8854\;\text{pF/mm})(0.005\;\text{mm}) = -4.427 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{pF}
$$

**Step 7: Percentage change.**

$$
\% \Delta C = \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{-4.427 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8854} \times 100 = -0.50\;\%
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{C_0 = 0.885\;\text{pF},\quad K = -0.885\;\text{pF/mm},\quad \%\Delta C = -0.50\%}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Capacitive sensors are **highly sensitive to small gap changes** but produce very small absolute capacitance values, demanding a precision charge-amplifier front-end. The negative sign indicates that an *increase* in gap *decreases* the capacitance (inverse relationship).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SNR and Dynamic Range of an Eddy-Current Crack-Detection Probe

### Problem Statement
A 100 kHz eddy-current probe delivers a signal amplitude of $V_s = 50\;\text{mV}$ when traversing a 0.5 mm deep surface crack, with a noise floor of $V_n = 200\;\mu\text{V}$. Compute:
(a) SNR in dB,
(b) dynamic range if the maximum crack-free signal is $V_{\max} = 4.0\;\text{V}$ and minimum detectable signal equals the noise floor.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Express the SNR definition.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{V_s}{V_n} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{50{,}000}{200} = 250
$$

**Step 3: Apply the logarithm.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(250)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(250) = \log_{10}(2.5) + 2 = 0.3979 + 2 = 2.3979
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \times 2.3979 = 47.96\;\text{dB} \approx 48\;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4: Dynamic range.**

$$
\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\max}}{V_{n,\min}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4.0}{200 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{V_{\max}}{V_{n,\min}} = \frac{4.0}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(20{,}000) = 20 \times 4.301 = 86.02\;\text{dB}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR} \approx 48\;\text{dB},\quad \text{DR} \approx 86\;\text{dB}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 48 dB SNR is acceptable for moderate crack-detection but not for sub-millimetre flaws in aerospace alloys; engineering practice demands SNR $\geq$ 60 dB, achieved by **lock-in amplification** and **narrow-band filtering**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Sensor

| Inspection Requirement | Recommended Sensor | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Surface scratch on steel | Laser triangulation + machine vision | Non-contact, µm resolution, fast |
| Internal void in aluminium casting | Ultrasonic TOF / X-ray CT | Volumetric inspection |
| Weld bead profile | Laser line scanner | 3-D profile, 360° coverage |
| Shaft concentricity | Capacitive / eddy-current | Sub-µm gap measurement |
| PCB solder joint | X-ray / AOI | Hidden joint inspection |
| Bottling line fill level | Vision + IR sensor | Hygienic, non-contact |
| Bearing race crack | Eddy-current array | Sensitive to surface + sub-surface cracks |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are Mermaid `flowchart`/`graph` constructs, validated against the V10 *Mermaid Compilation Safeguards*. Every node ID is alphanumeric + prefix to avoid reserved-keyword collisions. Labels contain plain uppercase alphanumeric text only.

---

## 4.1 Generic Automated Inspection System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PART UNDER INSPECTION] --> B[MULTI MODAL SENSOR BANK]
    B --> B1[VISION CAMERA CCD]
    B --> B2[LASER TRIANGULATION]
    B --> B3[EDDY CURRENT PROBE]
    B --> B4[ULTRASONIC TRANSDUCER]
    B1 --> C[SIGNAL CONDITIONING UNIT]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER ADC]
    D --> E[FEATURE EXTRACTION AND IMAGE PROCESSING MODULE]
    E --> F[DECISION ENGINE PASS OR REJECT]
    F --> G[PASS OUTPUT TO NEXT STATION]
    F --> H[REJECT OUTPUT TO RECYCLE BIN]
    E --> I[DATABASE AND SPC TRENDS]
    I --> J[QUALITY DASHBOARD FOR OPERATOR]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A part arrives on a conveyor, is interrogated by a **multi-modal sensor bank**, signals are amplified/filtered, digitised, fed to a **feature-extraction module** (where algorithms like edge detection, blob analysis, or FFT operate), and the **decision engine** compares features against tolerances. Pass parts continue; rejects are diverted; all data is archived for SPC.

---

## 4.2 Sensor Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START] --> Q1{IS THE PART METALLIC}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{INTERNAL OR SURFACE DEFECT}
    Q1 -- NO  --> Q3{IS SURFACE TEXTURE CRITICAL}
    Q2 -- SURFACE --> E1[EDDY CURRENT OR LASER]
    Q2 -- INTERNAL --> U1[ULTRASONIC OR XRAY CT]
    Q3 -- YES --> V1[MACHINE VISION 2D OR 3D]
    Q3 -- NO  --> C1[CAPACITIVE OR VISION]
    E1 --> OUT[INSPECTION SYSTEM DESIGNED]
    U1 --> OUT
    V1 --> OUT
    C1 --> OUT
```

---

## 4.3 Signal-Conditioning and Processing Pipeline (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[RAW SENSOR SIGNAL MV LEVEL NOISE] --> A1[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER GAIN 100 TO 1000]
    A1 --> A2[ACTIVE LOW PASS FILTER CUTOFF 10 KHZ]
    A2 --> A3[DC OFFSET REMOVAL]
    A3 --> A4[ADC 16 BIT 100 KSPS]
    A4 --> P1[DIGITAL FILTER MOVING AVERAGE]
    P1 --> P2[FEATURE EXTRACTION PEAK PEAK RMS]
    P2 --> P3[THRESHOLD COMPARATOR]
    P3 --> D1[DECISION PASS OR REJECT]
    P3 --> D2[DATA LOGGER TO SPC DATABASE]
```

**Why this architecture matters in KTU board examinations:** Examiners frequently test whether students can identify the *order* of signal-processing blocks. The correct sequence is **Amplify → Filter → Offset → Digitise → Process → Decide**, never the reverse.

---

## 4.4 Closed-Loop In-Process Inspection (Feedback to Production)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[CNC MACHINE] --> P1[WORKPIECE]
    P1 --> S1[INLINE SENSOR STATION]
    S1 --> Q[QUALITY DATA]
    Q --> C[CONTROLLER PLC]
    C -- ADJUST FEED --> M1
    Q --> D[DASHBOARD SPC]
```

**Interpretation:** Inspection data is *not just* for Pass/Reject — it is fed back to the **CNC controller** to adjust feed-rate, spindle speed, or tool-offset in real time. This is the **closed-loop quality paradigm** central to Industry 4.0 and a frequent 14-mark KTU question.
