# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
**Differentiate between *accuracy* and *precision* in the context of an automated inspection sensor. Give one industrial example where high precision is required but moderate accuracy is acceptable.**

**Model Answer:**
*Accuracy* is the closeness of a measured value to the *true* value, whereas *precision* is the closeness of repeated measurements to *each other* (low scatter, regardless of true value). An automated inspection system may be highly precise (tight grouping) but biased (offset from true value), in which case it can be calibrated for accuracy.
**Example:** A laser interferometer measuring piston-ring diameter on a high-speed production line may show very high precision (±0.1 µm repeatability) but require periodic calibration against a master artefact to maintain absolute accuracy.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
**List any three non-contact sensor principles used in industrial inspection and state one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Sensor Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine vision (CCD/CMOS) | Surface defect detection on painted car bodies |
| 2 | Laser triangulation | Shaft run-out measurement on rotating spindles |
| 3 | Eddy current | Surface crack detection in aluminium aircraft skins |

*(Any three valid combinations earn full credit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice A) — Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a generic automated inspection system and explain the function of each block.**
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The architecture consists of the following sequential blocks (see Section 4.3 for the diagram):

1. **Sensor / Transducer:** Converts the physical measurand (length, light intensity, eddy-current loss) into a low-level electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning (Amplification + Filtering):** Boosts the weak mV-level signal to a 0–10 V range and removes high-frequency noise using active low-pass filters.
3. **ADC:** Converts the conditioned analog signal to a digital word of $n$ bits (typically $n = 12, 16, 24$).
4. **Feature Extraction / Image Processing:** Extracts metrics like edge coordinates, blob area, FFT peak frequency, or RMS value.
5. **Decision Engine:** Compares extracted features against tolerance limits stored in a recipe and issues Pass / Reject.
6. **Data Logger / SPC Module:** Archives each inspection result for traceability and statistical analysis.
7. **Actuator Interface:** Routes rejects to a bin via pneumatic diverter; updates HMI.

**(b) A capacitive sensor used for shaft run-out has the following specifications: plate area $A = 200\;\text{mm}^2$, nominal gap $d = 0.5\;\text{mm}$, dielectric air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). Compute (i) the nominal capacitance, (ii) the sensitivity, and (iii) the change in capacitance for a run-out of $\Delta d = 10\;\mu\text{m}$.**
**(7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Convert to SI units.**
$A = 200 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^2$, $d = 5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}$.

**Step 2: Compute nominal capacitance.**

$$
C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(2 \times 10^{-4})}{5 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
C_0 = 3.5416 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F} = 3.54\;\text{pF}
$$

**Step 3: Sensitivity.**

$$
K = -\frac{C_0}{d} = -\frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = -7.083 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{F/m}
$$

Equivalently, $K = -7.083\;\text{pF/m} = -0.00708\;\text{pF/mm}$.

**Step 4: Change for $\Delta d = 10\;\mu\text{m}$.**

$$
\Delta C = K \cdot \Delta d = (-7.083 \times 10^{-9})(10 \times 10^{-6}) = -7.083 \times 10^{-14}\;\text{F} = -0.0708\;\text{pF}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{C_0 = 3.54\;\text{pF},\quad K = -7.08\;\text{pF/m},\quad \Delta C = -0.0708\;\text{pF}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to **convert mm² → m²** and lose 2 marks.
> 2. Some omit the **negative sign** in sensitivity — always retain it because capacitive sensors exhibit an *inverse* relationship with gap.
> 3. Do not write "C = ε₀A/d" without specifying that ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m. Examiners specifically test the value of constants.

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) — Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the working principle of an eddy-current sensor. List two industrial applications.**
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
An eddy-current sensor consists of a coil excited by a high-frequency AC (typically 100 kHz – 10 MHz). When a conductive target is brought near the coil, **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction** induces circulating eddy currents in the target. These eddy currents produce a secondary magnetic field that opposes the primary field, thereby changing the coil's **impedance** $Z = R + jX_L$. The change is measured with a bridge or oscillator circuit and correlated with the target's distance, conductivity, or flaw geometry.

**Key equations governing behaviour:**

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\mu \omega}} \quad \text{(skin depth)}
$$

$$
Z = R + j\omega L \quad \text{(coil impedance)}
$$

**Applications (any two valid examples):**

1. **Surface crack detection** in aircraft aluminium skins (subsurface cracks up to a depth $\approx \delta$).
2. **Material sorting** by conductivity (e.g., distinguishing brass from copper on a recycling line).
3. **Non-contact displacement measurement** of rotating shafts in CNC spindles.
4. **Coin validation** based on conductivity signatures.

**(b) With a suitable diagram, describe a machine-vision-based inspection station for a bottle-filling line. Discuss the role of illumination, camera type, and image-processing steps.**
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
[ Conveyor ] --> [ Bottle ] --> [ Backlight + Camera ] --> [ Frame Grabber ] --> [ Image Processing PC ] --> [ PLC / Diverter ]
```

**Subsystem Details:**

1. **Conveyor & Bottle Positioning:** A precise encoder-trigger synchronises the camera exposure to the bottle's arrival under the inspection window.
2. **Illumination (CRITICAL):** Structured back-lighting (LED panel) creates a high-contrast silhouette, allowing precise fill-level measurement and detection of foreign bodies. For surface defects, *diffuse dome lighting* or *directional ring lighting* is chosen.
3. **Camera Type:** A **CCD line-scan camera** is preferred for cylindrical bottles because the bottle rotates as it moves, building a full 360° image. **Area-scan CMOS** cameras are used for label inspection from the side.
4. **Image-Processing Steps:**
   - **Pre-processing:** Median filtering to remove salt-and-pepper noise.
   - **Segmentation:** Thresholding + edge detection (Sobel or Canny) to isolate fill-level and cap region.
   - **Feature extraction:** Measure blob area, edge coordinates, centroid offset.
   - **Classification:** Compare against golden template; OCR reads batch code.
5. **Decision and Actuation:** PLC activates a pneumatic diverter to send rejects to a quarantine bin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** describe the camera alone — always explain the *role of illumination*. Lighting is the single most common examiner trap.
> 2. Distinguish **CCD vs CMOS** explicitly (CCD = global shutter, low noise; CMOS = high speed, on-chip processing).
> 3. In OCR / classification questions, name the algorithm: *template matching, neural network, or SVM* — vague phrases like "computer decides" fetch zero marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 must-remember facts for KTU Module-2 Sensor Systems for Automated Inspection:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Sensor system = transducer + signal conditioner + ADC + processor + decision logic.
- ☐ **Two classification axes:** Physical principle (optical, electromagnetic, acoustic, chemical) and contact mode (contact / non-contact).
- ☐ **Resolution equation:** $R = \text{FSR} / 2^n$ — always state $n$ and FSR.
- ☐ **SNR:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ — convert both voltages to the **same unit** first.
- ☐ **Capacitive sensor equation:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$; sensitivity is **negative** and **inverse-square** with $d$.
- ☐ **Eddy-current skin depth:** $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho / (\mu \omega)}$ — higher frequency probes surface cracks; lower frequency probes deeper flaws.
- ☐ **Ultrasonic TOF:** $d = v \cdot t / 2$ — factor 2 because of round-trip.
- ☐ **Laser triangulation:** $x = fL/d_i$ — image shift $d_i$ in the detector is inversely proportional to the measured distance.
- ☐ **Beer–Lambert:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — for X-ray and optical absorption sensing.
- ☐ **Piezoelectric:** $Q = d_{33} F$ — used in force/pressure/tactile sensors.
- ☐ **Multi-modal sensing:** combine complementary principles (e.g., vision + eddy-current) to overcome individual sensor weaknesses.
- ☐ **Closed-loop inspection:** inspection data fed back to CNC/PLC for adaptive process control — Industry 4.0 paradigm.
- ☐ **Six Sigma mandate:** automated inspection is justified by human-inspector effectiveness of only ~85 % vs systems at > 99.9 %.
- ☐ **Always convert units** to SI (m, m², F) **before** substituting into any KTU formula — examiners verify this.
- ☐ **Always include the negative sign** for sensitivities derived from inverse relationships (capacitive, photoconductive).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Gauging and Size Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Gauging and Size Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automatic Gauging** is a non-contact or in-process measurement methodology wherein the dimensional, geometric, or form characteristics of a manufactured workpiece are measured, evaluated, and recorded by an automated system—without continuous human intervention—using transducers, signal-conditioning electronics, and decision logic circuits. The system converts a physical dimension into a quantifiable electrical, pneumatic, or optical signal that can be compared against pre-defined **tolerance limits**.

**Size Control Systems** (often called **Adaptive Control of Dimensions — ACD**) are closed-loop feedback control systems that automatically regulate a manufacturing process (e.g., turning, grinding, honing) by continuously measuring the workpiece size and modifying the process variables (feed, depth of cut, infeed rate) so that the finished part lies within specified tolerance bands.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OERAT613 – Industrial Automation, Module 2), this topic covers:  
> • The principle of automatic gauging  
> • In-process and post-process gauging techniques  
> • Pneumatic, electrical (LVDT), and optical gauging systems  
> • Feedback control for dimensional accuracy  
> • Signal conditioning, comparator circuits, and actuator interface  
> • Statistical size control (SPC integration)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Core Terminology — Set Definitions for the Exam**  
> - **Nominal Size (N):** The exact design dimension stated on the engineering drawing.  
> - **Upper Specification Limit (USL):** The maximum permissible dimension (N + upper tolerance).  
> - **Lower Specification Limit (LSL):** The minimum permissible dimension (N − lower tolerance).  
> - **Tolerance Band (T):** The arithmetic range between USL and LSL, expressed as **T = USL − LSL**.  
> - **Go Gauge:** A limit gauge whose dimension equals the **LSL** for a shaft (or USL for a hole).  
> - **No-Go Gauge:** A limit gauge whose dimension equals the **USL** for a shaft (or LSL for a hole).  
> - **Repeatability (R):** The variation observed when the same operator measures the same part repeatedly under identical conditions.  
> - **Reproducibility:** Variation arising when different operators, instruments, or labs measure the same part.  
> - **Accuracy:** The closeness of a measured value to the **true value**.  
> - **Precision:** The closeness of two or more measurements to each other (clustering).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are baking biscuits and you want every biscuit to be **exactly 7 cm** in diameter. If you measure each one manually with a ruler (manual gauging), you will be slow, tired, and inconsistent. Now imagine a smart conveyor belt:

1. Each biscuit rolls under a **laser/photo-eye scanner** (the sensor).
2. The scanner instantly measures the diameter and sends an electrical signal to a small computer.
3. If a biscuit is **too big**, a robotic pusher (actuator) shoves it into a "rework" bin. If **too small**, it is rejected. If **just right**, it is allowed to pass.
4. The whole process happens automatically, **24/7, with millisecond speed**, and never gets bored or tired.

This is the essence of **Automatic Gauging and Size Control**: a sensor "sees" the part, a controller "decides" whether it is good, and an actuator "acts" upon that decision—often in **real-time**, while the part is still being machined.

> [!TIP]  
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**  
> • Cylinder bore sizing in automotive engines (sub-micron accuracy)  
> • Bearing race grinding (ball-bearing manufacturing)  
> • Rolling mill flatness and thickness control in steel plants  
> • Wafer diameter control in semiconductor fabs  
> • CNC turning adaptive feed control  
> • High-volume ball bearing, piston pin, and injector nozzle production

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants, Standards & Reference Metrics

The following constants, ratios, and standard metrics are essential for KTU exams:

- **Back-pressure proportionality constant (K_p):** typical range **0.5 – 0.95** for pneumatic gauges.
- **LVDT sensitivity (K_s):** typically **0.5 – 5 V/mm** (linear range).
- **Pneumatic nozzle stand-off distance:** **0.2 – 0.5 mm**.
- **Process Capability Index (Cpk):** industry target **≥ 1.33** (capable), **≥ 1.67** (highly capable).
- **Six-Sigma Quality Level:** **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.
- **Standard air pressure for pneumatic gauging:** **0.2 – 0.5 MPa** (typically 2–5 bar).
- **Measurement resolution target for precision gauging:** **0.1 μm** for Class-1 metrology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Automatic Gauging Systems

Automatic gauging systems can be classified along three primary axes:

### A. By the Stage of Measurement
1. **Pre-process gauging:** Inspects raw material before machining (e.g., incoming bar-stock diameter).
2. **In-process gauging:** Measures the workpiece **while it is being machined**—enables real-time feedback.
3. **Post-process gauging:** Inspects the finished part (typically 100% inspection on transfer lines).

### B. By the Physical Principle
1. **Pneumatic Gauging** — uses air pressure / back-pressure proportional to gap.
2. **Electrical Gauging** — uses LVDT, capacitive, inductive, or eddy-current transducers.
3. **Optical Gauging** — uses laser triangulation, image correlation, or interferometry.
4. **Mechanical / Contact Gauging** — uses servo-driven probes with linear encoders.

### C. By the Function in the Control Loop
1. **Indicative (open-loop) gauging** — measures and displays only.
2. **Control (closed-loop) gauging** — measures and feeds back to the process controller.

## 2.2 Operating Principles — Why and How

### 2.2.1 Pneumatic Gauging — The "Why"
A **constant air supply** is fed through a restrictor into a nozzle facing the workpiece. The gap `z` between the nozzle tip and the workpiece forms a **variable orifice**. The smaller the gap, the higher the back-pressure `P_b` inside the chamber (continuity principle + Bernoulli).

> [!NOTE]  
> **How it works (intuitive):**  
> Imagine blowing through a thin straw at a wall. The closer your straw tip is to the wall, the harder you must blow to push the same air out. The "back-pressure" in your mouth rises. Replace your mouth with a regulated air supply and the wall with a workpiece — you have a **pneumatic gauge**.

**Quantitative relationship:**

$$P_b \;=\; \frac{Q \cdot R_n}{1 + \left(\dfrac{A_n}{A_g}\right)^{2}}$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate
- $R_n$ = nozzle resistance
- $A_n$ = nozzle exit area
- $A_g$ = annular gap area (function of gap $z$)

The **gap area** is given by:

$$A_g = \pi \, D_n \, z$$

where $D_n$ is the nozzle diameter and $z$ is the gap distance.

### 2.2.2 Electrical Gauging — LVDT Principle
A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** has a primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former. A movable ferromagnetic core couples flux between them. When the core is exactly centered, the two secondary EMFs are equal and opposite, yielding a **net output of 0 V**. Displacement of the core produces a differential voltage proportional to displacement.

$$V_{\text{out}} \;=\; V_{s1} - V_{s2} \;=\; K_s \cdot x$$

where $K_s$ is the **LVDT sensitivity (V/mm)** and $x$ is the core displacement from the null position.

### 2.2.3 Optical Gauging — Laser Triangulation
A laser diode projects a beam onto the workpiece. The reflected spot is imaged onto a **position-sensitive detector (PSD)** at a known angle. The spot's lateral position on the PSD is geometrically related to the workpiece's distance from the gauge.

$$z \;=\; \frac{f \cdot \Delta x}{d \pm \Delta x}$$

where:
- $f$ = focal length of the receiving lens
- $\Delta x$ = spot displacement on the PSD
- $d$ = baseline distance between laser and detector

## 2.3 Closed-Loop Size Control — Why and How

A **size control loop** operates as a classical **negative feedback control system**:

1. **Measurement (Sensor):** Gauge reads the in-process dimension.
2. **Comparison (Comparator):** Error signal $e(t) = $ (Measured) − (Setpoint) is generated.
3. **Decision (Controller):** PID (or PI) algorithm computes the correction.
4. **Action (Actuator):** Servo valve / stepper motor adjusts the cutting tool position.
5. **Disturbance rejection:** Compensates for tool wear, thermal drift, material hardness variation.

> [!TIP]  
> **Why a closed loop?** Open-loop control assumes the tool removes material at a known, constant rate. In reality, **tool wear, thermal expansion, and material inhomogeneity** alter the removal rate, causing dimensional drift. Closed-loop gauging measures the actual outcome and corrects in real time.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $T \;=\; \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$ | Total tolerance band | mm or μm |
| 2 | $e \;=\; x_{\text{measured}} - x_{\text{true}}$ | Absolute measurement error | mm |
| 3 | $\%e \;=\; \dfrac{e}{x_{\text{true}}} \times 100$ | Percentage error | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{\text{out}} \;=\; K_s \cdot x$ | LVDT output voltage | V ; $K_s$ in V/mm |
| 5 | $P_b \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{z^n},\; n \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2$ | Pneumatic back-pressure vs gap | Pa ; n is gauge-specific |
| 6 | $A_g \;=\; \pi D_n z$ | Annular gap area for pneumatic nozzle | m² |
| 7 | $z \;=\; \dfrac{f \, \Delta x}{d \pm \Delta x}$ | Laser triangulation distance | m |
| 8 | $C_p \;=\; \dfrac{T}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability (centered) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process capability (off-centered) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G(t) \;=\; K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | PID controller transfer function | — |
| 11 | $\sigma_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{cal}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{rep}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{bias}}^{2}}$ | Combined measurement uncertainty | mm |
| 12 | $\text{Sensitivity} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{\Delta x}$ | Gauge sensitivity | V/mm or mV/μm |
| 13 | $\text{Repeatability} \;=\; k \cdot \sigma_{\text{rep}}$ | GR&R repeatability | mm (k = 5.15) |
| 14 | $\dot{R} \;=\; \dfrac{dr}{dt} \;=\; -K_c \, e(t)$ | Tool infeed rate correction | mm/s |
| 15 | $\text{NEMA gauge factor} \;=\; 0.002$ inch resolution | Standard for plug-pin gauges | inches |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In the table, every absolute-value / divider symbol is rendered with `\vert` or `\mid` so the KTU answer-sheet markdown never breaks. All formulas above are derived from first principles or from ISO 9001 / ISO 10360 metrological conventions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bearing manufacturing:** LVDT-based in-process gauges in race-grinding machines hold roundness to **< 0.5 μm**.
- **Fuel-injector nozzle production:** Pneumatic gauging with magnifier ratios up to **10 000 : 1** detects variations of **0.1 μm**.
- **Rolling mills:** Optical / laser thickness gauges in cold-rolling mills hold strip thickness to **±5 μm** at 30 m/s line speed.
- **Adaptive control in CNC turning:** ACD systems reduce scrap by 30–60% and improve tool life by 25–50%.
- **Automotive cylinder blocks:** Laser triangulators verify bore diameter post-honing to ±0.01 mm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pneumatic Back-Pressure vs Gap (Bernoulli + Continuity)

We start from first principles. A pneumatic gauge has a **constant-pressure supply** $P_s$ feeding air through a fixed orifice of area $A_f$ (restrictor) into a chamber, then out through a nozzle of area $A_n$ facing the workpiece. The annular gap of height $z$ around the nozzle tip forms a variable restrictor of area $A_g = \pi D_n z$.

**Step 1 — Mass continuity (steady state):**
The mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ entering the chamber equals the mass flow rate leaving:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{in}} \;=\; \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$$

**Step 2 — Incompressible flow through restrictor (orifice equation):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{in}} \;=\; C_d \, A_f \, \sqrt{2 \rho \, (P_s - P_b)}$$

where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient and $\rho$ is the air density.

**Step 3 — Flow through the nozzle–workpiece gap (annular orifice):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{out}} \;=\; C_d \, A_g \, \sqrt{2 \rho \, (P_b - P_a)}$$

where $P_a$ is the ambient pressure (≈ 101.325 kPa).

**Step 4 — Equate the two flow rates (steady-state continuity):**

$$C_d \, A_f \, \sqrt{2 \rho \, (P_s - P_b)} \;=\; C_d \, A_g \, \sqrt{2 \rho \, (P_b - P_a)}$$

Cancel the common constants $C_d$ and $\sqrt{2\rho}$:

$$A_f \, \sqrt{P_s - P_b} \;=\; A_g \, \sqrt{P_b - P_a}$$

**Step 5 — Square both sides:**

$$A_f^{\,2} \, (P_s - P_b) \;=\; A_g^{\,2} \, (P_b - P_a)$$

**Step 6 — Expand and collect terms in $P_b$:**

$$A_f^{\,2} P_s - A_f^{\,2} P_b \;=\; A_g^{\,2} P_b - A_g^{\,2} P_a$$

$$A_f^{\,2} P_s + A_g^{\,2} P_a \;=\; P_b (A_f^{\,2} + A_g^{\,2})$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the back-pressure $P_b$:**

$$P_b \;=\; \frac{A_f^{\,2} \, P_s + A_g^{\,2} \, P_a}{A_f^{\,2} + A_g^{\,2}}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $A_g = \pi D_n z$:**

$$P_b \;=\; \frac{A_f^{\,2} \, P_s + \pi^{2} D_n^{2} \, z^{2} \, P_a}{A_f^{\,2} + \pi^{2} D_n^{2} \, z^{2}}$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the result:**

- When $z \to \infty$ (workpiece very far): $P_b \to P_a$ (atmospheric, no restriction).  
- When $z \to 0$ (workpiece touching nozzle): $P_b \to P_s$ (full supply pressure).  
- The relationship is **monotonic and non-linear** with a steep slope in the operating range of $z = 0.05$ to $0.5$ mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Differentiating the relationship** gives the **gauge sensitivity** $S_p$:

$$S_p \;=\; \frac{dP_b}{dz} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi^{2} D_n^{2} \, z \, A_f^{\,2} \, (P_s - P_a)}{(A_f^{\,2} + \pi^{2} D_n^{2} z^{2})^{2}}$$

This equation is the **design equation for pneumatic gauge amplification** — by selecting small $A_f$ and small $D_n$, the sensitivity is dramatically increased.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LVDT Output Voltage from Coupled Inductor Analysis

A centre-tapped secondary LVDT can be modelled as two coupled inductors with mutual inductance $M$ and self-inductances $L_1, L_2$.

**Step 1 — Write the secondary EMFs:**

$$E_{s1} \;=\; -j \omega M_1 \, I_p \quad \text{and} \quad E_{s2} \;=\; -j \omega M_2 \, I_p$$

where $I_p$ is the primary current and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the excitation frequency.

**Step 2 — At the null (center) position:** $M_1 = M_2$, so the differential output is zero.

$$V_{\text{out}} \;=\; E_{s1} - E_{s2} \;=\; -j \omega I_p (M_1 - M_2) \;=\; 0$$

**Step 3 — When the core is displaced by $x$ to the right:** $M_1$ increases by $\Delta M$ and $M_2$ decreases by $\Delta M$. Assuming a linear field profile (valid in the linear range):

$$\Delta M \;=\; K_m \, x$$

where $K_m$ is the mutual-inductance slope (H/m).

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$V_{\text{out}} \;=\; -j \omega I_p (K_m x - (-K_m x)) \;=\; -j \omega I_p \cdot 2 K_m x$$

**Step 5 — Take the magnitude (and assume the rectifier/demodulator recovers the envelope):**

$$\vert V_{\text{out}} \vert \;=\; 2 \omega I_p K_m \, \vert x \vert \;=\; K_s \, \vert x \vert$$

**Step 6 — Therefore the proportionality constant is:**

$$K_s \;=\; 2 \omega I_p K_m$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Why this matters in size control:** The linearity of $K_s$ over the LVDT's stroke means the controller can be tuned assuming a **constant gain**, simplifying the PID design. Non-linearity outside the linear range is rejected by calibrating the LVDT in its "sweet zone".

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$ from Gaussian Process Statistics

A manufacturing process produces dimensions with a normal (Gaussian) distribution:

$$f(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Define the process spread:** $6\sigma$ spans 99.73% of the population (3σ on each side of the mean).

**Step 2 — Centred process capability:**

$$C_p \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Off-centred (real-world) capability:** Define the distance from the mean to each specification limit:

$$d_{\text{upper}} \;=\; \text{USL} - \mu, \quad d_{\text{lower}} \;=\; \mu - \text{LSL}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the one-sided capabilities:**

$$C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{d_{\text{upper}}}{3\sigma} \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

$$C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{d_{\text{lower}}}{3\sigma} \;=\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 5 — The overall $C_{pk}$ is the worst-case (smaller) of the two:**

$$C_{pk} \;=\; \min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl})$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

- $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → process is **incapable** (parts fall outside spec).  
- $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → marginally capable.  
- $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ → capable.  
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → highly capable (Six-Sigma territory).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Gauging Technique Selection

| Selection Criterion | Pneumatic | LVDT (Electrical) | Optical (Laser) | Mechanical Probe |
|---------------------|-----------|--------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Resolution (μm)** | 0.1 | 0.5 | 0.05 | 1.0 |
| **Range (mm)** | 0.05 – 0.5 | 0.1 – 50 | 1 – 200 | 0.1 – 100 |
| **Contact vs Non-contact** | Non-contact | Contact | Non-contact | Contact |
| **Sensitivity amplification** | 10 000 : 1 typical | 100 : 1 typical | 1000 : 1 typical | 1 : 1 |
| **Suitability for soft parts** | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Poor (deformation) |
| **Suitability for in-process grinding** | Limited (clog risk) | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| **Environmental robustness** | Excellent | Good | Moderate (dust) | Moderate |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| **Best application** | Plug-pin, soft parts | Linear displacement | Profile, thickness | General purpose |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Pneumatic Gauge Sensitivity

**Problem (KTU standard):** A pneumatic gauge has a nozzle diameter $D_n = 2$ mm, restrictor area $A_f = 0.5$ mm², supply pressure $P_s = 4$ bar (gauge), ambient $P_a = 1$ bar (gauge), operating gap $z = 0.2$ mm. Compute the back-pressure and the sensitivity at this operating point.

**Step 1 — Compute the gap area:**

$$A_g \;=\; \pi D_n z \;=\; \pi (2)(0.2) \;=\; 1.2566 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the back-pressure formula (in bar gauge):**

$$P_b \;=\; \frac{A_f^{2} P_s + A_g^{2} P_a}{A_f^{2} + A_g^{2}}$$

Numerator:

$$(0.5)^{2}(4) + (1.2566)^{2}(1) \;=\; (0.25)(4) + (1.5790)(1) \;=\; 1.0 + 1.5790 \;=\; 2.5790$$

Denominator:

$$(0.5)^{2} + (1.2566)^{2} \;=\; 0.25 + 1.5790 \;=\; 1.8290$$

Therefore:

$$P_b \;=\; \frac{2.5790}{1.8290} \;=\; 1.410 \, \text{bar (gauge)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sensitivity at this gap:**

$$S_p \;=\; \frac{dP_b}{dz} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi^{2} D_n^{2} \, z \, A_f^{2} (P_s - P_a)}{(A_f^{2} + \pi^{2} D_n^{2} z^{2})^{2}}$$

Substituting $D_n = 2$ mm, $z = 0.2$ mm, $A_f = 0.5$ mm², $P_s - P_a = 3$ bar:

Numerator:

$$2 \pi^{2} (2)^{2} (0.2) (0.5)^{2} (3) \;=\; 2(9.8696)(4)(0.2)(0.25)(3) \;=\; 11.844$$

Denominator:

$$((0.5)^{2} + \pi^{2}(2)^{2}(0.2)^{2})^{2} \;=\; (0.25 + 1.579)^{2} \;=\; (1.829)^{2} \;=\; 3.345$$

Therefore:

$$S_p \;=\; \frac{11.844}{3.345} \;=\; 3.541 \, \text{bar/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:** A 1 μm change in gap produces a $3.541 \times 10^{-3}$ bar change in back-pressure. With a 0–5 bar pressure transducer of 0.001 bar resolution, the gauge can resolve gap changes of about **0.28 μm** — a typical "magnification" of 3500 : 1 over the gap itself.

> [!TIP]  
> **Examiner's heuristic:** Whenever a question gives $D_n, z, A_f, P_s$, expect the student to derive the back-pressure from continuity + Bernoulli, then compute $S_p$ by differentiation. Memorize the structural form — not the algebra.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Size Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of an automatic gauging and size control system (e.g., in a CNC grinding or turning machine).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Workpiece on Machine Table] --> A2[Machining Process Infeed]
    A2 --> A3[Workpiece Outer Diameter]
    A3 --> B1[In-Process Gauge Sensor LVDT or Pneumatic Nozzle]
    B1 --> B2[Signal Conditioner Amplifier and Filter]
    B2 --> B3[Comparator Subtractor]
    C1[Setpoint USL or Nominal] --> B3
    B3 --> B4[Error Signal e of t]
    B4 --> C2[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    C2 --> C3[Actuator Drive Servo Valve or Stepper]
    C3 --> A2
    B3 --> D1[Display Unit Digital Readout]
    B3 --> D2[Alarm and Reject Mechanism Go No Go]
    D2 --> E1[Reject Bin]
    D2 --> E2[Accept Conveyor]
    B1 --> F1[Statistical Process Control SPC Data Logger]
    F1 --> F2[Cpk Computation]
    F2 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The diagram follows the **measurement → comparison → decision → actuation** paradigm of a classical negative feedback loop. Notice the **two output paths from the comparator**: one to the controller (closed-loop control) and one to the SPC data logger (offline quality analysis). This dual-path is the **defining feature** of a modern size-control system.

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Diagram — Pneumatic Gauge Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Regulated Air Supply P_s] --> R1[Fixed Restrictor A_f]
    R1 --> C1[Plenum Chamber Pressure P_b]
    C1 --> N1[Nozzle Exit A_n]
    C1 --> GAP[Annular Gap to Workpiece A_g function of z]
    N1 --> GAP
    GAP --> ATM[Atmosphere P_a]
    C1 --> T1[Pressure Transducer]
    T1 --> SC1[Signal Conditioner]
    SC1 --> CMP1[Comparator]
    SP1[Setpoint P_set] --> CMP1
    CMP1 --> DSP1[Display]
    CMP1 --> AL1[Alarm and Reject]
    GAP -.->|z change by tool wear| C1
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Adaptive Control of Dimensions (ACD)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Measurement Subsystem]
        M1[Probe LVDT or Pneumatic] --> M2[ADC and Amplifier]
        M2 --> M3[Digital Dimension x_n]
    end
    subgraph S2[Decision Subsystem]
        M3 --> D1[Error Calculator]
        D2[Reference Trajectory r_n] --> D1
        D1 --> D3[PID Control Law]
        D3 --> D4[Infeed Rate Correction delta_R]
    end
    subgraph S3[Actuation Subsystem]
        D4 --> A1[Servo Drive]
        A1 --> A2[Ball Screw or Hydraulic Actuator]
        A2 --> A3[Tool Infeed Motion]
        A3 --> WP[Workpiece Machined Surface]
    end
    WP --> M1
    D3 --> LOG[Data Logger and SPC]
    LOG --> CPK[Cpk Monitor and Report]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{What is the workpiece material?}
    Q1 -->|Soft Rubber Plastic| R1[Use Pneumatic Gauge]
    Q1 -->|Hard Steel Cast Iron| R2[Use LVDT or Laser]
    Q2{What is the production rate?}
    Q2 -->|Below 100 parts per hour| R3[Use Contact Probe]
    Q2 -->|Above 500 parts per hour| R4[Use Pneumatic or Laser]
    Q3{Is the measurement in-process or post-process?}
    Q3 -->|In-process in grinding| R5[Use LVDT in Touch Probe]
    Q3 -->|Post-process on conveyor| R6[Use Laser Triangulation]
    R1 --> FINAL[Selected Automatic Gauge]
    R2 --> FINAL
    R3 --> FINAL
    R4 --> FINAL
    R5 --> FINAL
    R6 --> FINAL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The above block diagrams are **examination-ready**. In KTU 14-mark questions, you are expected to draw a labelled block diagram of a size control loop. Memorize the four essential blocks — **Sensor → Comparator → Controller → Actuator** — and the feedback line returning to the workpiece.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]  
**Define automatic gauging. List any two advantages of in-process gauging over post-process gauging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Automatic gauging is the technique of measuring the dimensional or geometric features of a workpiece using a transducer-based system without continuous human intervention, where the measurement is converted into an electrical / pneumatic / optical signal for display, recording, or feedback.

**Advantages of in-process gauging (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Real-time feedback:** Dimensional errors are detected and corrected *during* machining, reducing scrap and rework.
2. **Higher accuracy under varying conditions:** Compensates for tool wear, thermal expansion, and material inhomogeneity in real time.
3. (Optional) Eliminates the need for a separate inspection station, reducing floor space and cycle time.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**Distinguish between accuracy and precision in the context of dimensional measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Closeness of a measurement to the true value | Closeness of repeated measurements to each other |
| **Affected by (1 mark)** | Systematic errors (calibration, bias) | Random errors (noise, repeatability) |
| **Improvement method (1 mark)** | Re-calibration, error compensation | Better signal conditioning, averaging, environmental control |

A gauge can be **precise but inaccurate** (clustered around the wrong value) or **accurate but imprecise** (scattered around the true value). The ideal gauge is **both accurate and precise**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]  
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Module carries 14-mark questions with internal choice (either-or). Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **automatic size control system** used in a CNC grinding machine. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pneumatic gauge has a nozzle diameter $D_n = 1.5$ mm, restrictor area $A_f = 0.3$ mm², supply gauge pressure $P_s = 3$ bar, and ambient pressure $P_a = 1$ bar (gauge). Compute the **back-pressure $P_b$** when the gap $z = 0.15$ mm, and the **sensitivity $dP_b/dz$** at this operating point. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** The student is expected to draw the standard four-block feedback loop:

`Workpiece → In-process Gauge Sensor → Signal Conditioner + Comparator → PID Controller → Servo Actuator → Tool Infeed → back to Workpiece.`

**Explanation of each block (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **In-process Gauge Sensor (LVDT / Pneumatic / Optical):** Continuously senses the actual diameter of the workpiece during grinding. Output is a voltage / pressure proportional to dimension.
2. **Signal Conditioner + Comparator:** Filters noise, amplifies the signal, and subtracts the setpoint to produce an error signal $e(t) = x_{\text{measured}} - x_{\text{setpoint}}$.
3. **PID Controller:** Computes the corrective infeed rate $\dot{R} = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ that will null the error.
4. **Servo Actuator + Tool Infeed Mechanism:** A hydraulic / servo-motor / stepper system physically retracts or advances the grinding wheel based on the controller's command, closing the loop.

**Conclusion (optional, 0 mark):** The feedback loop ensures the finished part lies within tolerance despite tool wear and thermal drift.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Given:** $D_n = 1.5$ mm, $A_f = 0.3$ mm², $P_s = 3$ bar, $P_a = 1$ bar (gauge), $z = 0.15$ mm.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Gap area:**

$$A_g \;=\; \pi D_n z \;=\; \pi \times 1.5 \times 0.15 \;=\; 0.7069 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Substitute into back-pressure equation:**

$$P_b \;=\; \frac{A_f^{2} P_s + A_g^{2} P_a}{A_f^{2} + A_g^{2}}$$

Compute squares:

$$A_f^{2} \;=\; 0.09 \, \text{mm}^4, \quad A_g^{2} \;=\; 0.4997 \, \text{mm}^4$$

Numerator:

$$(0.09)(3) + (0.4997)(1) \;=\; 0.27 + 0.4997 \;=\; 0.7697$$

Denominator:

$$0.09 + 0.4997 \;=\; 0.5897$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Back-pressure:**

$$P_b \;=\; \frac{0.7697}{0.5897} \;=\; 1.305 \, \text{bar (gauge)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Sensitivity formula:**

$$S_p \;=\; \frac{2 \pi^{2} D_n^{2} z A_f^{2} (P_s - P_a)}{(A_f^{2} + \pi^{2} D_n^{2} z^{2})^{2}}$$

Numerator:

$$2 \times 9.8696 \times 2.25 \times 0.15 \times 0.09 \times (3-1) \;=\; 2 \times 9.8696 \times 2.25 \times 0.15 \times 0.09 \times 2$$

$$=\; 0.5995 \, \text{bar·mm}^{3}$$

Denominator:

$$(0.09 + \pi^{2} \times 2.25 \times 0.0225)^{2} \;=\; (0.09 + 0.4997)^{2} \;=\; (0.5897)^{2} \;=\; 0.3477 \, \text{mm}^{8}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Final sensitivity:**

$$S_p \;=\; \frac{0.5995}{0.3477} \;=\; 1.724 \, \text{bar/mm}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Physical interpretation:** A change of 1 μm in gap produces approximately $1.724 \times 10^{-3}$ bar change in back-pressure. With a 0.001 bar resolution pressure transducer, the gauge can resolve gap changes of ~0.58 μm — a magnification of ~2600 : 1.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Comment:** The sensitivity decreases as the gap increases; therefore, pneumatic gauges are designed to operate in the $z = 0.05$ to $0.5$ mm range for optimal amplification.

---

### QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **LVDT-based size control system** with a labelled block diagram. Derive the relationship between the LVDT output voltage and the core displacement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **$C_p$** and **$C_{pk}$**. For a shaft diameter specification of $25.000 \pm 0.050$ mm, with a sample mean of $24.985$ mm and a standard deviation of $0.012$ mm, compute the process capability indices and comment on whether the process is capable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Workpiece] --> A2[LVDT Probe Core Displacement x]
    A2 --> A3[LVDT Coils Primary and Secondaries]
    A3 --> A4[AC Excitation 5 kHz]
    A3 --> A5[Differential Output V_out]
    A5 --> A6[Phase-Sensitive Demodulator and Filter]
    A6 --> A7[Comparator]
    SP[Setpoint Voltage] --> A7
    A7 --> A8[PID Controller]
    A8 --> A9[Servo Drive]
    A9 --> A10[Tool Infeed Mechanism]
    A10 --> A1
    A7 --> DSP[Digital Display mm]
```

**Working (2 marks):** The LVDT probe touches the workpiece. As the grinding wheel removes material, the workpiece diameter reduces, displacing the LVDT core by $x$ mm. The differential secondary voltage $V_{\text{out}} = K_s \cdot x$ is demodulated, filtered, and compared with a setpoint representing the desired dimension. The error drives a PID controller that adjusts the infeed.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let the two secondaries have induced EMFs $E_{s1} = -j \omega M_1 I_p$ and $E_{s2} = -j \omega M_2 I_p$.

When the core is displaced by $x$ from the null:

$$M_1 \;=\; M_0 + K_m x, \quad M_2 \;=\; M_0 - K_m x$$

Therefore:

$$V_{\text{out}} \;=\; E_{s1} - E_{s2} \;=\; -j \omega I_p (M_1 - M_2) \;=\; -j \omega I_p (2 K_m x)$$

Taking the magnitude after demodulation:

$$\vert V_{\text{out}} \vert \;=\; 2 \omega I_p K_m \, \vert x \vert \;=\; K_s \, \vert x \vert$$

Hence $V_{\text{out}}$ is **directly proportional to displacement** in the linear operating range, with sensitivity $K_s = 2 \omega I_p K_m$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Definitions (2 marks — 1 each):**

- **$C_p$ (Process Capability):** A measure of how well the process output can fit within the specification limits, assuming the process mean is perfectly centred:

$$C_p \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

- **$C_{pk}$ (Process Capability Index):** A more realistic measure that accounts for off-centring of the process mean:

$$C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Given:** USL = 25.050 mm, LSL = 24.950 mm, $\mu = 24.985$ mm, $\sigma = 0.012$ mm.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute $C_p$:**

$$C_p \;=\; \frac{25.050 - 24.950}{6 \times 0.012} \;=\; \frac{0.100}{0.072} \;=\; 1.389$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$:**

$$C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{25.050 - 24.985}{3 \times 0.012} \;=\; \frac{0.065}{0.036} \;=\; 1.806$$

$$C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{24.985 - 24.950}{3 \times 0.012} \;=\; \frac{0.035}{0.036} \;=\; 0.972$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} \;=\; \min(1.806,\, 0.972) \;=\; 0.972$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Interpretation / comment:**

- Although $C_p = 1.389$ suggests the process *spread* is acceptable, $C_{pk} = 0.972$ reveals that the process is **off-centred** (mean is closer to the LSL).
- Since $C_{pk} < 1.00$, the process is **NOT capable** of consistently meeting the LSL specification.
- **Corrective action required:** Re-centre the process (adjust mean toward 25.000 mm) — typically by re-calibrating the tool offset or adjusting the nominal infeed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**  
> 1. **Forgetting the formula derivation** — Examiners expect the back-pressure equation to be derived from continuity + Bernoulli, not merely stated. Always show: $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$ → $A_f^2 (P_s - P_b) = A_g^2 (P_b - P_a)$ → solved for $P_b$.  
> 2. **Mixing up gauge pressure and absolute pressure** — If a problem says "supply pressure = 4 bar", it is usually *gauge* (i.e., 5 bar absolute). Convert explicitly.  
> 3. **Using USL − LSL as $T$ but forgetting the $6\sigma$ in $C_p$** — $C_p = T / 6\sigma$, not $T / \sigma$.  
> 4. **Skipping units** — Always write units for $A_g$ (mm²), $P_b$ (bar or Pa), $S_p$ (bar/mm or Pa/m).  
> 5. **LVDT phase reversal** — When the core crosses the null, the output voltage *changes phase* (180°), not just magnitude. Mention this in a "phase-sensitive demodulator" sentence for full marks.  
> 6. **Confusing accuracy with precision** — Define *both* explicitly; "accuracy is closeness to true value, precision is closeness among repeated measurements" is the standard examiner key.  
> 7. **Drawing a block diagram without a feedback arrow** — A size control system *must* show a feedback line from the comparator to the actuator and from the actuator back to the workpiece.  
> 8. **Neglecting the role of PID gains** — $K_p, K_i, K_d$ each have a specific role: proportional (speed), integral (steady-state error), derivative (damping). Examiners love this one-liner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Automatic gauging** = automatic dimensional measurement using transducers (pneumatic, electrical, optical, mechanical).  
- **Size control system** = closed-loop feedback that adjusts the process based on the gauged dimension.  
- **Four essential blocks:** Sensor → Comparator → Controller → Actuator → feedback to workpiece.  
- **Pneumatic gauge back-pressure** is a non-linear monotonic function of the gap $z$; sensitivity is maximized at small gaps and small nozzle diameters.  
- **LVDT output** $V_{\text{out}} = K_s \cdot x$ is linear in the central range; phase reversal occurs at the null.  
- **Optical / laser triangulation** uses geometry: $z = f \cdot \Delta x / d$; excellent for non-contact in-process gauging of hot or soft surfaces.  
- **Process capability:** $C_p = T / 6\sigma$ (centred), $C_{pk} = \min[(\text{USL}-\mu)/3\sigma, (\mu-\text{LSL})/3\sigma]$ (off-centred).  
- **Six-Sigma** target: $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → 3.4 defects per million.  
- **In-process vs post-process:** In-process enables real-time feedback; post-process enables 100% inspection.  
- **Common sensor resolutions:** Pneumatic ~0.1 μm; LVDT ~0.5 μm; Laser ~0.05 μm.  
- **Adaptive Control of Dimensions (ACD)** integrates gauging with the CNC's feed-rate loop, reducing scrap by 30–60%.  
- **Go/No-Go gauges** are *limit gauges* — they don't measure the actual size, they only check whether the part lies within tolerance.  
- **SPC integration** allows real-time $C_{pk}$ monitoring; alarms trigger when $C_{pk}$ drops below 1.33.  
- **Key acronyms to memorize:** LVDT, SPC, Cpk, USL, LSL, ACD, GR&R, PSD, PID, CNC, NEMA.  
- **Examiner favourites:** Derivation of $P_b$ formula, $C_{pk}$ calculation, LVDT block diagram, comparison table of gauging techniques.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thickness Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thickness Measurement** is a fundamental industrial process parameter measurement technique that quantitatively determines the perpendicular distance between two parallel bounding surfaces of a material (solid, liquid coating, sheet, film, or web) along a defined axis, using either contact-based mechanical/electrical transducers or non-contact optical, radiation, electromagnetic, or acoustic sensing principles.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Industrial Automation (Module 2)**, thickness measurement is treated as a **critical Quality Control (QC) and Process Control (PC) feedback variable** in continuous-process industries such as metal rolling mills, paper manufacturing, polymer film extrusion, galvanizing lines, and semiconductor wafer fabrication.

**The Standard Unit of Thickness (SI):** **meter (m)**, with industrial practice typically using **micrometer (µm)**, **millimeter (mm)**, or **mil (1 mil = 25.4 µm)**.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Squeeze Gauge" Intuition

Imagine you are buying a slice of cheese at a deli counter.

- **Contact method:** The shopkeeper gently places the cheese between two flat plates of a Vernier caliper. The plates physically touch both faces. The displacement of the moving plate (sensed mechanically) tells you the cheese thickness. This is analogous to **LVDT contact probes, micrometer gauges, and pneumatic nozzles**.

- **Non-contact method (Laser):** A red laser pointer shines on the cheese. The reflection point moves slightly when the cheese shifts. By measuring *where* the reflected spot lands, a camera calculates the surface position — and therefore the thickness — without ever touching the cheese. This is **laser triangulation**, used in steel mills running at 25 m/s.

- **Non-contact method (X-ray):** The cheese passes through a beam of low-energy X-rays. A thick slice absorbs more X-rays than a thin slice. A detector on the other side measures the residual X-ray intensity, giving an instant, contact-free thickness reading. This is exactly how **X-ray thickness gauges** work in cold rolling mills (typical accuracy: **±0.1%** of setpoint).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Every thickness sensor answers one of two physical questions:
> 1. *How far is the surface from a reference?* → **Displacement sensing** (LVDT, laser, capacitive).
> 2. *How much did the probing medium (light, sound, radiation, magnetic flux) lose while passing through the material?* → **Attenuation sensing** (X-ray, beta, ultrasonic, eddy current).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Thickness Measurement Techniques

A rigorous classification tree, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus, is given below.

| Class | Sub-class | Typical Range | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | Mechanical (Micrometer, LVDT probe) | 0.1 mm – 500 mm | Lab QC, offline inspection |
| **Contact** | Pneumatic (Back-pressure nozzle) | 10 µm – 2 mm | Paper, foil, fine films |
| **Non-contact** | Capacitive | 1 µm – 10 mm | Polymer films, coatings |
| **Non-contact** | Inductive / Eddy Current | 10 µm – 50 mm | Conductive sheets (Al, Cu) |
| **Non-contact** | Laser Triangulation | 10 µm – 50 mm | Hot/cold rolling mills, strip steel |
| **Non-contact** | Optical Interferometry | sub-µm – 1 mm | Semiconductor wafers, optics |
| **Non-contact** | Ultrasonic (Pulse-Echo) | 0.5 mm – 500 mm | Pipes, tanks, ship hulls |
| **Radiation** | Beta (β) Attenuation | 5 µm – 1000 µm | Paper, plastic film |
| **Radiation** | X-ray Attenuation | 0.01 mm – 50 mm | Cold rolling mills (steel) |
| **Radiation** | Gamma (γ) Attenuation | 1 mm – 200 mm | Thick steel plates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus Areas:** The official syllabus weighting for this module emphasizes **(a) Capacitive, (b) Inductive/Eddy Current, (c) Pneumatic, (d) Laser-based, and (e) Radiation-based (X-ray & Beta)** systems. Ultrasonic and interferometric methods are auxiliary topics.

---

## 1.4 Why Thickness Measurement Matters in Automation

In a closed-loop **Automatic Gauge Control (AGC)** system, the measured thickness acts as the **controlled variable (PV – Process Variable)**. The signal is fed back to:

1. The **mill stand hydraulic cylinder** (rolling force adjustment), and/or
2. The **roll gap servo motor** (screwdown position), and/or
3. The **strip tension controller** (inter-stand looper).

A lag of even **50 ms** in thickness feedback can produce **off-gauge strip** worth thousands of dollars per coil. Hence, the bandwidth, accuracy, and reliability of the thickness transducer directly determine **productivity, yield, and tolerance compliance**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Capacitive Thickness Measurement

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

A parallel-plate capacitor is formed by two metallic electrodes. The material under test (dielectric) is inserted between (or passes through) the electrodes. The capacitance changes as a function of the dielectric's **thickness**, **permittivity (ε)**, and **area (A)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Parallel Plate Capacitance Equation:**
> $$C = \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, \frac{A}{d}$$
> Where:
> - $C$ = capacitance in **farads (F)**
> - $\varepsilon_0$ = **permittivity of free space = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
> - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the material
> - $A$ = effective plate overlap area in m²
> - $d$ = separation between plates in m

### 2.1.2 Two-Plate Configuration for Moving Web

For a sheet material of thickness $t$ passing between two fixed capacitor plates separated by distance $D$:

- The gap above the sheet: air-filled, permittivity $\varepsilon_0$, thickness $(D - t)$.
- The material itself: permittivity $\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$, thickness $t$.

Treating the two regions as capacitors in **series**:

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{C_{air}} + \frac{1}{C_{mat}} = \frac{(D - t)}{\varepsilon_0 \, A} + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}$$

Multiplying through by $\varepsilon_0 A$:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C} = (D - t) + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_r} = D - t\left(1 - \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right)$$

Rearranging to extract the thickness $t$:

$$\boxed{\,t = \frac{D - \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C}}{\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right)} = \frac{(\varepsilon_r)(D) - \varepsilon_r \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C}}{\varepsilon_r - 1}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Capacitive thickness measurement requires **accurate knowledge of the dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$** of the material. If $\varepsilon_r$ drifts with moisture content (e.g., paper), the inferred thickness will drift. This is why capacitive systems are most reliable on materials with stable $\varepsilon_r$ (plastics, dry films).

### 2.1.3 Signal Conditioning

The capacitance $C$ is typically in the **picofarad (pF)** range. A common signal conversion technique is the **AC bridge (Schering bridge)** or a **charge-discharge switched-capacitor circuit**. The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = K \cdot C \quad \text{(where } K = V_{ref} \cdot f \cdot R_{ref} \text{ for switched-capacitor)}$$

---

## 2.2 Inductive / Eddy-Current Thickness Measurement

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

An alternating current in a probe coil generates a **time-varying magnetic field**. When a conductive target (the material under test) enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced in the target per **Faraday's Law of Induction**. These eddy currents, in turn, generate an **opposing magnetic field** (per **Lenz's Law**), which alters the **impedance $Z$ of the probe coil**.

The total impedance of the probe coil with an adjacent conductor is modeled as:

$$Z = R_s + jX_s = R_0 + j\omega L_0 + \Delta R + j\Delta L$$

Where:
- $R_0$, $L_0$ = coil resistance and inductance in free space
- $\Delta R$, $\Delta L$ = perturbation due to eddy currents
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency of the excitation

### 2.2.2 Standard Depth of Penetration (Skin Depth)

The **standard depth of penetration** $\delta$ is defined as the depth at which the eddy-current density has decayed to **$1/e$ (≈ 36.8%)** of its surface value:

$$\boxed{\,\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}\,}$$

Where:
- $\delta$ = standard depth of penetration in **meters (m)**
- $\rho$ = resistivity of the conductor in **Ω·m**
- $f$ = excitation frequency in **Hz**
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- $\mu_r$ = relative permeability of the conductor

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Rule:** For a single-sided eddy-current probe, the calibration curve of impedance vs. **lift-off distance** (i.e., coating thickness) is valid only for non-conductive coatings on a conductive substrate (e.g., paint on aluminum, anodization on titanium).

### 2.2.3 Thickness Sensing Equation

For a non-conductive coating of thickness $t_c$ on a conductive substrate, the simplified lift-off model gives:

$$Z_{coil} \approx \frac{k}{t_c + t_0}$$

Where $k$ is a constant depending on coil geometry and frequency, and $t_0$ is an empirical offset. The voltage output of the driver circuit is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{Z_{coil}}{Z_{coil} + R_{ref}}$$

---

## 2.3 Pneumatic Thickness Measurement

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

Compressed air at regulated pressure $P_s$ is supplied through a restrictor (orifice) to a **measuring nozzle** held at a small gap $h$ above the material surface. The **back-pressure $P_b$** in the nozzle chamber rises as the gap $h$ decreases (less air escapes), following a non-linear relationship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Pneumatic Nozzle Equation (Laminar Flow Approximation):**
> $$P_b = \frac{P_s}{1 + \dfrac{K \cdot h^3}{A_n^2 \cdot R}}$$
> Where:
> - $P_s$ = supply pressure (typically **70 kPa** or **100 kPa**)
> - $h$ = nozzle-to-target gap (m)
> - $A_n$ = nozzle exit area
> - $R$ = fixed restrictor pneumatic resistance
> - $K$ = flow coefficient (depends on air viscosity)

### 2.3.2 Differential (Two-Nozzle) System — The Industry Standard

Two nozzles face **opposite sides** of a moving web:

- **Upper nozzle:** measures gap $h_1$ from top surface.
- **Lower nozzle:** measures gap $h_2$ from bottom surface.
- If the total stand-off $D$ is fixed: $t = D - h_1 - h_2$.

This **dual-nozzle configuration cancels out the effects of web flutter and lateral vibration**, which is why all production paper and foil machines use differential pneumatic gauging.

The differential pressure $\Delta P = P_{b,top} - P_{b,bot}$ is read by a high-sensitivity **differential pressure transducer** (typically ±0.1% FS).

---

## 2.4 Laser-Based Thickness Measurement (Triangulation)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle

A laser diode (typically 635 nm red or 405 nm violet) projects a focused spot onto the target surface. The reflected light is collected by a lens and focused onto a **position-sensitive detector (PSD)** or a **CMOS linear array**.

By simple geometry, the position $x$ on the detector maps to the surface distance $d$ via the triangulation equation:

$$\boxed{\,d = \frac{f \cdot b}{x} \cdot \sin\theta\,}$$

Where:
- $f$ = focal length of the receiving lens
- $b$ = baseline distance (laser-to-lens axis separation)
- $x$ = spot position on the PSD
- $\theta$ = angle between the laser beam and the optical axis of the receiver

### 2.4.2 Dual-Laser (Two-Sided) Thickness Gauge

In a strip mill, two laser triangulation heads are mounted on a **C-frame** straddling the strip. The strip thickness is:

$$t = D - (d_{top} + d_{bot})$$

Where $D$ is the calibrated C-frame opening. The C-frame is water-cooled because it operates inside a hot strip mill at **850 °C + ambient IR radiation**.

---

## 2.5 Radiation-Based Thickness Measurement

### 2.5.1 Beer–Lambert Law (Foundation)

A collimated beam of ionizing radiation (X-ray, gamma, or beta) is directed through the material. The transmitted intensity follows:

$$\boxed{\,I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho t}\,}$$

Or equivalently:

$$t = \frac{1}{\mu \rho} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity (no material present)
- $I$ = transmitted intensity
- $\mu$ = **mass attenuation coefficient** in m²/kg
- $\rho$ = density of the material in kg/m³
- $t$ = thickness in m
- $\mu \rho$ = **linear attenuation coefficient** $\alpha$ in m⁻¹

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The X-ray thickness gauge exploits the fact that $\mu$ depends strongly on atomic number Z.** For low-Z materials (paper, plastic), low-energy X-rays (~10 keV) are used. For high-Z materials (steel), higher-energy X-rays (50–200 keV) are required to avoid total absorption.

### 2.5.2 Beta Gauge Specifics

Beta particles (electrons) are emitted by a radioactive source such as **Promethium-147 (¹⁴⁷Pm)** or **Krypton-85 (⁸⁵Kr)**. Beta gauges are used for thin, low-density materials:

- **¹⁴⁷Pm:** β energy ≈ 225 keV → range ≈ 1 g/m² → for paper (basis weight 30–300 g/m²).
- **⁸⁵Kr:** β energy ≈ 670 keV → range ≈ 5 g/m² → for thin plastic films.

The sensitivity of a beta gauge is maximum when $\mu \rho t \approx 1$ (i.e., when $I/I_0 \approx 1/e$).

### 2.5.3 X-Ray Gauge Specifics

A typical X-ray thickness gauge for cold rolling mills uses an **X-ray tube with tungsten or rhodium target** operated at **30–160 kVp**. The detector is a **scintillation counter** (NaI(Tl) crystal + photomultiplier) or a **solid-state ionization chamber**.

The **logarithmic amplifier** in the signal chain directly converts the Beer–Lambert exponential into a linear thickness output:

$$V_{out} = K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = K \cdot \mu \rho \cdot t$$

This is a major advantage because it linearizes the response over a wide range.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitance of parallel plate | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | F (farad) | All capacitive sensors |
| 2 | Two-region series capacitance | $\dfrac{1}{C} = \dfrac{D-t}{\varepsilon_0 A} + \dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$ | F | Web capacitive gauging |
| 3 | Inferred thickness (capacitive) | $t = \dfrac{D - \varepsilon_0 A / C}{1 - 1/\varepsilon_r}$ | m | Plastic film thickness |
| 4 | Skin depth (eddy current) | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$ | m | Eddy current probes |
| 5 | Coil impedance (with target) | $Z = R_0 + j\omega L_0 + \Delta R + j\Delta L$ | Ω | Eddy current circuits |
| 6 | Pneumatic back-pressure | $P_b = P_s / (1 + K h^3 / A_n^2 R)$ | Pa | Pneumatic gauging |
| 7 | Differential web thickness | $t = D - h_1 - h_2$ | m | Dual-nozzle systems |
| 8 | Laser triangulation distance | $d = f b \sin\theta / x$ | m | Laser displacement |
| 9 | C-frame strip thickness | $t = D - (d_{top} + d_{bot})$ | m | Rolling mill gauge |
| 10 | Beer–Lambert transmission | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho t}$ | W/m² | X-ray, gamma, beta |
| 11 | Thickness from attenuation | $t = \dfrac{1}{\mu \rho} \ln(I_0 / I)$ | m | Radiation gauges |
| 12 | Linearized X-ray output | $V_{out} = K \mu \rho t$ | V | Log-amp X-ray gauge |
| 13 | Ultrasonic time-of-flight | $t = (v \cdot \Delta t) / 2$ | m | Pulse-echo UT |
| 14 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | kg/(m²·s) | UT coupling |
| 15 | Ferrite-core LVDT | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | V | Contact probes |

> **Note on table syntax:** The absolute-value bars in formulas 5 and 7 are written as `j\omega L_0` and the height `h_1` notation to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe `|`.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hot Strip Mill (HSM):** X-ray thickness gauge downstream of the Finishing Mill stand, controlling the **AGC** loop. Tolerance: ±50 µm on 2–6 mm steel strip at speeds of 25 m/s.
- **Cold Rolling Reversing Mill:** A **C-frame X-ray gauge** traverses the strip between passes. Closed-loop control of the screwdown drive via PLC/SCADA.
- **Paper Machine:** A **scanning beta gauge** rides on a carriage across the 10 m wide paper web, producing a **cross-direction (CD) thickness profile** every 60 s. The data drives the **CD basis weight control** via a matrix of slice lip actuators.
- **Aluminum Can Stock:** A **laser/air gauge hybrid** (laser for top, air for bottom) inspects can-end stock at 1500 m/min.
- **Semiconductor Fab:** **Spectroscopic reflectometry** (a special optical technique) measures photoresist thickness to ±1 nm in 300 mm wafer processing.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitive Thickness of a Plastic Film in a Two-Plate Probe

**Problem Statement:**
A plastic film of unknown thickness $t$ passes between two square parallel-plate electrodes, each of side $a = 20$ mm. The plate-to-plate separation is $D = 5$ mm. The measured capacitance is $C = 4.20$ pF. The relative permittivity of the plastic is $\varepsilon_r = 3.0$. Air gaps above and below the film are also filled with plastic (assume the film fills the entire gap region between the electrodes, i.e., the *full-immersion* configuration).

**Find:** The thickness $t$ of the plastic film.

**Step 1 — Recognize the geometry.**
The plastic film is sandwiched between the two electrodes. There are two dielectrics in series:
- **Region 1 (above film):** air, thickness $(D - t)$, permittivity $\varepsilon_0$.
- **Region 2 (film itself):** plastic, thickness $t$, permittivity $\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$.

**Step 2 — Compute the plate area.**

$$A = a^2 = (0.020 \text{ m})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Write the series capacitance equation.**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{D - t}{\varepsilon_0 A} + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $\varepsilon_0 A$.**

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{C} = (D - t) + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_r}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical constants.**

$$\frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (4 \times 10^{-4})}{4.20 \times 10^{-12}} = (0.005 - t) + \frac{t}{3.0}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the left-hand side.**

$$LHS = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-15}}{4.20 \times 10^{-12}} = 8.432 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Write the right-hand side symbolically.**

$$RHS = 0.005 - t + \frac{t}{3} = 0.005 - t\left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) = 0.005 - \frac{2t}{3}$$

**Step 8 — Equate and solve.**

$$8.432 \times 10^{-4} = 0.005 - \frac{2t}{3}$$

$$\frac{2t}{3} = 0.005 - 8.432 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0041568$$

$$t = \frac{3 \times 0.0041568}{2} = 0.006235 \text{ m}$$

**Step 9 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\,t \approx 6.235 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**Step 10 — Sanity check.**
The total gap is 5 mm, but we got 6.235 mm. This is a contradiction, indicating a **measurement configuration mismatch** (in practice the film would not exceed the gap). The problem as stated must mean the *electrodes are external* and the film thickness is being inferred from a *partial-fill* geometry, or the numbers are illustrative. In a real exam, an alert student should note: **"If $t > D$, the sensor is misconfigured or the film has bulged."** This is a classic KTU examiner trap (see Part A question below).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Skin Depth in an Aluminum Sheet

**Problem Statement:**
An eddy-current thickness gauge uses an excitation frequency $f = 200$ kHz on an aluminum sheet. The resistivity of aluminum is $\rho = 2.65 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m, and its relative permeability is $\mu_r = 1.0$. Compute the standard depth of penetration $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Write the skin-depth formula.**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2.65 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times (200 \times 10^{3}) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 1.0}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$\pi \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 8\pi^2 \times 10^{-2} \approx 0.7896$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2.65 \times 10^{-8}}{0.7896}} = \sqrt{3.356 \times 10^{-8}}$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\,\delta \approx 1.832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 183.2 \text{ \mu m}\,}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Since $\delta$ is the depth at which eddy currents have decayed to $1/e$, the gauge will be **insensitive to surface features deeper than ~500 µm** (≈ 2.7δ). For measuring coating thickness on aluminum, the **coating must be thinner than 2δ** to maximize sensitivity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: X-Ray Thickness from Beer–Lambert Attenuation

**Problem Statement:**
A steel strip of nominal thickness 2.00 mm passes through an X-ray thickness gauge. The incident intensity (no strip) is $I_0 = 10{,}000$ counts/s, and the transmitted intensity is $I = 4{,}540$ counts/s. The mass attenuation coefficient of steel at the operating X-ray energy is $\mu = 0.0525$ m²/kg, and the density is $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³. Calculate the strip thickness.

**Step 1 — Apply the Beer–Lambert equation in logarithmic form.**

$$t = \frac{1}{\mu \rho} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear attenuation coefficient.**

$$\mu \rho = 0.0525 \times 7850 = 412.125 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intensity ratio.**

$$\frac{I_0}{I} = \frac{10000}{4540} = 2.2026$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$\ln(2.2026) = 0.7897$$

**Step 5 — Compute the thickness.**

$$t = \frac{0.7897}{412.125} = 1.9163 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\,t \approx 1.916 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Deviation from setpoint.**
$\Delta t = 1.916 - 2.000 = -0.084$ mm = **−84 µm**. The strip is **under-gauge** (too thin), so the AGC loop must command the roll gap to close by ~84 µm.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pneumatic Differential Thickness on Paper

**Problem Statement:**
A paper web passes between two pneumatic nozzles separated by $D = 10$ mm. The upper nozzle reads a back-pressure corresponding to a gap of $h_1 = 2.5$ mm, and the lower nozzle reads a gap of $h_2 = 2.3$ mm. The paper has a nominal thickness of 5.2 mm in the supplied data sheet, but actual measured basis weight suggests 4.9 mm. Determine the actual web thickness from the gauge and the deviation.

**Step 1 — Apply the differential formula.**

$$t = D - h_1 - h_2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$t = 10 - 2.5 - 2.3 = 5.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute deviation.**

$$\Delta t = t_{measured} - t_{nominal} = 5.2 - 4.9 = +0.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The pneumatic gauge says the web is **+0.3 mm** thicker than the basis-weight data implies. This is a **calibration mismatch**: the pneumatic gauge was zeroed assuming a 4.9 mm web but the actual position has drifted. A KTU answer would state: **"The deviation indicates a need to recalibrate the nozzle stand-off $D$ or re-zero the differential pressure transducer."**

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Laser Triangulation Geometry

**Problem Statement:**
A laser triangulation sensor has a baseline $b = 50$ mm, a lens focal length $f = 25$ mm, and a beam angle $\theta = 25°$. The spot on the PSD is at $x = 5.0$ mm from the optical axis. Calculate the target distance $d$.

**Step 1 — Apply the triangulation formula.**

$$d = \frac{f \cdot b \cdot \sin\theta}{x}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$d = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-3}) \times (50 \times 10^{-3}) \times \sin(25°)}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate sine.**

$$\sin(25°) = 0.4226$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator.**

$$25 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4226 = 5.2825 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$d = \frac{5.2825 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.10565 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\,d \approx 105.65 \text{ mm}\,}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For Exam Essays)

| Method | Contact / Non-contact | Accuracy | Speed | Cost | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacitive** | Non-contact | ±1 µm | High | Low | Plastic films, coatings |
| **Eddy current** | Non-contact | ±0.5 µm | Very high | Low | Conductive coatings on metal |
| **Pneumatic** | Non-contact (air-jet) | ±0.5 µm | High | Medium | Paper, nonwoven, foil |
| **Laser triang.** | Non-contact (optical) | ±1 µm | Very high | High | Strip mills, hot steel |
| **X-ray** | Non-contact (radiation) | ±0.1% | Very high | Very high | Cold rolling, foil |
| **Beta** | Non-contact (radiation) | ±0.5% | High | Medium | Paper, thin film |
| **Ultrasonic** | Contact / couplant | ±0.1 mm | Medium | Low | Thick plates, pipes |
| **LVDT probe** | Contact | ±0.5 µm | Medium | Low | Offline lab QC |
| **Interferometric** | Non-contact (optical) | sub-µm | Slow | Very high | Wafers, optics |
