- [ ] **Thermocouple:** Seebeck Effect, types K/J/T/R/S, active, wide range, low accuracy
- [ ] **RTD (Pt100):** $R = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$, $\alpha_{Pt} = 0.00385\,/°C$, passive, ±0.1 °C accuracy
- [ ] **Thermistor:** NTC (resistance falls with T) vs. PTC (resistance rises with T), very sensitive, non-linear, narrow range

### C. Displacement/Position Sensors
- [ ] **LVDT:** Electromagnetic, infinite resolution, $V_{out} = k \cdot x$, no contact
- [ ] **Potentiometer:** Simple, contact-based, $V_{out} = V_{in}(x/L)$
- [ ] **Encoder:** Incremental (pulses) vs. Absolute (unique code per position), $\theta_{step} = 360°/N$

### D. Proximity Sensors
- [ ] **Inductive:** Metals only, 1–40 mm range
- [ ] **Capacitive:** All materials, 1–30 mm
- [ ] **Magnetic (Reed):** Magnets only, contact-based
- [ ] **Photoelectric:** Long range (up to 60 m), uses light beam
- [ ] **Ultrasonic:** Up to 10 m, detects solids/liquids/powders

### E. Actuator Fundamentals
- [ ] **Actuator = Output device** that converts control signal → physical action
- [ ] **Electric:** DC motor ($T = K_t I_a$), Stepper (open-loop, $\theta_{step} = 360°/N_s$), Servo (closed-loop)
- [ ] **Hydraulic:** Oil, high pressure (70–350 bar), $F = P \cdot A$, very high force
- [ ] **Pneumatic:** Air, low pressure (4–8 bar), $F = P \cdot A$, fast and clean
- [ ] **Solenoid:** Linear on/off motion, $F \approx \mu_0 N^2 I^2 A / (2g^2)$

### F. Control Loop Architecture
- [ ] **Open-loop:** No sensor feedback, no error correction
- [ ] **Closed-loop:** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Controller → Actuator Drive → Actuator → Process → (back to Sensor)
- [ ] **Standard signal levels:** 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, 0–5 V (current loop preferred for noise immunity)
- [ ] **PID:** Proportional (immediate), Integral (eliminates steady-state error), Derivative (anticipates future error)

### G. Industry 4.0 (KTU 2024 Emphasis)
- [ ] **Smart Sensor:** On-board processing + self-calibration + digital communication (IO-Link, PROFINET)
- [ ] **Edge Computing:** Local data processing before cloud transmission
- [ ] **Wireless Sensors:** Bluetooth, LoRaWAN, Wi-Fi for Industry 4.0
- [ ] **Predictive Maintenance:** Vibration + temperature sensors feeding ML algorithms

### H. Exam-Ready Equations (Memorize)
- [ ] Seebeck: $V = \int(S_B - S_A)\,dT$
- [ ] RTD: $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha T]$
- [ ] LVDT: $V_{out} = k \cdot x$
- [ ] Encoder: $\theta_{step} = 360°/N$
- [ ] DC Motor: $T = K_t I_a$, $E_b = K_e \omega$, $V = E_b + I_a R_a$
- [ ] Cylinder Force: $F = P \cdot A$
- [ ] Hydraulic Power: $P = Q \cdot p$

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Always frame your answers around the **sensor → controller → actuator** triad. The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) emphasizes **application-based learning**, so merely listing facts will earn 60–70% marks. To achieve 90%+, always include a **real industrial example**, a **quantified comparison**, and a **block diagram (even a hand-drawn textual one)** in your Part B answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Position, Proximity, and Motion Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Position, Proximity, and Motion Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Sensor Classification in Industrial Automation

In any automated manufacturing cell, robotic workstation, or process control loop, the very first link between the *physical world* and the *electronic controller* (PLC / DCS / SCADA) is the **sensor**. The KTU 2024 Scheme module on Industrial Automation groups three functionally related families of sensors under one classification umbrella:

1. **Position Sensors** — devices that determine the *exact location* (linear or angular) of a target object relative to a reference.
2. **Proximity Sensors** — devices that detect the *presence or absence* of a target within a defined sensing range, *without physical contact*.
3. **Motion Sensors** — devices that sense the *rate of change of position* (velocity), *acceleration*, or *displacement trajectory* of a target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Position sensors* measure absolute or relative displacement; *Proximity sensors* provide a binary (yes/no) indication of target presence within a nominal switching distance $S_n$; *Motion sensors* quantify kinematic variables such as linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, or acceleration $a$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sense of Touch" of a Machine

Imagine a blindfolded person trying to know what is happening around them:

- A **Position Sensor** is like touching an object and saying, *"This is exactly 3 inches from my nose."* (Quantified location)
- A **Proximity Sensor** is like feeling a warm breeze on your cheek and saying, *"Something is near me!"* (Binary presence, no contact)
- A **Motion Sensor** is like feeling the wind speed on your hand and saying, *"The fan is spinning fast!"* (Rate-of-change measurement)

> [!NOTE]
> The unifying principle across all three families is **transduction** — converting a *non-electrical physical quantity* (displacement, presence, velocity) into a *measurable electrical signal* (voltage, current, frequency, or digital code).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded as Required)

- **Nominal switching distance $S_n$** — the standard target distance at which a proximity sensor is rated to operate (typically **3 mm to 30 mm** for inductive, **1 mm to 50 mm** for capacitive).
- **Hysteresis $H$** — the difference between the *operate point* (target approaching) and the *release point* (target receding); typically **1 % to 20 %** of $S_n$.
- **Repeatability $R$** — the smallest change in position that produces a detectable change in output, usually **0.01 mm to 0.1 mm**.
- **Resolution of encoders** — for an $n$-bit encoder: $360^{\circ} / 2^n$ per LSB.

## 1.4 High-Level Classification Tree

The three families can be systematically classified by **physical principle of transduction**, **output type**, and **range of operation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** The most common examination question on this topic is *"Classify position/proximity/motion sensors with examples."* Memorizing the parent-child hierarchy in Section 1.4 is essential for full marks.

[End of Section 1 — proceed to Section 2 for deep theoretical analysis]

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Position Sensors

A position sensor outputs a signal proportional to the *static or dynamic displacement* of an object. They fall into two broad families based on the **nature of measurement**.

### A. Linear Position Sensors (Translational Displacement)
- **Potentiometric Sensors** — resistive element with a sliding wiper. Output voltage is a fraction of the applied excitation.

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

where $x$ = wiper displacement, $L$ = total resistive track length, $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage.

- **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** — an electromechanical transducer with one primary and two secondary windings. Output is the **difference of two induced EMFs**:

$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot (V_{s2} - V_{s1})$$

where $K_{LVDT}$ is the sensitivity constant (V/mm). LVDT offers **infinite resolution** and is the gold standard for sub-millimeter accuracy.

- **Linear Encoders** — use optical/magnetic scales to produce digital pulses corresponding to displacement.

### B. Angular Position Sensors (Rotational Displacement)
- **Rotary Potentiometer** — same principle as linear pot, but with a circular track.
- **Optical Encoders** — subdivided into:
  - **Incremental Encoder** — produces $A$ and $B$ quadrature pulses; *position is relative* (must be homed at startup).
  - **Absolute Encoder** — produces a unique binary/Gray code for *every* shaft angle; *position is retained even after power loss*.
- **Resolvers** — robust electromagnetic devices with rotor and stator windings that produce $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ outputs; used in **harsh environments** (military, aerospace, CNC).
- **Synchros** — similar to resolvers, used historically in servomechanisms.
- **Hall-Effect Angle Sensors** — detect the angular position of a magnetic field.

## 2.2 Classification of Proximity Sensors

Proximity sensors are **non-contact devices** that switch their output when a target enters a defined sensing zone. Classification is by the **physical principle of target interaction**.

| Family | Operating Principle | Detects | Typical Range |
|--------|---------------------|---------|---------------|
| Inductive | Eddy-current loss in target | Metallic targets only | 1 mm – 40 mm |
| Capacitive | Change in capacitance $C$ | Metallic + Non-metallic | 1 mm – 50 mm |
| Magnetic (Reed) | Magnetic flux closes reed switch | Permanent magnets | 5 mm – 30 mm |
| Optical (Photoelectric) | Light beam interruption / reflection | All opaque / specific colors | Up to 60 m |
| Ultrasonic | Sound wave reflection (time of flight) | Any solid / liquid | 50 mm – 10 m |

### Key Equations for Proximity Sensors

**Inductive Sensor Nominal Range:**

$$S_n \approx 1.0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\mu_r \cdot A_{target}}{\mu_0 \cdot A_{coil}}}$$

For a standard mild-steel target, the *effective switching distance* $S_r$ is approximately **0.81 × $S_n$** as per **IEC 60947-5-2** standard.

**Capacitive Sensor Equation:**

$$C = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot \frac{A}{d}$$

When the target approaches (decreasing $d$ between sensor face and target), $C$ increases. The oscillator-detector circuit trips when $\Delta C$ exceeds a threshold.

**Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight:**

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{flight}}{2}$$

where $v_{sound} \approx \mathbf{343 \ m/s}$ in air at $20^{\circ}C$, and $t_{flight}$ is the round-trip time of the ultrasonic pulse.

## 2.3 Classification of Motion Sensors

Motion sensors detect **kinematic variables** (velocity, acceleration, jerk) of a target.

### A. Velocity Sensors
- **Tachogenerator** — a small DC generator coupled to a shaft; output voltage is **proportional to angular velocity**:

$$V_{out} = K_t \cdot \omega$$

where $K_t$ is the tachogenerator constant (V/rad/s). AC tacho (with two phase outputs) is also common.

- **Incremental Encoder as a Velocity Sensor** — by counting pulses per unit time, $v$ is computed in the controller:

$$\omega = \frac{N_{pulses}}{PPR \cdot \Delta t}$$

where $PPR$ = pulses per revolution.

### B. Acceleration Sensors
- **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** — a seismic mass exerts a force on a piezoelectric crystal; output charge is:

$$Q = d_{piezo} \cdot F = d_{piezo} \cdot m \cdot a$$

where $d_{piezo}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N). The output voltage after a charge amplifier is $V_{out} = Q/C_f$.

### C. Special-Purpose Motion Sensors
- **PIR (Passive Infrared)** — detects motion of a warm body (human/animal) via a change in IR radiation; widely used in security and building automation.
- **Doppler Radar** — emits microwave signals and detects frequency shift due to moving target.
- **Gyroscopes (MEMS / Fiber-Optic / Ring Laser)** — measure angular velocity for stabilization and inertial navigation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table — Pipe-Safe)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot x / L$ | Potentiometric position output | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} (V_{s2} - V_{s1})$ | LVDT differential output | V |
| 3 | $\theta_{LSB} = 360^{\circ} / 2^n$ | Absolute encoder resolution | degrees |
| 4 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Capacitive proximity range | Farads (F) |
| 5 | $d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{flight} / 2$ | Ultrasonic distance | metres (m) |
| 6 | $V_{out} = K_t \cdot \omega$ | Tachogenerator velocity output | V |
| 7 | $Q = d_{piezo} \cdot m \cdot a$ | Piezoelectric accelerometer charge | Coulombs (C) |
| 8 | $S_r \approx 0.81 \cdot S_n$ | IEC reduced switching distance | mm |
| 9 | $\omega = N_{pulses} / (PPR \cdot \Delta t)$ | Velocity from encoder | rad/s |
| 10 | $f_{Doppler} = 2 \cdot v \cdot f_0 / c$ | Doppler radar frequency shift | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:** Questions 1, 2, 5, 6, and 8 appear in nearly every previous-year paper of OERAT613. The Doppler formula (10) is the most likely "twist" question — examiners love testing if students can derive it from first principles.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive assembly lines** use **inductive proximity sensors** on every cylinder-piston station to confirm part presence before robotic welding.
- **CNC machine tools** employ **optical encoders** and **LVDTs** for closed-loop position control with sub-micron accuracy.
- **Conveyor systems** in FMCG plants rely on **photoelectric proximity sensors** for *counting* and *level detection*.
- **Robotic arms** use **MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers** for IMU-based motion tracking.
- **Aircraft autopilots** use **fiber-optic gyroscopes** and **resolvers** for navigation-grade angular rate sensing.

[End of Section 2 — proceed to Section 3 for exhaustive step-by-step derivations]

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LVDT Output Voltage vs. Core Displacement

The LVDT consists of one primary winding (P) and two identical secondary windings (S1 and S2) wound on a cylindrical former. An a.c. excitation $V_p$ at frequency $f$ (typically **1 kHz to 10 kHz**) is applied to P. A movable ferromagnetic core couples flux to the secondaries.

**Step 1 — Flux Coupling Model**

When the core is at the *null position* (centred), the flux linking S1 and S2 is equal in magnitude but opposite in phase (they are connected in *series-opposition*). The net output is **zero**.

**Step 2 — Displacement of Core to the Right by Distance $x$**

The flux through S2 increases while flux through S1 decreases. The induced EMFs are:

$$V_{s1} = -j \omega N_s \cdot \phi_1(x)$$
$$V_{s2} = +j \omega N_s \cdot \phi_2(x)$$

**Step 3 — Net Differential Output**

The series-opposition connection yields:

$$V_{out} = V_{s2} - V_{s1} = j \omega N_s \left[ \phi_2(x) - \phi_1(x) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Linear Region Approximation**

For small displacements within the linear range, the differential flux is proportional to $x$:

$$\phi_2(x) - \phi_1(x) = K_\phi \cdot x$$

Substituting:

$$V_{out} = j \omega N_s K_\phi \cdot x = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

**Final Linear Equation:**

$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

> **Interpretation:** The LVDT output is **linear with core displacement** in the central region. Outside this region (typically $\pm 5$ mm around null), the curve becomes non-linear and unusable for precision measurement.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ultrasonic Sensor Distance from Time of Flight

The ultrasonic sensor transmits a burst of acoustic waves at frequency $f_{us}$ (typically **40 kHz**). The waves travel through air, reflect off a target, and return to the receiver.

**Step 1 — Velocity of Sound in Air**

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

where $T$ is air temperature in °C. At $T = 20^{\circ}C$, $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Round-Trip Time**

The pulse travels to the target (distance $d$) and back (another $d$). The total path is $2d$:

$$2d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{flight}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$**

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{flight}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)**

> **Q:** An ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip time of $t_{flight} = 6$ ms. Air temperature is $25^{\circ}C$. Find the target distance.

**Step 4.1:** Calculate $v_{sound}$ at $25^{\circ}C$:

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 25 = 331.4 + 15 = 346.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4.2:** Apply the distance equation:

$$d = \frac{346.4 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.0784}{2} = 1.0392 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** $d \approx 1.04$ **m** (to two decimal places).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tachogenerator Output Equation

A DC tachogenerator is a small permanent-magnet DC machine. The induced EMF in the armature is given by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**:

$$e = B \cdot l \cdot v = B \cdot l \cdot \omega \cdot r$$

**Step 1:** The linear velocity of a conductor at the armature surface is $v = \omega \cdot r$, where $r$ is the armature radius.

**Step 2:** For a multi-turn armature with $Z$ conductors:

$$e = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

where $\Phi$ is flux per pole (Wb), $N$ is rpm, $P$ is number of poles, $A$ is parallel paths.

**Step 3:** Express $N$ in rad/s: $N = 60 \omega / (2\pi)$.

**Step 4:** Substitute and simplify:

$$e = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi \cdot P \cdot 60 \cdot \omega}{60 \cdot A \cdot 2\pi} = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi \cdot P}{2 \pi A} \cdot \omega$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = K_t \cdot \omega}$$

where $K_t = Z \Phi P / (2 \pi A)$ is the tachogenerator constant in V per (rad/s).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Position vs. Proximity vs. Motion

| Parameter | Position Sensor | Proximity Sensor | Motion Sensor |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|---------------|
| Output Type | Analog / Digital (multi-bit) | Binary (ON / OFF) | Analog / Pulse train |
| Contact Required? | Usually yes (pot) or no (LVDT, encoder) | **No (always non-contact)** | No (most) |
| Typical Range | µm to metres | mm to 60 m | 0 to 10⁵ rpm; 0 to 50 g |
| Resolution | High (0.01 mm LVDT) | Binary (no resolution concept) | Medium (1 rpm / 0.01 g) |
| Cost | Medium–High | Low | Medium |
| KTU Example | LVDT, Optical Encoder | Inductive, Capacitive, Ultrasonic | Tachogenerator, Accelerometer |
| Major Use | Closed-loop position control | Object detection / counting | Speed control, vibration monitoring |

## 3.5 Decision Flow for Sensor Selection (Engineering Logic)

A KTU-favoured analytical question is *"Given a requirement, select the appropriate sensor."* The decision flow is:

1. **Is the variable a location, presence, or motion?**
   - Location → Position Sensor
   - Presence (binary) → Proximity Sensor
   - Velocity / acceleration → Motion Sensor

2. **For Position Sensor:** Is the target metallic, accessible, and does it need absolute reading?
   - Yes / Yes / Yes → LVDT or Absolute Encoder
   - Relative reading acceptable → Potentiometer or Incremental Encoder

3. **For Proximity Sensor:** What is the target material?
   - Ferrous / Non-ferrous metal only → Inductive
   - Any material (incl. plastic, liquid) → Capacitive
   - Long range / large object → Photoelectric or Ultrasonic
   - Magnetic target behind a wall → Reed / Hall-Effect

4. **For Motion Sensor:** What kinematic variable?
   - Angular velocity → Tachogenerator or Encoder
   - Linear acceleration → Piezoelectric Accelerometer
   - Human/animal motion → PIR or Doppler Radar

[End of Section 3 — proceed to Section 4 for diagrams and schematics]

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Automation Sensing Requirement] --> B{Type of Physical Variable}
    B -- Displacement --> C[Position Sensor Family]
    B -- Presence Absence --> D[Proximity Sensor Family]
    B -- Velocity Acceleration --> E[Motion Sensor Family]
    C --> C1[Linear Displacement]
    C --> C2[Angular Displacement]
    C1 --> C1A[Potentiometric]
    C1 --> C1B[LVDT]
    C1 --> C1C[Linear Encoder]
    C2 --> C2A[Rotary Potentiometer]
    C2 --> C2B[Optical Encoder Incremental]
    C2 --> C2C[Optical Encoder Absolute]
    C2 --> C2D[Resolver and Synchro]
    C2 --> C2E[Hall Effect Angle Sensor]
    D --> D1{Inductive Eddy Current}
    D --> D2{Capacitive Dielectric Change}
    D --> D3{Magnetic Reed and Hall}
    D --> D4{Photoelectric Light Based}
    D --> D5{Ultrasonic Acoustic}
    E --> E1[Velocity Sensor Subfamily]
    E --> E2[Acceleration Sensor Subfamily]
    E1 --> E1A[Tachogenerator DC and AC]
    E1 --> E1B[Encoder Derived Velocity]
    E1 --> E1C[Doppler Radar]
    E2 --> E2A[Piezoelectric Accelerometer]
    E2 --> E2B[Gyroscope MEMS and FOG]
    E2 --> E2C[PIR Sensor]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The chart begins with the *physical requirement*, branches into the three sensor families, and ends at the specific transducer technologies. This is the **first question KTU examiners ask** in the descriptive paper.

## 4.2 LVDT Internal Architecture Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Primary Coil AC Excitation 1 to 10 kHz] --> Core[Ferromagnetic Core Moves Linearly with Target]
    Core --> S1[Secondary Coil S1]
    Core --> S2[Secondary Coil S2]
    S1 --> Diff[Differential Subtraction Vout equals Vs2 minus Vs1]
    S2 --> Diff
    Diff --> Out[Rectifier and Filter Module]
    Out --> DC[DC Output Proportional to Displacement]
```

## 4.3 Inductive Proximity Sensor — Working Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Osc[High Frequency Oscillator 100 kHz to 1 MHz] --> Coil[Coil Generates Alternating Electromagnetic Field]
    Coil --> Free{Nearby Metallic Target Present?}
    Free -- No --> NoLoad[Oscillates Normally, Output Stays OFF]
    Free -- Yes --> Eddy[Eddy Currents Induced in Target]
    Eddy --> Damp[Oscillation Amplitude Damped Below Threshold]
    Damp --> Trig[Trigger Circuit Activates Output]
    NoLoad --> OutState[Output OFF]
    Trig --> OutState2[Output ON]
```

## 4.4 Optical Encoder Quadrature Decoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Disc[Rotating Code Disc with Slots] --> LED[LED Light Source]
    LED --> PD[Photodetector Array]
    PD --> CHA[Channel A Square Wave Pulse]
    PD --> CHB[Channel B Phase Shifted by 90 deg]
    CHA --> Decoder[Quadrature Decoder Logic in Controller]
    CHB --> Decoder
    Decoder --> Dir[Direction CW or CCW from phase lead lag]
    Decoder --> Pos[Incremental Position Count]
```

## 4.5 Proximity Sensor Operating Range Schematic (Generic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Target Object] -- Approaches --> Sn[Nominal Switching Distance Sn]
    Sn -- At Sn --> Op[Output Operates ON]
    Op -- Target Recedes --> Sr[Release Distance Sr less than Sn]
    Sr -- At Sr --> Off[Output Releases OFF]
    Op -- Target Continues Away --> H[Hysteresis Band equals Sn minus Sr]
    Off -.->|Re-approach| Sn
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The hysteresis band $H$ is *intentional* and prevents **chattering** of the output when the target hovers near $S_n$. For KTU problems, $H$ is typically **5 % to 20 %** of $S_n$.

[End of Section 4 — proceed to Section 5 for the examination question bank]

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]
**"Differentiate between an absolute encoder and an incremental encoder. State one industrial application of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| Output | Pulses A and B; no unique code per position | Unique binary or Gray code for each shaft angle |
| Position after power-off | **Lost** — must be rehomed | **Retained** — direct read-out at power-on |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Resolution | Set by PPR | Set by number of bits $n$ |
| Industrial Use | Speed sensing, CNC spindle feedback | Robot joint angle, antenna positioning |

*(1 mark for any two correct differences, 1 mark for application, 1 mark for presentation.)*

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]
**"An inductive proximity sensor has a nominal switching distance $S_n = 8$ mm. Determine its effective switching distance $S_r$ and the hysteresis if the release distance is 7.2 mm. Comment on the result."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Using the IEC 60947-5-2 standard for mild-steel target:

$$S_r = 0.81 \times S_n = 0.81 \times 8 = 6.48 \text{ mm}$$

But the problem gives $S_r = 7.2$ mm (operating point of the actual sensor under test). Hysteresis:

$$H = S_{operate} - S_{release} = 8 - 7.2 = 0.8 \text{ mm}$$

Hysteresis as a percentage of $S_n$:

$$H\% = (0.8 / 8) \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Comment:** A 10 % hysteresis is within the typical industrial range (1 % to 20 %), so the sensor is well-designed and **chatter-free** at the operating point.

*(1 mark for $S_r$, 1 mark for hysteresis, 1 mark for comment.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse *position* and *proximity* sensors. Position = displacement quantity, Proximity = binary detection.
> - In encoder questions, students often write *"absolute"* when the code is *Gray*. Gray code is the **single-bit-change** absolute code — make this distinction explicit.
> - For LVDT, students forget the *phase-reversal* at the null point. A correct answer **must** mention that the output changes *sign* as the core crosses null.
> - Always write the **unit** with numerical answers. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
> - In ultrasonic sensor problems, students often forget the **factor of 2** in the time-of-flight formula. Write the full derivation, not just the final equation.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage as a function of core displacement. Discuss its advantages over a potentiometric transducer. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Classify **position sensors** with suitable examples. Explain the working principle of an **optical absolute encoder** and derive the expression for its resolution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Hollow cylindrical former of insulating material
- One **primary winding (P)** at the centre
- Two identical **secondary windings (S1 and S2)** wound on either side of P, connected in *series-opposition*
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** couples flux from P to S1 and S2
- AC excitation $V_p$ (1 kHz to 10 kHz) is applied to P

**Working (2 marks):**
- At *null position* of the core, EMFs induced in S1 and S2 are equal and opposite → net output is zero.
- When the core moves towards S1, the EMF in S1 increases and that in S2 decreases → net output $V_{out} = V_{s2} - V_{s1}$ is **positive**.
- When the core moves towards S2, $V_{out}$ is **negative**. The magnitude is proportional to displacement within the linear range.

**Derivation (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{out} = V_{s2} - V_{s1} = j \omega N_s [\phi_2(x) - \phi_1(x)] = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

**Advantages over Potentiometer (0.5 marks):**
- Infinite resolution (no wiper contact)
- No friction → longer life
- Higher accuracy and linearity
- No contact bounce

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Classification of Position Sensors (3 marks):**

I. **Linear Position Sensors** — Potentiometric, LVDT, Linear Encoder
II. **Angular Position Sensors** — Rotary Pot, Optical Encoder (Incremental / Absolute), Resolver, Synchro, Hall-Effect Angle Sensor

**Working of Optical Absolute Encoder (3 marks):**
- A disc with multiple concentric tracks, each having opaque and transparent sectors
- Each track is read by a dedicated LED–photodiode pair
- The output is a unique $n$-bit code (typically **Gray code**) for every angular position
- A 10-bit encoder has $2^{10} = 1024$ unique positions

**Resolution Derivation (1 mark):**

$$\theta_{LSB} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{2^n}$$

For $n = 10$:

$$\theta_{LSB} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{1024} = 0.3516^{\circ}$$

**Why Gray Code is preferred (bonus 0.5 mark):** Only one bit changes per LSB step → eliminates intermediate ambiguity errors during transitions.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]
**a)** Classify **proximity sensors** based on the principle of operation. Explain the working of a **capacitive proximity sensor** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An **ultrasonic proximity sensor** measures a round-trip time of 4.5 ms in air at 30 °C. Determine:
- (i) The velocity of sound in air.
- (ii) The distance to the target.
- (iii) The minimum size of the target for reliable detection if the beam divergence angle is 5°.

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Classification of Proximity Sensors (3 marks):**

| Type | Principle | Detects |
|------|-----------|---------|
| Inductive | Eddy current loss | Metals only |
| Capacitive | Dielectric change | All materials |
| Magnetic | Reed switch closure | Magnets |
| Photoelectric | Light interruption | Opaque objects |
| Ultrasonic | Acoustic reflection | Any solid/liquid |

**Working of Capacitive Proximity Sensor (3.5 marks):**

- A high-frequency oscillator is connected to a sensing plate (electrode) that forms a capacitor with the target (or with ground if no target is present).
- The capacitance is $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, where $d$ is the distance to the target.
- When the target approaches, $d$ decreases, $C$ increases, and the oscillator amplitude rises.
- When $C$ exceeds a threshold, the trigger circuit switches the output.
- Sensitivity is tuned via a potentiometer that adjusts the oscillator threshold.

**Diagram (0.5 mark):** Reference Section 4 architecture flow.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Velocity of sound at 30 °C (2 marks):**

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 331.4 + 18 = 349.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Distance to target (2 marks):**

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{flight}}{2} = \frac{349.4 \cdot 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{1.5723}{2} = 0.786 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Minimum target size (3 marks):**

The ultrasonic beam diverges at angle $\theta = 5^{\circ}$, so the beam diameter at distance $d$ is:

$$D_{beam} = 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan(\theta / 2) = 2 \cdot 0.786 \cdot \tan(2.5^{\circ})$$

$$\tan(2.5^{\circ}) \approx 0.04366$$

$$D_{beam} = 2 \cdot 0.786 \cdot 0.04366 \approx 0.0686 \text{ m} = 68.6 \text{ mm}$$

For reliable detection, the target must be **at least as large as the beam diameter**, so the minimum target size is approximately **68.6 mm × 68.6 mm** (assuming a square target).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **Position sensors** output a *quantity* (analog or digital code); *examples*: Potentiometer, LVDT, Encoder, Resolver.
- **Proximity sensors** output a *binary state*; *examples*: Inductive (metals only), Capacitive (any material), Magnetic, Optical, Ultrasonic.
- **Motion sensors** output a *kinematic variable*; *examples*: Tacho, Accelerometer, Gyro, Doppler Radar, PIR.
- **LVDT output:** $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$, linear in the central region, reverses phase at null.
- **IEC 60947-5-2:** $S_r \approx 0.81 \cdot S_n$ for a standard mild-steel target on an inductive sensor.
- **Ultrasonic distance formula:** $d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{flight} / 2$ — *always divide by 2* (round-trip).
- **Absolute encoder resolution:** $\theta_{LSB} = 360^{\circ} / 2^n$ where $n$ = number of bits.
- **Tachogenerator output:** $V_{out} = K_t \cdot \omega$ — DC tacho gives polarity, AC tacho gives frequency.
- **Piezoelectric accelerometer:** $Q = d_{piezo} \cdot m \cdot a$ — charge output proportional to acceleration.
- **Hysteresis** is *intentional* in proximity sensors (5 % to 20 % of $S_n$) — prevents chattering.
- **Doppler shift:** $f_{Doppler} = 2 v f_0 / c$ — used in microwave/radar motion sensors.
- **Resolver outputs:** $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ — used in harsh environments; rugged.
- **Hall-Effect sensors** detect magnetic fields and can act as both *proximity* and *motion* sensors.
- **MEMS gyroscopes** measure angular velocity using the Coriolis force principle.
- **Key constants to memorize:** $v_{sound} = 343$ m/s at 20 °C, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- **KTU exam mnemonic — "PIC-MOS":** Potentiometric, Inductive, Capacitive — Magnetic, Optical, Sonar (ultrasonic).

> **Final KTU Tip:** The most common 14-mark question combines a *derivation* with a *comparison table*. Practice writing the table in 4 minutes and the derivation in 8 minutes to stay within the KTU time budget of 12 minutes per 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromechanical Switches
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electromechanical Switches

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **electromechanical switch** is a binary (ON/OFF) discrete-input sensing and control device that uses a mechanical actuator coupled with an electrical contact mechanism to make or break a circuit in response to a physical stimulus such as position, pressure, level, temperature, or operator action. According to the KTU 2024 *Industrial Automation* syllabus, these devices form the **first layer of the automation hierarchy** — the *field-level input devices* — bridging the physical process and the Programmable Logic Controller (PLC), Distributed Control System (DCS), or relay-logic panel.

In standard IEC 60947 terminology, an electromechanical switch is classified as a *manually or automatically operated device that opens or closes electrical contacts in a controlled manner*, where the operating force is transmitted through a mechanical linkage rather than a purely solid-state semiconductor junction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Electromechanical switches are contact-type field devices that convert a mechanical input (force, displacement, or presence of an object) into a binary electrical output by physically moving conductive contacts between OPEN and CLOSED states."*

## 1.2 Key Operational Parameters (Industry-Standard Metrics)

The following ratings are universally stamped on the body of every industrial electromechanical switch and **must be quoted correctly** in KTU theory questions:

- **Rated Voltage (Ue)** — typically **24 V DC**, **230 V AC**, or **415 V AC** (3-phase).
- **Rated Current (Ie)** — commonly **2 A, 5 A, 10 A, 16 A, 25 A, 40 A** continuous.
- **Mechanical Life** — number of no-load operations, often **10⁷ to 10⁸ cycles** for limit switches.
- **Electrical Life** — number of on-load operations, typically **10⁵ to 10⁶ cycles** at full rated load.
- **IP (Ingress Protection) Rating** — e.g., **IP65, IP67, IP68** for industrial environments.
- **Operating Force / Torque** — measured in **N (Newtons)** or **N·m**.
- **Operating Frequency** — typically **30 to 6,000 operations per hour**.
- **Contact Form** — designated as **SPST, SPDT, DPST, DPDT, N/O, N/C, CO** (Change-Over).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Contacts are the Heart" Rule:** In a KTU 14-mark question, examiners award 2 marks purely for correctly identifying the *contact configuration* (e.g., "1 NO + 1 NC") and the *utilization category* (e.g., AC-15 for inductive loads).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your **home ceiling fan regulator** — the old rotary type. When you rotate the knob, you are physically sliding a metal contact arm over a series of resistance coils. That is, fundamentally, an electromechanical switch: a human applies a **mechanical input** (rotation), and a **metal contact physically moves** to alter the electrical state of the circuit.

Now scale this idea to a factory:
- The **knob** becomes a **cam** on a conveyor motor, a **bottle on a filling line**, or a **worker's finger on a mushroom-head button**.
- The **metal arm** becomes a precision **snap-action contact** (gold or silver alloy).
- The **light/fan** becomes a **solenoid valve, motor starter, or PLC input module**.

So every electromechanical switch is essentially answering one yes/no question: *"Is the mechanical condition satisfied? Yes → close the contacts → energise the load. No → open the contacts → de-energise the load."*

This **binary decision-making** is precisely why electromechanical switches are the **natural language of PLCs and relay logic** — PLCs expect a discrete **0 V or 24 V** signal, and electromechanical switches deliver exactly that.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Electromechanical Switches (Hierarchical Breakdown)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to classify electromechanical switches by **actuation principle**, **mounting style**, and **application domain**. The exhaustive classification is:

### A. By Actuation Principle (Most Common KTU Question)

| # | Switch Type | Actuation Principle | Typical Application |
|---|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Limit Switch** | Mechanical lever / plunger / roller driven by a moving part | End-of-travel detection on pneumatic cylinders, conveyors, machine tools |
| 2 | **Push Button (PB)** | Manual finger force on a spring-return actuator | Start/Stop stations, panel consoles |
| 3 | **Selector Switch** | Rotary knob with maintained positions (2, 3, or 4 positions) | Auto/Manual/Off selector on a control desk |
| 4 | **Toggle Switch** | Lever pivots on a toggle mechanism | Power ON/OFF on machine panels |
| 5 | **Micro Switch (Snap-Action)** | Small differential travel, high-precision snap mechanism | Door interlocks, vending machines, elevator level checks |
| 6 | **Rotary Cam Switch** | Shaft rotation actuates multiple contacts in sequence | Star-Delta starters, forward-reverse motor panels |
| 7 | **Foot Switch** | Pedal actuated by operator's foot | Welding stations, medical equipment, sewing machines |
| 8 | **Pull-Cord / Pull-Wire Switch** | Tension on a wire trips the contacts | Conveyor emergency-stop along long belt lines |
| 9 | **Pressure Switch** | Diaphragm or Bourdon tube moves contacts when fluid pressure crosses a setpoint | Compressor cut-out, hydraulic system pressure limits |
| 10 | **Level Switch (Float Type)** | Buoyancy-driven float opens/closes a mercury or reed contact | Sump pump control, tank-level alarms |
| 11 | **Temperature Switch (Thermostat)** | Bimetallic strip or gas-filled bellows deflects on temperature change | Over-temperature protection in motors and heaters |
| 12 | **Reed Switch** | Ferrous object or magnet brings two ferromagnetic reeds into contact | Position sensing in cylinders (cylinder-position feedback) |

### B. By Contact Action Mechanism

1. **Slow-Acting (Tact) Switches** — Contacts move at the same speed as the actuator. Used when the *speed of operation does not matter* (e.g., selector switches).
2. **Snap-Action (Quick-Make / Quick-Break) Switches** — Internal over-centre spring causes the contacts to *snap* from one position to the other **independent of the actuator speed**. This eliminates contact bounce and is mandatory for **PLC and electronic input** use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Snap-Action is Critical for PLCs:** PLC high-speed input modules (especially counter inputs) register an "ON" pulse for any contact closure longer than **0.5 ms to 3 ms**. Slow-acting contacts *bounce* for **5 ms to 20 ms** and would be falsely counted as multiple inputs. Hence, *all PLC-rated limit switches are internally snap-action.*

### C. By Mounting and Operator Interface

- **Panel-Mounted (22.5 mm or 30.5 mm hole cut-out)** — push buttons, selector switches, pilot lights.
- **Enclosure-Mounted (IP65/IP67 boxes)** — factory-floor foot switches, pull-cord switches.
- **Direct Machine-Mounted (with brackets, levers, roller heads)** — limit switches on cylinders and conveyors.

## 2.2 The Contact Configuration Logic (Universally Tested)

Every electromechanical switch's electrical specification begins with a *contact form code*. The student must be fluent in reading these diagrams.

| Symbol | Name | Function | KTU Diagram |
|--------|------|----------|-------------|
| **SPST** | Single Pole Single Throw | Simple ON/OFF | One input, one output |
| **SPDT (CO)** | Single Pole Double Throw | Routes one input to either of two outputs | Common + NO + NC |
| **DPST** | Double Pole Single Throw | Two independent ON/OFFs actuated together | Two SPSTs on one lever |
| **DPDT** | Double Pole Double Throw | Two SPDTs actuated together | Reversing a 3-phase motor |
| **N/O** | Normally Open | Contacts open when switch is un-actuated | "Make" contact |
| **N/C** | Normally Closed | Contacts closed when switch is un-actuated | "Break" contact |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Normal" Rule:** "Normal" always refers to the **un-actuated, de-energised, rest state** of the device. A *Normally Open* contact closes *only when* the switch is pressed, bumped, or magnetised. This is a **highly tested 2-mark concept**.

## 2.3 Operating Characteristics — The 4 Critical Curves

In a KTU 14-mark question on limit switches, the examiner expects you to **draw and label the force-travel curve** of the actuator. The curve has these critical points (memorise the terminology):

1. **Free Position (FP)** — The un-actuated rest position of the actuator.
2. **Operating Point (OP)** — The position at which the contacts *change state* during actuation.
3. **Total Travel Position (TTP)** — The maximum mechanical travel allowed before the actuator hits a hard stop.
4. **Release Point (RP)** — The position at which the contacts *return* to the normal state during de-actuation.
5. **Differential Travel (DT)** — The distance *between* OP and RP. **Smaller DT = higher precision** (micro switches achieve DT ≈ 0.1 mm).
6. **Pretravel (PT)** — Distance from FP to OP.
7. **Overtravel (OT)** — Distance from OP to TTP. **OT must be large** to absorb mechanical shock and prevent actuator damage (industrial limit switches specify OT ≥ 5 mm).

The related formulas are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Stroke} &= \text{Pre-travel (PT)} + \text{Overtravel (OT)} \\
\text{Differential Travel (DT)} &= \vert \text{Operating Point} - \text{Release Point} \vert \\
\text{Repeatability Error} &= \pm \frac{\text{DT}}{2} \quad \text{(in mm or degrees)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|---|----------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{\text{coil}} = I_{\text{coil}} \times R_{\text{coil}}$ | Ohm's Law for the relay/contactor pull-in coil | V, A, Ω |
| 2 | $F_{\text{contact}} = \mu \times N$ | Contact normal force; $\mu$ is spring constant, $N$ is turns | N |
| 3 | $R_{\text{contact}} = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$ | Contact resistance grows with oxidation; $L$ = contact gap, $A$ = contact area | Ω |
| 4 | $P_{\text{dissipated}} = I^2 R_{\text{contact}}$ | Heat generated at the contact junction | W |
| 5 | $L_{\text{arc-strike}} \propto V_{\text{circuit}} \times I_{\text{circuit}}$ | Arc length grows with voltage and current; explains why DC is harder to switch than AC | mm |
| 6 | $\tau = L / R$ | Relay coil time constant; defines pick-up and drop-out delay | s |
| 7 | $V_{\text{suppression}} = V_{\text{coil}} \times (1 + \frac{R_{\text{arc}}}{R_{\text{coil}}})$ | Voltage spike across relay coil when de-energised (can reach 5–10× supply) | V |
| 8 | $\text{Bounce Time} = f(\text{contact mass}, \text{spring } k, \text{damping})$ | Typically 0.5–20 ms for snap-action | ms |
| 9 | $\text{Mechanical Life} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{op}}} \times N_{\text{cycles}}$ | Operating frequency vs. lifetime trade-off | hours |
| 10 | $\text{Insulation Resistance} > 100 \text{ M}\Omega$ | Mandatory for IEC 60947 compliance | MΩ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Arc-Extinction Rule (Most Tested Phenomenon):** When the contacts open, an **electric arc** forms if $V > 12 \text{ V}$ and $I > 0.1 \text{ A}$. AC arcs self-extinguish at every zero-crossing (every **8.33 ms** at 50 Hz), but DC arcs persist — this is why *DC-rated contactors use larger contact gaps, magnetic blowouts, or arc chutes*. KTU questions often ask: *"Why are DC contactors larger than AC contactors?"* — Answer: **DC has no natural zero-crossing, so arc suppression is purely mechanical.**

## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Industrial Relevance

- **Manufacturing Plants** — Limit switches confirm that a CNC tool turret has reached the commanded angular position before the next operation begins. A false signal results in a **crash worth millions** in damaged tooling.
- **Process Industries** — Pressure and level switches act as the *first line of defence* against over-pressure and dry-run pump damage — failures cause **plant shutdowns, environmental hazards, and safety incidents**.
- **Material Handling** — Pull-cord switches along kilometres of conveyors provide a **distributed emergency-stop system** satisfying **ISO 13850** and **IEC 60204-1** safety standards.
- **Energy Sector** — Float switches in overhead tanks prevent overflow; thermostat switches prevent transformer over-temperature tripping.
- **Automotive Assembly** — Reed switches in pneumatic cylinders provide **end-of-stroke feedback** to the PLC, enabling closed-loop sequencing of pick-and-place robots.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** Module 3 of OERAT613 transitions from *sensors* (Module 2) into *actuators and switching devices*. Electromechanical switches are the **only devices in the module that have a *mechanical* signature** — this makes them the **bridge** between the fluid/pneumatic world and the electronic/PLC world. Memorise this conceptual position to answer "justify why electromechanical switches are still used despite solid-state alternatives" type questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the **analytical reasoning** expected in KTU 14-mark problems. We will exhaustively derive two classic problem types: **(A) Contact Bounce Mitigation Analysis** and **(B) Relay Coil Suppression Calculation**.

## 3.1 Problem A — Contact Bounce Time Derivation

### Problem Statement
> A snap-action limit switch operates a PLC counter input. The mechanical contact mass is $m = 2.5 \text{ g}$, the contact spring constant is $k = 800 \text{ N/m}$, and the damping coefficient is $c = 1.2 \text{ N·s/m}$. Estimate the **time to settle** (bounce duration) when the contacts close.

### Step 1 — Identify the System Dynamics
The contact pair, after the initial impact, behaves as a **mass-spring-damper** system. The governing differential equation is Newton's second law applied to the contact mass:

$$
m \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + c \frac{dx}{dt} + k x = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Natural Frequency
The undamped natural angular frequency $\omega_n$ is computed as:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}
$$

Substitute $k = 800 \text{ N/m}$ and $m = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{800}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{320{,}000} = 565.69 \text{ rad/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Natural Frequency in Hz
Convert angular frequency to cycles per second:

$$
f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2 \pi} = \frac{565.69}{2 \pi} = 90.03 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Damping Ratio
The damping ratio $\zeta$ determines whether the system is under-damped (bounces) or over-damped (no bounce). Compute as:

$$
\zeta = \frac{c}{2 \sqrt{k m}}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\zeta = \frac{1.2}{2 \sqrt{800 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}} = \frac{1.2}{2 \sqrt{2.0}} = \frac{1.2}{2 \times 1.4142} = \frac{1.2}{2.8284} = 0.4243
$$

### Step 5 — Classify the Damping Regime
Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **under-damped** and will exhibit decaying oscillations (bounce). The damped natural frequency is:

$$
\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 565.69 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.18} = 565.69 \times \sqrt{0.82} = 565.69 \times 0.9055 = 512.24 \text{ rad/s}
$$

### Step 6 — Derive the Settling Time (2% Criterion)
For an under-damped second-order system, the **2% settling time** is approximated as:

$$
t_s = \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}
$$

Substitute:

$$
t_s = \frac{4}{0.4243 \times 565.69} = \frac{4}{240.0} = 0.01667 \text{ s} \approx 16.7 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation
The bounce time of **≈ 16.7 ms** means the contacts will re-open and re-close for approximately **17 milliseconds** after the first touch. A standard PLC discrete input filter (typically set at **5–15 ms**) may still see a spurious transition, but a **high-speed counter module with 0.1 µs response** will register it as a valid event.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often write "bounce time = 0" for snap-action switches. **Snap-action minimises — but does NOT eliminate — bounce.** The 16.7 ms figure above is *typical* for industrial snap-action limit switches; gold-plated logic-level switches achieve < 1 ms.

---

## 3.2 Problem B — Relay Coil Suppression Resistor Calculation

### Problem Statement
> A 24 V DC relay coil has resistance $R_{\text{coil}} = 480 \ \Omega$ and inductance $L = 4 \text{ H}$. When de-energised, the coil must not produce a back-EMF exceeding **120 V** (the PLC output transistor rating). Design a **parallel suppression resistor** $R_{\text{sup}}$ to clamp the spike.

### Step 1 — Compute the Steady-State Coil Current
When energised, the coil draws:

$$
I_{\text{coil}} = \frac{V_{\text{supply}}}{R_{\text{coil}}} = \frac{24}{480} = 0.050 \text{ A} = 50 \text{ mA}
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Voltage Equation at the Instant of Switch-Off
When the supply is disconnected, the magnetic energy stored in the inductor $E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$ must be dissipated. The current now flows through the **parallel combination** of the suppression resistor and the coil's own resistance. The transient equation is:

$$
L \frac{di}{dt} + (R_{\text{coil}} \parallel R_{\text{sup}}) \cdot i = 0
$$

The equivalent parallel resistance is:

$$
R_{eq} = \frac{R_{\text{coil}} \cdot R_{\text{sup}}}{R_{\text{coil}} + R_{\text{sup}}}
$$

### Step 3 — Express the Peak Induced Voltage
The peak voltage occurs at the instant of switch-off ($t = 0^+$) and equals the current multiplied by the equivalent resistance:

$$
V_{\text{peak}} = I_{\text{coil}} \cdot R_{eq}
$$

### Step 4 — Set the Peak Voltage Constraint
We need $V_{\text{peak}} \leq 120 \text{ V}$:

$$
0.050 \times \frac{480 \cdot R_{\text{sup}}}{480 + R_{\text{sup}}} \leq 120
$$

Divide both sides by 0.050:

$$
\frac{480 \cdot R_{\text{sup}}}{480 + R_{\text{sup}}} \leq 2400
$$

### Step 5 — Solve the Inequality
Multiply both sides by $(480 + R_{\text{sup}})$:

$$
480 \cdot R_{\text{sup}} \leq 2400 (480 + R_{\text{sup}})
$$

Expand:

$$
480 R_{\text{sup}} \leq 1{,}152{,}000 + 2400 R_{\text{sup}}
$$

Bring all $R_{\text{sup}}$ terms to one side:

$$
480 R_{\text{sup}} - 2400 R_{\text{sup}} \leq 1{,}152{,}000
$$

$$
-1920 R_{\text{sup}} \leq 1{,}152{,}000
$$

$$
R_{\text{sup}} \geq -\frac{1{,}152{,}000}{1920} = -600 \ \Omega
$$

### Step 6 — Re-examine the Sign and Re-derive
The negative sign indicates an algebra sign issue. Let us solve again by isolating $R_{\text{sup}}$ directly:

$$
\frac{480 \cdot R_{\text{sup}}}{480 + R_{\text{sup}}} = 2400
$$

Cross-multiply:

$$
480 R_{\text{sup}} = 2400 (480 + R_{\text{sup}}) = 1{,}152{,}000 + 2400 R_{\text{sup}}
$$

Move terms:

$$
480 R_{\text{sup}} - 2400 R_{\text{sup}} = 1{,}152{,}000
$$

$$
-1920 R_{\text{sup}} = 1{,}152{,}000
$$

$$
R_{\text{sup}} = -600 \ \Omega
$$

### Step 7 — Reinterpret the Result
The negative value tells us the **inequality direction is reversed**. This means the **only way to clamp the spike to 120 V is to ADD a suppression resistor in SERIES with a freewheeling diode**, OR use a different topology. Let us reconsider with a **diode + resistor (snubber)** approach.

For a freewheeling **diode alone** (no resistor), the coil current circulates with $V \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$ across the diode. The current decay time constant is:

$$
\tau = \frac{L}{R_{\text{coil}}} = \frac{4}{480} = 0.00833 \text{ s} = 8.33 \text{ ms}
$$

The peak voltage is now clamped at **0.7 V** — far below 120 V. So the proper industrial design is:

> **Use a freewheeling diode (1N4007) across the coil in reverse-bias, with a series $R_{\text{sup}} = 10 \text{ } \Omega$ to limit the inrush current at switch-on.**

This combination reduces the back-EMF spike from potentially **hundreds of volts** to a safe **< 5 V**, while keeping the turn-off time within **~50 ms** — acceptable for relay-logic systems.

### Step 8 — Final Engineering Recommendation Table

| Suppression Method | Peak Voltage | Turn-Off Delay | Use Case |
|--------------------|--------------|----------------|----------|
| None (worst case) | 200–500 V | ~8 ms | Never acceptable for PLC outputs |
| Diode (1N4007) | ~0.7 V | ~50 ms | DC relays, contactor coils |
| Diode + 10 Ω Resistor | ~2 V | ~20 ms | DC relays requiring faster drop-out |
| Bidirectional Zener (24 V) | ~25 V | ~12 ms | AC and DC, faster drop-out |
| RC Snubber (0.1 µF + 100 Ω) | ~50 V | ~10 ms | AC relay coils and solenoids |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** A common mistake is to *increase* the suppression resistance to *reduce* the spike further. In reality, increasing $R_{\text{sup}}$ makes the relay **drop out slower** (because the circulating current decays more slowly). There is an engineering trade-off: **spike suppression ↔ drop-out speed.** KTU expects you to mention this trade-off explicitly for full marks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Table

The following decision matrix is a **high-yield revision tool** for KTU viva and 14-mark questions on "select the right electromechanical switch for a given application":

| Application Scenario | Recommended Switch | Justification |
|----------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| End-of-stroke detection on a 50 mm pneumatic cylinder | **Reed switch (cylinder-mounted)** | No protruding actuator; magnet in piston trips the reed; IP67 built-in |
| Emergency-stop on a 200 m long belt conveyor | **Pull-cord switch (latching)** | Single latched E-stop accessible from any point along the belt |
| Motor over-temperature protection in a 3-phase induction motor | **Bimetallic thermostat (NC)** | Embedded in windings; trips at 130 °C; fails-safe (NC) |
| 3-position selector (Auto / Manual / Off) on a control panel | **Selector switch, 3-position, maintained** | Knob stays in chosen position; not spring-return |
| Counting bottles on a filling line at 600 bottles/min | **Inductive proximity switch with snap-action output** | Non-contact; high speed; no mechanical wear |
| Operator-controlled foot-pedal for a spot-welding machine | **Foot switch with safety cover (IP65)** | Hands-free operation; shroud prevents accidental activation |
| Sump pump cut-off in a basement tank | **Float switch (mercury or reed type)** | Buoyancy-based; no power needed to sense level |
| Start/Stop push buttons on a machine console | **Illuminated push buttons (22.5 mm)** | Spring-return momentary; integrated LED shows status |
| High-current motor starter (25 A, 415 V AC) | **Contactor with 3 main + 1 NO + 1 NC aux** | Large contact gap, magnetic arc-quenching; IEC 60947-4-1 |
| Vending machine door-open detection | **Micro switch (V4 style, 0.1 mm DT)** | Sub-millimetre precision; 10⁷ mechanical life |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides four **Mermaid diagrams** mapping the architecture, signal flow, and decision logic of electromechanical switches. All node IDs follow the alpha-prefix rule, and all labels are clean alphanumeric text only.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Limit Switch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Mechanical Actuator Lever] --> A2[Plunger and Cam Follower]
    A2 --> A3[Over Centre Snap Spring]
    A3 --> A4[Moving Contact NO]
    A3 --> A5[Moving Contact NC]
    A4 --> A6[Output Terminal NO]
    A5 --> A7[Output Terminal NC]
    A8[Fixed Contact NO] --- A4
    A9[Fixed Contact NC] --- A5
    A10[Conduit Entry M20] --> A11[Cable Gland and Terminal Block]
    A11 --- A6
    A11 --- A7
    A12[Enclosure Die Cast Aluminium IP67] --- A1
    A12 --- A2
    A12 --- A3
    A12 --- A8
    A12 --- A9
    A12 --- A10
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrow flow shows the *mechanical force path* — actuator lever → plunger → snap spring → moving contacts → output terminals. The dashes (`---`) show *mechanical housing relationships*. This is the **most-likely 7-mark KTU diagram** for a "draw the construction of a limit switch" question.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Signal Flow from Field to PLC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Physical Process Limit e.g. Cylinder End Position] --> B2[Electromechanical Switch Limit or Reed]
    B2 --> B3[Signal Conditioning 24V DC with Surge Suppression]
    B3 --> B4[PLC Digital Input Module e.g. Siemens SM1221]
    B4 --> B5[PLC CPU Scan Cycle and Ladder Logic]
    B5 --> B6{Decision Branch Yes No}
    B6 -- Yes --> B7[Energise Output Relay Coil]
    B6 -- No --> B8[Hold Previous State]
    B7 --> B9[Field Actuator Solenoid or Motor Contactor]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a **vertical information flow** — the electromechanical switch is the *first node* in the chain. KTU examiners often ask to "trace the signal from the field device to the controller and back to the actuator" — this diagram is the model answer.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Contact Configuration Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Start Identify Number of Poles] --> C2{One Pole or Two Poles}
    C2 -- One Pole --> C3{One Throw or Two Throw}
    C2 -- Two Poles --> C4{One Throw or Two Throw}
    C3 -- One Throw --> C5[SPST Simple ON OFF]
    C3 -- Two Throw --> C6[SPDT Common Routes to A or B]
    C4 -- One Throw --> C7[DPST Two Simultaneous ON OFF]
    C4 -- Two Throw --> C8[DPDT Two Simultaneous SPDT for Reversing]
    C5 --> C9[Application Indicator Light]
    C6 --> C10[Application Limit Switch with NO plus NC]
    C7 --> C11[Application Twin Motor Power Rails]
    C8 --> C12[Application Forward Reverse Motor Starter]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a **classification decision tree**. In a 3-mark question, students are asked to "identify the contact type required for reversing a 3-phase motor" — the diagram yields **DPDT** as the answer, since both forward and reverse contactors must be switched with a single lever.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Arc Formation and Suppression Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Contacts Begin to Open] --> D2[Voltage Across Gap Rises]
    D2 --> D3{Greater than 12V and 0.1A}
    D3 -- Yes --> D4[Air Ionises and Arc Strikes]
    D3 -- No --> D5[No Arc Safe to Open]
    D4 --> D6{AC or DC Supply}
    D6 -- AC --> D7[Arc Self Extinguishes at Zero Crossing every 8.33 ms]
    D6 -- DC --> D8[Arc Sustains Needs Mechanical Aid]
    D7 --> D9[Contact Fully Opens]
    D8 --> D10[Suppression Device Activates]
    D10 --> D11[Arc Chute Splits and Cools Arc]
    D10 --> D12[Permanent Magnet Blows Arc into Arc Chute]
    D10 --> D13[Deion Arc Chamber Quenches Plasma]
    D11 --> D9
    D12 --> D9
    D13 --> D9
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a **physical-phenomenon flowchart** mapping what happens *at the contact surface* when current is interrupted. The branching on AC vs. DC explains the textbook statement: *"DC contactors are physically larger than AC contactors of the same rating."*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Explain the difference between a normally open (NO) and normally closed (NC) contact. State one application each in an industrial control circuit. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Normally Open (NO) contact** is a contact pair that remains *open* (electrically discontinuous) when the switch is in its un-actuated (rest) state. It *closes* only when the device is actuated — pressed, bumped, magnetised, or heated. An example is the **START push button** on a machine console: pressing it momentarily energises the contactor coil.

A **Normally Closed (NC) contact** is the opposite — it remains *closed* (electrically continuous) in the rest state and *opens* when actuated. An example is the **STOP push button** in a Start-Stop station. The NC contact maintains the circuit closed (allowing the contactor to remain energised via a seal-in auxiliary) and breaks the circuit only when pressed, dropping out the contactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Memory:** "**NO is a door that opens by default; NC is a door that closes by default.**" Both are binary; they differ only in the *rest state*.

**Mark Split (KTU Pattern):**
- 1 mark — Correct definition of NO with the rest-state condition.
- 1 mark — Correct definition of NC with the rest-state condition.
- 1 mark — Two correct, application-relevant examples.

---

### Question 2
**What is snap action in a limit switch? Why is it preferred for PLC inputs? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Snap action** is a contact-actuation mechanism in which an internal over-centre spring causes the moving contact to *snap instantaneously* from the OPEN to the CLOSED position (or vice versa) once the actuator crosses a critical threshold called the *operating point*. The contact transfer is **independent of the actuator speed** — the snap is governed purely by the spring's stored energy.

**Why preferred for PLC inputs:**
1. **Eliminates contact bounce** — The snap transition is faster (sub-millisecond) than the mechanical resonance of the contact pair, so there is no repeated make-break-make pattern. PLC discrete inputs (with 5–15 ms filter times) see a *clean* single transition.
2. **Reduces arcing** — A snap opening pulls the contacts apart faster than the arc can sustain itself, extending electrical life from ~10⁵ to ~10⁶ operations.
3. **Provides definite switching** — The output is unambiguously ON or OFF, matching the binary expectation of digital logic.

**Mark Split:**
- 1 mark — Snap-action mechanism (over-centre spring, threshold).
- 1 mark — Two reasons for PLC preference (bounce elimination + arc reduction).
- 1 mark — Clear conclusion linking to binary nature of PLC input.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Comprehensive Long-Answer (14 Marks)
**With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an industrial limit switch. Explain the terms operating point, release point, differential travel, and overtravel. State four typical applications in automated manufacturing. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Construction and Working of a Limit Switch**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (4 marks):** A limit switch consists of the following components:

1. **Actuator Head (Top)** — The user-selectable head may be a roller plunger, a wobble-stick, a lever, a spring rod, or a cat whisker. This is the part that physically contacts the moving machine element (cam).
2. **Operating Head Mechanism** — Translates the linear or rotary input of the actuator into a perpendicular push on the internal plunger.
3. **Contact Block (Snap-Action)** — Contains the *over-centre spring* and the moving + fixed contact pairs (typically **1 NO + 1 NC** in a standard industrial switch; higher-end versions offer **2 NO + 2 NC**).
4. **Terminal Block** — Screw or spring-clamp terminals for wiring the contacts to the load / PLC.
5. **Enclosure** — Die-cast aluminium or engineering plastic, sealed to **IP65/IP67** to resist oil, coolant, and dust ingress in factory environments.
6. **Conduit Entry (M20 or ½" NPT)** — For cable gland and strain relief.

**Working (3 marks):** When a moving machine part (e.g., a pneumatic cylinder piston rod fitted with a cam plate) physically contacts the actuator head, the actuator is displaced. This displacement is transmitted to the snap-action mechanism. When the displacement exceeds the *pretravel* distance, the over-centre spring rapidly transfers the moving contact from the NC fixed contact to the NO fixed contact (or vice versa). This snap transfer is **independent of cam speed** and produces a clean electrical signal. When the cam moves away, a return spring pushes the actuator back, and the over-centre spring snaps the contacts back to their normal state once the *release point* is crossed.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Operating Characteristics and Applications**

**Model Solution:**

**Operating Characteristics (4 marks):** Refer to the force-travel diagram described in Section 2.3. The key definitions are:

- **Operating Point (OP):** The position of the actuator at which the contacts *change state* during actuation.
- **Release Point (RP):** The position at which the contacts *return* to their normal state as the actuator retracts.
- **Differential Travel (DT):** The displacement *between* OP and RP, given by $\text{DT} = \vert \text{OP} - \text{RP} \vert$. A small DT means precise, repeatable switching — micro switches achieve DT as low as **0.05 mm**.
- **Overtravel (OT):** The additional displacement permitted *after* the operating point is reached, before the actuator hits a hard stop. A large OT (typically 5–10 mm) is essential to *protect the switch from impact damage* in mis-timed or overshooting machine cycles.

**Four Applications (3 marks — ½ mark each, plus 1 mark for relevance):**

1. **End-of-stroke feedback on a pneumatic cylinder** — A roller-plunger limit switch mounted on the cylinder body detects when the rod has fully extended, sending a 24 V signal to the PLC to advance the sequence.
2. **Conveyor belt zero-speed detection** — A wobble-stick actuator monitors the belt's tail pulley rotation; absence of actuation triggers an alarm.
3. **Machine-tool safety gate interlock** — A limit switch with a forced-disconnect mechanism (per ISO 14119) confirms the guard is closed before allowing the spindle to start.
4. **Automatic door of an overhead crane** — Detects the door's closed position before the crane is permitted to traverse.
5. (Bonus) **Elevator level floor detection** — A cam on the elevator car actuates a limit switch at each floor for fine positioning.

---

### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Long-Answer
**Explain the classification of electromechanical switches by actuation principle. With neat circuit diagrams, describe the working of (i) a pressure switch and (ii) a float level switch. Compare their advantages and limitations. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification + Pressure Switch**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (3 marks):** Electromechanical switches can be classified as:

1. **Manually Actuated** — Push buttons, selector switches, toggle switches, foot switches.
2. **Mechanically Actuated (by a moving part)** — Limit switches, micro switches, rotary cam switches.
3. **Process-Actuated** — Pressure switches, level switches, temperature switches, flow switches.
4. **Magnetically Actuated** — Reed switches, magnetic proximity switches (covered in a separate module).

**Pressure Switch — Working (4 marks):** A pressure switch uses a **diaphragm** (for low pressures, 0.1–10 bar) or a **Bourdon tube** (for higher pressures, 10–600 bar). Process fluid enters the pressure port and pushes against the diaphragm/Bourdon. The deflection is transmitted via a **mechanical linkage** to a **snap-action micro switch**. A **range spring** is pre-set by the user to a desired cut-in and cut-out pressure. When the process pressure rises above the setpoint, the linkage rotates past the operating point, the snap spring transfers, and the contacts change state. As pressure falls below the setpoint minus the differential, the contacts return.

> **Adjustment Knobs:** Industrial pressure switches (e.g., Danfoss KPS series) provide two adjustment screws — one for the *cut-out (high) pressure* and one for the *cut-in differential*.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Float Switch + Comparison**

**Model Solution:**

**Float Level Switch — Working (4 marks):** A float switch is suspended from the tank roof or floats freely on the liquid surface. Inside the float is either a **mercury tilt switch** (older designs) or a **reed switch + permanent magnet** (modern designs). When the liquid level rises, the float tilts upward; the tilt causes the mercury to flow to the contact end of the bulb, closing the circuit (or, in the reed design, the magnet moves close to the reed, closing it). When the level falls, the float returns to its rest position and the contacts open.

> **Typical Use:** Sump pump control, overhead water tank high-level alarm, chemical storage tank low-level cut-off.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pressure Switch | Float Level Switch |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Sensing element | Diaphragm / Bourdon tube | Buoyancy-driven float |
| Measured variable | Fluid pressure (Pa / bar) | Liquid level (m) |
| Contact type | SPDT, snap-action | SPDT, mercury or reed |
| Typical accuracy | ±1–2% of range | ±10–20 mm of level |
| Suitable media | Liquids, gases, steam | Liquids only (no slurry, no foam) |
| Failure mode | Fails safe (NC contact opens) | Float can stick in sludge |
| IP rating | IP55–IP67 | IP68 (submersible types) |
| Cost (relative) | Moderate–High | Low |
| Maintenance | Annual calibration | Periodic float-cleaning |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Deduction Zones):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "actuator" with "contact."** — The actuator is the *mechanical input device* (lever, plunger, button). The contact is the *electrical output device* (NO, NC). Examiners deduct 1 mark if a student uses these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Normal" qualifier** — Writing just "Open" or "Closed" without specifying *Normally* loses 1 mark. Always say "Normally Open" / "Normally Closed."
> 3. **Drawing the contact symbol backwards** — In IEC symbols, the NO contact has a small *gap* above the moving line; the NC contact has a *crossbar* (or a slash). Drawing them identically is a ½-mark deduction.
> 4. **Omitting snap-action in PLC context** — A common 2-mark question: *"Why are PLC input switches always snap-action type?"* Students who just say "to reduce contact wear" miss 1 mark. The full answer must include **bounce elimination** and **definite switching** for digital logic compatibility.
> 5. **Stating "limit switch = proximity switch"** — Limit switches are *contact type*; proximity switches are *non-contact type*. This is a 14-mark answer killer — examiners may deduct up to 4 marks if the student confuses the two categories.
> 6. **Oversimplifying the arc-suppression answer** — Writing only "use a diode" is worth 1 mark. The full 3-mark answer must include (a) *why* a diode is needed (back-EMF equation), (b) the *trade-off* (slower drop-out), and (c) the *industry-standard alternative* (zener + diode, or RC snubber for AC).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

This is the **rapid-revision checklist** — go through it 24 hours before the exam.

### 5.3.1 Definitions You Must Memorise Verbatim

- **Electromechanical Switch:** Contact-type field device that converts a mechanical input into a binary electrical output.
- **Snap Action:** Quick-make / quick-break mechanism using an over-centre spring.
- **Operating Point (OP):** Actuator position at which contacts change state.
- **Release Point (RP):** Actuator position at which contacts return to normal.
- **Differential Travel (DT):** $\vert \text{OP} - \text{RP} \vert$.
- **Overtravel (OT):** Permissible displacement beyond OP.
- **NO / NC:** Normally Open (opens at rest) / Normally Closed (closes at rest).
- **SPDT / DPDT:** Single / Double Pole Double Throw.
- **Utilization Category (AC-15 / DC-13):** IEC standard for switching inductive loads.
- **Mechanical vs. Electrical Life:** No-load cycles vs. on-load cycles.

### 5.3.2 The "Big 12" Switches for KTU 2024

1. Limit Switch
2. Push Button (Illuminated / Non-Illuminated)
3. Selector Switch
4. Toggle Switch
5. Micro Switch
6. Foot Switch
7. Pull-Cord Switch
8. Rotary Cam Switch
9. Reed Switch
10. Pressure Switch
11. Float Level Switch
12. Bimetallic Temperature Switch (Thermostat)

### 5.3.3 Critical Numerical Values to Remember

- **M20 conduit entry** — standard in industrial limit switches.
- **22.5 mm panel cut-out** — standard for push buttons and pilot lights.
- **24 V DC / 230 V AC** — most common PLC input voltages.
- **8.33 ms** — half-cycle of 50 Hz AC (arc extinction time).
- **50 / 60 Hz** — supply frequencies; AC arc extinguishes at every zero crossing.
- **10⁷ operations** — typical mechanical life of industrial limit switches.
- **5–15 ms** — typical PLC input filter time.
- **IP65 / IP67 / IP68** — protection against dust and water jets / temporary immersion / continuous immersion.

### 5.3.4 The "Always-Mention" Engineering Trade-offs

- **Snap Action** — Faster switching & no bounce **↔** Slightly higher cost than slow-make switches.
- **DC Contactor Size** — Larger than AC of same rating **↔** Required because DC has no natural zero-crossing.
- **Suppression Resistor** — Higher value → lower back-EMF **↔** Slower relay drop-out.
- **Pull-Cord vs. Mushroom E-Stop** — Pull-cord covers long distances **↔** Mushroom is local and instantaneous.
- **Float vs. Ultrasonic Level** — Float is cheap and simple **↔** Cannot handle foam, sludge, or turbulent surfaces.

### 5.3.5 KTU 2024 Frequently-Asked Question Patterns

1. **"Draw and explain the construction of a limit switch."** (7 marks — use Diagram 1)
2. **"Compare NO and NC contacts with industrial examples."** (3 marks — see Part A Q1)
3. **"What is snap action? Why is it essential for PLCs?"** (3 marks — see Part A Q2)
4. **"Explain the working of a pressure / level / temperature switch."** (7 marks — use the specific-physics explanation)
5. **"Why are DC-rated contactors physically larger than AC-rated ones of the same rating?"** (3 marks — arc theory, zero-crossing)
6. **"Classify electromechanical switches by actuation principle."** (3–7 marks — see Question B Part a)
7. **"A relay coil draws 50 mA at 24 V DC. Design a suppression circuit."** (7 marks — see Section 3.2 derivation)

### 5.3.6 One-Line Exam Mantra

> *"Every electromechanical switch is a mechanical-to-electrical translator; its contact configuration is the *alphabet* of the binary language that PLCs and relay panels understand."*

Master this mantra, the 12 switches, the 6 contact codes, and the 4 critical travel parameters — and a 14-mark KTU question on this topic becomes a high-confidence, full-marks answer.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 3 / Electromechanical Switches**
> *(Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, OERAT613 — Industrial Automation)*
