# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a Pneumatic-to-Electrical (P/E) converter. State the standard input and output signal ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Pneumatic-to-Electrical (P/E) converter is a signal-conditioning device that converts a **variable pneumatic pressure input signal** (standardised at **3–15 psig** or **20–100 kPa**) into a **proportional electrical output signal** (standardised at **4–20 mA DC** or **1–5 V DC**).

**Standard Ranges:**
- **Pneumatic input:** 3 psig (lower) to 15 psig (upper) — span = 12 psig.
- **Electrical output:** 4 mA (lower) to 20 mA (upper) — span = 16 mA.

It is used to **interface pneumatic field instruments with electronic control systems** (DCS, PLC, SCADA) in modern process automation. The "live zero" of both signals (3 psi and 4 mA) helps in **fault detection** — a reading below these values indicates a broken line.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the concept of "live zero" in pneumatic and electrical signal standards with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept of Live Zero:** A "live zero" means that the **lowest normal operating value** of a signal is **above zero** (not zero itself). This is a deliberate engineering convention in industrial instrumentation.

**Pneumatic Live Zero:** The standard lower range is **3 psig (not 0 psig)**. The signal range is 3–15 psig.

**Electrical Live Zero:** The standard lower range is **4 mA DC (not 0 mA)**. The signal range is 4–20 mA.

**Example (Fault Detection):**
- If a pneumatic line is broken, the pressure drops to **0 psig** — below the live zero of 3 psig. This **immediately indicates a fault** rather than a legitimate zero measurement.
- Similarly, if a 4–20 mA wire is broken, the current drops to **0 mA** — below the 4 mA live zero. The PLC/DCS can be programmed to flag this as a **"signal fail" alarm**.

The live zero principle is **critical for plant safety** and is the reason why 3–15 psi and 4–20 mA became global standards.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 3 contains an internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 - Module 3 Choice 1]**

**Q.A (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a force-balance type Pneumatic-to-Electrical (P/E) converter. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **force-balance (null-balance) P/E converter** is the industry-standard design for high-accuracy conversion of pneumatic signals to electrical current.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 functional architecture)

**Working Principle — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Pneumatic Input Application:** The input pressure $P_{in}$ (3–15 psig) is fed into a **metallic bellows or capsule**. The bellows expands by a small displacement proportional to $P_{in}$.

2. **Measuring Force Generation:** The bellows exerts a **measuring force** on a pivoted beam:
$$F_m = P_{in} \times A_e$$
where $A_e$ is the effective bellows area.

3. **Beam Deflection & Nozzle-Flapper Motion:** The force tilts the beam, moving a **flapper** closer to (or further from) a **nozzle**. This changes the back-pressure in the nozzle.

4. **Position Sensing:** An **LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) detects the small beam displacement and produces a proportional AC voltage.

5. **Electronic Amplification:** The LVDT signal is rectified and amplified by an **op-amp** stage to drive the output current loop.

6. **Current Output:** The amplifier output is the **4–20 mA DC** current, which is sent to the load (DCS/PLC).

7. **Electromagnetic Feedback:** The same output current flows through a **feedback coil** placed in a **permanent magnetic field**. This generates an electromagnetic force:
$$F_{fb} = k_m \times I_{out} \times N$$

8. **Null-Balance Equilibrium:** The feedback force acts on the **opposite end** of the beam, opposing $F_m$. Equilibrium is reached when:
$$F_m = F_{fb} \Rightarrow P_{in} \cdot A_e = k_m \cdot I_{out} \cdot N$$
This gives a **linear** relationship between $I_{out}$ and $P_{in}$.

**Advantages:**
- Excellent linearity (hysteresis-free).
- High accuracy (±0.1% of span).
- Beam movement is in micro-inches → minimal friction.
- Long-term stability.

**Conclusion:** The force-balance principle converts pneumatic pressure to current with high fidelity by continuously nulling the mechanical deflection through electromagnetic feedback. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.A (b) A P/E converter has the following specifications: input range = 3 to 15 psig, output range = 4 to 20 mA. If the input pressure is 10.5 psig, calculate: (i) the output current, (ii) the output voltage across a 250 Ω load, and (iii) the percentage of span for both input and output. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_{LRV} = 3$ psig, $P_{URV} = 15$ psig
- $I_{LRV} = 4$ mA, $I_{URV} = 20$ mA
- $P_{in} = 10.5$ psig
- $R_{load} = 250$ Ω

**(i) Output Current:**

Step 1: Find the input percentage of span.

$$
\% P_{in} = \frac{P_{in} - P_{LRV}}{P_{URV} - P_{LRV}} \times 100 = \frac{10.5 - 3}{15 - 3} \times 100 = \frac{7.5}{12} \times 100 = 62.5\%
$$

Step 2: Apply the same percentage to the output range.

$$
I_{out} = I_{LRV} + \left( \frac{\% P_{in}}{100} \right) \times (I_{URV} - I_{LRV}) = 4 + 0.625 \times 16 = 4 + 10 = \mathbf{14 \text{ mA}}
$$

**(ii) Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{out} = I_{out} \times R_{load} = 14 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = \mathbf{3.5 \text{ V}}
$$

**(iii) Percentage of Span:**

- Input % of span: **62.5%** (computed above)
- Output % of span: $\dfrac{14 - 4}{20 - 4} \times 100 = \dfrac{10}{16} \times 100 = \mathbf{62.5\%}$

**Verification (using direct formula):**

$$
I_{out} = 4 + \frac{4}{3}(10.5 - 3) = 4 + \frac{4}{3}(7.5) = 4 + 10 = 14 \text{ mA} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result Summary:** $I_{out} = 14$ mA, $V_{out} = 3.5$ V, % Span = 62.5% for both input and output. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Module 3 Choice 2]**

**Q.B (a) Compare the working principle of force-balance and direct-displacement type P/E converters. List two advantages of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Force-Balance P/E Converter | Direct-Displacement P/E Converter |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Uses **negative feedback** to nullify beam motion; output current generates opposing force. | Mechanical displacement is **sensed directly** by LVDT, strain gauge, or capacitive element. |
| **Beam Motion** | Very small (micro-inches) | Larger, full displacement range |
| **Accuracy** | Very high (±0.1% to ±0.5% FS) | Moderate (±1% to ±2% FS) |
| **Hysteresis** | Negligible | Present (due to friction) |
| **Linearity** | Excellent (inherently linear) | Depends on sensor linearity |
| **Sensitivity** | High (force amplification possible) | Limited by sensor |
| **Complexity** | Higher (feedback loop, amplifier) | Simpler |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (feedback coil, amp) | Lower |

**Two Advantages of Force-Balance Type:**
1. **Hysteresis-free operation** because the beam barely moves — friction and mechanical non-linearities are eliminated.
2. **Excellent linearity and accuracy** (typically ±0.1% of span) suitable for **precision process control** and reference-grade instrumentation.

**Two Advantages of Direct-Displacement Type:**
1. **Simpler construction** with fewer components — easier to manufacture, calibrate, and maintain.
2. **Lower cost and lower power consumption** — suitable for **field-mounted, low-power applications** where high accuracy is not critical.

**Conclusion:** Force-balance converters are preferred for **precision control loops** (refineries, DCS), while direct-displacement converters are used in **cost-sensitive field applications** where moderate accuracy is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.B (b) With the help of a functional block diagram, explain how a P/E converter and an E/P converter together form a hybrid control loop. State one application. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of a Hybrid Control Loop:**

A **hybrid control loop** uses **pneumatic field devices** for sensing/actuation and **electronic devices** for control/decision-making. The P/E and E/P converters serve as the **translators** at the boundaries.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 diagram)

**Working — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Sensing (Field side):** A **pneumatic transmitter** (e.g., pressure, flow, level) senses the process variable and outputs a **3–15 psig** signal.

2. **P/E Conversion:** The **P/E converter** at the boundary receives the 3–15 psig signal and converts it to a **4–20 mA DC** electrical signal.

3. **Electronic Control:** The **electronic PID controller** (DCS or PLC) receives the 4–20 mA signal, performs the **control algorithm** (compute error, integral, derivative), and generates a **corrective 4–20 mA output**.

4. **E/P Conversion:** The **E/P converter** receives the 4–20 mA controller output and converts it back to a **3–15 psig** pneumatic signal.

5. **Actuation (Field side):** The pneumatic signal drives a **pneumatic valve actuator** (diaphragm actuator or piston actuator) which adjusts the process.

6. **Closed Loop:** The new process condition is sensed, completing the loop.

**Application (one example):**
**Refinery Distillation Column Control** — A pneumatic temperature transmitter on the column senses tray temperature and outputs 3–15 psig. The P/E converter interfaces this to a DCS, which compares with the setpoint and outputs 4–20 mA. The E/P converter drives a pneumatic reflux control valve. This combination is common in older refineries that are being modernised incrementally.

**Advantages of Hybrid Loops:**
- **Intrinsic safety:** Pneumatic field side is safe in hazardous (explosive) areas.
- **Reliability:** Pneumatic actuators survive extreme temperatures, dust, and vibration.
- **Modern control:** Electronic brain enables advanced algorithms (cascade, feedforward, adaptive).
- **Cost-effective retrofit:** Legacy pneumatic instruments don't need to be replaced. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the "Live Zero" (–1 to –2 marks):** When computing output for a given input, students often treat the input range as 0–12 psig or output as 0–16 mA. **Always remember:** 3 psig ↔ 4 mA and 15 psig ↔ 20 mA. Offsets matter!
> 
> 2. **Unit Mix-up (–1 mark):** Mixing psig, kPa, bar, and mm H₂O without conversion. KTU questions sometimes switch units mid-problem. Always normalise to one unit (preferably kPa or psig) before computing.
> 
> 3. **Confusing P/E with E/P (–2 marks):** P/E = **P**neumatic input → **E**lectrical output. E/P = **E**lectrical input → **P**neumatic output. The direction is critical and frequently tested.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the Force-Balance Equation Derivation (–2 to –3 marks):** If the question asks to "derive the transfer function," simply writing $I_{out} = K \cdot P_{in}$ without showing $F_m = F_{fb}$ gets partial marks only. Always start from $P_{in} \cdot A_e = k_m \cdot I_{out} \cdot N$.
> 
> 5. **Block Diagram Omission (–2 marks):** Any "with neat block diagram" question without a diagram is capped at 60% marks. Always include a clear, labelled block diagram.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Real-World Examples (–1 mark):** KTU examiners reward practical application. Always conclude with at least one industrial example.
> 
> 7. **In hybrid loop questions, students often forget to mention the E/P converter in the actuation path**, describing only the P/E converter. Both must appear for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** P/E converter = pneumatic-pressure-input (3–15 psig) → electrical-current-output (4–20 mA DC) device.
- **Standard Ranges (must memorise):** Pneumatic = **3–15 psig (20–100 kPa)**; Electrical = **4–20 mA DC**.
- **Live Zero:** Both signals start above zero (3 psi, 4 mA) → enables fault detection (0 psi or 0 mA = broken line).
- **Core Force-Balance Equation:** $P_{in} \cdot A_e = k_m \cdot I_{out} \cdot N$
- **Output Transfer Function:** $I_{out} = 4 + \dfrac{4}{3}(P_{in} - 3)$ mA (when $P_{in}$ in psig).
- **Percentage Method:** Output % = Input % = $\dfrac{\text{Input} - LRV}{URV - LRV} \times 100\%$
- **Two Major Types:**
  1. **Force-Balance** (null-balance, high accuracy ±0.1% FS) — industry standard.
  2. **Direct-Displacement** (LVDT, strain gauge, capacitive) — simpler, lower cost.
- **Sensing Element:** Bellows or capsule (converts pressure to mechanical force).
- **Position Sensor:** LVDT (most common) or strain gauge.
- **Feedback Mechanism:** Electromagnetic coil in a magnetic field provides opposing force.
- **Key Advantages:** Linearity, accuracy, reliability, intrinsic safety compatibility, live-zero fault detection.
- **Hybrid Loop Architecture:** Field (pneumatic) → P/E → Electronic Controller → E/P → Field (pneumatic actuator).
- **Applications:** Refineries, chemical plants, cement mills, paper mills, pharmaceutical batch control, power plant boiler control.
- **KTU Exam Pattern (Module 3, 14-mark question):** Always includes a working-principle sub-part (with block diagram) and a numerical/problem sub-part. **Practice both.**
- **Most Tested Formula:** $I_{out} = 4 + \dfrac{4}{3}(P_{in} - 3)$ mA — **memorise and apply confidently**.
- **Common Confusions to Avoid:** P/E vs E/P direction; force-balance vs direct-displacement; live-zero vs true zero; mA vs mV output distinction.
- **Examiner's Heaviest Weight Topics:** Force-balance derivation, percentage normalisation, hybrid loop block diagram, advantages/disadvantages comparison.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Circuits and Hands-on Models on Material Handling Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Circuits and Hands-on Models on Material Handling Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Material Handling Systems (MHS)** in industrial automation refer to the integrated assembly of mechanical, electrical, electronic, pneumatic, hydraulic, and computer-controlled subsystems designed to transport, protect, store, and control the flow of materials and products throughout a manufacturing or distribution facility with minimal human intervention.

A **Material Handling Circuit** is a logically structured control system — typically implemented using **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC)**, **electro-pneumatic actuators**, **sensors**, and **motor drives** — that executes a pre-defined sequence of operations such as picking, conveying, sorting, lifting, and placing of workpieces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *Material handling automation encompasses the design, integration, and control of equipment used to move, store, and process materials using automated mechanisms. The KTU 2024 OERAT613 syllabus emphasizes hands-on circuit design using PLCs, pneumatic systems, and sensors to construct functional prototypes.*

**Key Sub-Systems under Material Handling:**

| Sub-System | Primary Function | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Conveyor Systems | Continuous transport of goods | Assembly lines, packaging |
| Pick & Place Robots | Discrete transfer of objects | Sorting, palletizing |
| Automated Storage/Retrieval (AS/RS) | Warehousing with retrieval logic | Inventory management |
| Pneumatic/Hydraulic Lifters | Vertical positioning | Press shops, foundries |
| AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) | Floor-level autonomous transport | Warehouses, hospitals |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Luggage System"

Imagine a **modern airport baggage handling system**:
- Your suitcase is placed on a moving belt (**conveyor**)
- A barcode scanner reads the tag (**sensor identification**)
- A diverter pushes it onto the correct ramp (**actuator**)
- The PLC orchestrating the entire process acts like the **air traffic controller**

This is exactly how industrial material handling works — the *workpiece* is the suitcase, the *sensors* are the scanners, the *actuators* are the diverters, and the *PLC* is the controller making split-second decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** If you can design a circuit that automatically sorts colored balls into different bins, you have essentially understood the core of industrial material handling automation.

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard Air Pressure (Pneumatics):** **6 bar (≈ 600 kPa)** industrial supply
- **Standard Hydraulic Pressure:** **70–210 bar** industrial supply
- **Cylinder Stroke Velocity:** Typically **0.1 – 1.5 m/s** for pneumatic
- **Conveyor Belt Speed:** **0.5 – 2.5 m/s** (assembly) up to **5 m/s** (bulk)
- **PLC Scan Time:** **1–10 ms** standard
- **VFD Switching Frequency:** **2–16 kHz** typical
- **Gravity Constant (Design Reference):** **g = 9.81 m/s²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Every material handling design must satisfy three KTU-cited criteria: **(1) Safety, (2) Reliability, (3) Throughput.** Skipping any one leads to a failed industrial deployment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Architecture of a Typical Material Handling Automation Cell

A KTU-compliant material handling cell consists of **four logical layers**, each with distinct design responsibilities:

### Layer 1 — Sensing Layer (Inputs)
- **Inductive Proximity Sensors** → Detect metallic workpieces
- **Capacitive Proximity Sensors** → Detect non-metallic (plastic, wood, liquid)
- **Photoelectric Sensors** → Detect object presence via light beam
- **Limit Switches** → Detect actuator end positions (extended/retracted)
- **Encoders** → Measure position/speed of conveyors
- **Color/RFID/Barcode Sensors** → Identify object type

### Layer 2 — Control Layer (Decision Engine)
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** — Ladder Logic, FBD, or Structured Text
- **Microcontroller-based embedded boards** (Arduino, Raspberry Pi) — academic prototypes
- **Industrial PC (IPC)** — SCADA/HMI integration

### Layer 3 — Actuation Layer (Outputs)
- **Pneumatic Cylinders (single/double acting)**
- **DC/AC Motors + Drives (VFD)**
- **Solenoid Valves (5/2, 5/3, 3/2 way)**
- **Conveyor belts, gears, racks & pinions**

### Layer 4 — Mechanical Layer
- Frame, guides, fixtures, gripping mechanisms, structural supports

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this Layered Architecture?** It mirrors the KTU 2024 outcome-based learning approach: students must demonstrate competency in **sensing (CO3)**, **control logic (CO4)**, and **actuation (CO5)** as distinct, assessable skill sets.

## 2.2 Design Workflow — From Problem Statement to Working Prototype

**Step 1 — Define the Operation Sequence**
- List the **Start → Process → End** events in I/O table form
- Example: *Detect → Conveyor ON → Sensor triggers → Pneumatic cylinder extends → Delay → Cylinder retracts → Loop*

**Step 2 — Identify I/O Allocation**

$$
\text{Number of Inputs} = N_{\text{sensors}} + N_{\text{limit switches}} + N_{\text{buttons}} + N_{\text{emergencyswitches}}
$$

$$
\text{Number of Outputs} = N_{\text{contactors}} + N_{\text{solenoid valves}} + N_{\text{indicators}} + N_{\text{motor starters}}
$$

**Step 3 — Ladder Logic Synthesis**
Convert the I/O table into **ladder rungs** using:
- Normally Open (NO) and Normally Closed (NC) contacts
- Latching (Set/Reset) coils
- Timers (TON, TOF, TP)
- Counters (CTU, CTD)
- Master Control Relay (MCR) for safety

**Step 4 — Pneumatic / Electrical Schematic Drafting**
Follow **ISO 1219** standards for pneumatic symbols and **IEC 60617** for electrical symbols.

**Step 5 — Simulation & Debugging**
Use **FluidSIM (pneumatics)**, **PLCSIM (Siemens)**, or **Tinkercad (basic PLC)** before hardware integration.

**Step 6 — Hardware Implementation**
Wire components on **breadboard** or **PCB**, mount on **aluminum profile frame**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \times A$ | $F$ = Thrust, $P$ = Pressure, $A$ = Piston Area | Cylinder output force | N, Pa, m² |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$ | $A$ = Piston area, $D$ = Bore diameter | Effective cylinder area | m² |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $v$ = Piston velocity, $Q$ = Flow rate | Cylinder extension speed | m/s, m³/s |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | $T$ = Torque, $r$ = radius, $J$ = inertia | Conveyor/rotational dynamics | N·m, kg·m² |
| 5 | $F_{\text{conveyor}} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g + m \cdot a$ | $\mu$ = friction coeff, $m$ = mass, $a$ = accel | Force to drive belt | N |
| 6 | $P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta}$ | $P$ = Power, $\eta$ = efficiency | Motor power sizing | W |
| 7 | $N_{\text{PLC I/O}} = N_{\text{in}} + N_{\text{out}}$ | — | Total I/O count for sizing | Integer |
| 8 | $t_{\text{scan}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | $t_i$ = instruction exec time | PLC scan time | ms |
| 9 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{ext}} + T_{\text{ret}} + 2T_d$ | $T_d$ = dwell time | Cylinder cycle time | s |
| 10 | $S_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | — | Pieces per hour (PPH) | parts/hr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** KTU frequently asks students to size a pneumatic cylinder or calculate the cycle time. Memorize formulas 1, 3, 9, and 10 — they appear in **80% of past papers**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| Automotive Assembly | Robotic pick-and-place of body panels |
| FMCG Packaging | Bottle filling, capping, labeling lines |
| Pharmaceutical | Vial sorting, blister-pack indexing |
| E-commerce Warehouses | Amazon-style Kiva robots (AGV) |
| Food & Beverage | Conveyorized sorting by weight/vision |
| Semiconductor | Wafer-handling in cleanrooms |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pneumatic Cylinder Force & Sizing

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder is required to push a 25 kg load vertically upward against gravity with a 30% safety factor. The supply pressure is **6 bar** (industrial standard). Find the minimum bore diameter.

### Given Data
- Mass of load: $m = 25$ kg
- Supply pressure: $P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Safety factor: $SF = 1.3$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step 1 — Compute the Effective Load Force

The cylinder must overcome gravity plus the safety margin.

$$
F_{\text{load}} = m \cdot g \cdot SF
$$

$$
F_{\text{load}} = 25 \times 9.81 \times 1.3
$$

$$
F_{\text{load}} = 318.825 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Force-Pressure Equation

$$
F = P \times A
$$

Rearranging for required area:

$$
A = \dfrac{F_{\text{load}}}{P}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{318.825}{6 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
A = 5.31375 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Area to Bore Diameter

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}
$$

Solving for $D$:

$$
D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 5.31375 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi}}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{6.766 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
D = 0.02601 \text{ m} = 26.01 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Standard Bore Size Selection

Standard pneumatic bores (ISO 15552) come in: **8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100 mm**.

We select the **next higher standard size**: $D = 32$ mm.

$$
A_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.032)^2}{4} = 8.042 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Output Force

$$
F_{\text{output}} = P \times A_{\text{actual}} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 8.042 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F_{\text{output}} = 482.55 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\text{Actual SF} = \dfrac{482.55}{25 \times 9.81} = 1.967 \;\;\; (\text{acceptable} > 1.3)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A **32 mm bore double-acting cylinder at 6 bar** safely handles the 25 kg load. This is a KTU-classic design problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conveyor Throughput & Cycle Time

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A conveyorized pick-and-place system uses a double-acting cylinder with a 200 mm stroke, extending in 0.8 s, retracting in 0.6 s, with a 0.5 s dwell at each end. Calculate the **parts per hour throughput**.

### Given
- Stroke length: $L = 200$ mm = 0.2 m
- Extension time: $T_{\text{ext}} = 0.8$ s
- Retraction time: $T_{\text{ret}} = 0.6$ s
- Dwell (each end): $T_d = 0.5$ s

### Step 1 — Compute Total Cycle Time

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{ext}} + T_{\text{ret}} + 2T_d
$$

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 0.8 + 0.6 + 2(0.5)
$$

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 0.8 + 0.6 + 1.0 = 2.4 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Throughput (PPH)

$$
S_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{\text{cycle}}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{3600}{2.4}
$$

$$
S_{\text{throughput}} = 1500 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

### Step 3 — Velocity Verification (Optional)

$$
v_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{0.2}{0.8} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_{\text{ret}} = \dfrac{0.2}{0.6} \approx 0.333 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The system handles **1500 PPH**. If the requirement is 2000 PPH, the design must be revised (reduce dwell or increase flow rate).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motor Power for Belt Conveyor

**Problem Context (KTU Standard):** A horizontal belt conveyor must move a total mass of 200 kg at 1.2 m/s with a coefficient of friction of 0.25. System efficiency is 85%. Find the required motor power.

### Given
- Total mass: $m = 200$ kg
- Belt speed: $v = 1.2$ m/s
- Friction coefficient: $\mu = 0.25$
- Efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step 1 — Compute Conveyor Drive Force

Neglect acceleration (steady-state, $a = 0$):

$$
F_{\text{conveyor}} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g + m \cdot a
$$

$$
F_{\text{conveyor}} = 0.25 \times 200 \times 9.81 + 0
$$

$$
F_{\text{conveyor}} = 490.5 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Useful Mechanical Power

$$
P_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{conveyor}} \cdot v
$$

$$
P_{\text{out}} = 490.5 \times 1.2 = 588.6 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Motor Input Power (with efficiency)

$$
P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta}
$$

$$
P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{588.6}{0.85}
$$

$$
P_{\text{motor}} = 692.47 \text{ W} \approx 0.7 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Standard Motor Selection

Standard motor ratings: 0.37, 0.55, **0.75**, 1.1, 1.5 kW. We select **0.75 kW** (≈ 1 HP) motor with overload protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A **0.75 kW AC induction motor** with a gear reducer (1:10 ratio typical) is the KTU-recommended selection.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Cylinder Type Selection

| Selection Criterion | Single-Acting | Double-Acting |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of force | One direction (spring return) | Both directions |
| Holding force in return | Low (spring only) | High (pneumatic) |
| Cycle speed | Slower (spring delay) | Faster |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (2 ports) |
| KTU Application | Simple push, clamping | Pick & place, sorting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Hands-on models in the lab almost always use **double-acting cylinders** because they demonstrate both extension and retraction control — a key learning outcome.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Automated Material Handling Cell

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    A1[Start Push Button PB1] --> B1[PLC CPU - Siemens S7-1200]
    A2[Stop Push Button PB2 NC] --> B1
    A3[Emergency Stop E-Stop] --> B1
    A4[Inductive Proximity Sensor S1] --> B1
    A5[Photoelectric Sensor S2] --> B1
    A6[Limit Switch LS1 Cylinder Extended] --> B1
    A7[Limit Switch LS2 Cylinder Retracted] --> B1
    A8[Conveyor Encoder] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Output Module]
    C1 --> D1[Contactor K1 Conveyor Motor]
    C1 --> D2[Solenoid Valve Y1 5 by 2 Cylinder Extend]
    C1 --> D3[Solenoid Valve Y2 5 by 2 Cylinder Retract]
    C1 --> D4[Indicator Lamp Green Running]
    C1 --> D5[Indicator Lamp Red Fault]
    C1 --> D6[Buzzer Alarm]
    D1 --> E1[AC Induction Motor 0.75 kW]
    E1 --> E2[Belt Conveyor Unit]
    D2 --> F1[Double Acting Cylinder 32 mm Bore]
    D3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Pick and Place Gripper]
    B1 --> G1[HMI Touch Panel 7 inch]
    G1 --> G2[Operator Interface]
    B1 --> H1[SCADA Server OPC UA]
    H1 --> H2[Plant Level Monitoring]

---

## 4.2 Pneumatic Circuit Schematic (Double-Acting Cylinder with 5/2 Solenoid)

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    A[Compressed Air Supply 6 bar] --> B[FRL Unit Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    B --> C[Manual Shut-off Valve]
    C --> D[5 by 2 Way DCV Double Solenoid]
    D -- Position A Extend --> E[Pneumatic Cylinder Bore 32 mm]
    D -- Position B Retract --> E
    E --> F[Flow Control Valve Meter-out]
    F --> G[Exhaust Silencer]
    D --> G
    LS1[Limit Switch Extended] -.-> H[PLC Input]
    LS2[Limit Switch Retracted] -.-> H

---

## 4.3 PLC Ladder Logic Flow (Conveyor + Pick & Place Sequence)

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    Start([Start PB]) --> S1[Set M0 Run Coil]
    S2[Stop PB] --> R0[Reset M0]
    S1 --> C1{Conveyor Sensor Detected?}
    C1 -- No --> M1[Motor ON Conveyor]
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Delay 2 sec Stabilize]
    D1 --> C2{Cylinder Retracted?}
    C2 -- Yes --> V1[Energize Y1 Extend]
    C2 -- No --> W1[Wait 0.5 sec]
    W1 --> C2
    V1 --> D2[Delay 1 sec Stroke]
    D2 --> V2[Energize Y2 Retract]
    V2 --> C3{End of Production?}
    C3 -- No --> C1
    C3 -- Yes --> R0
    M1 --> C1

---

## 4.4 Hands-on Model: Conveyorized Color-Based Sorting System

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    A[Loading Station Hopper] --> B[Belt Conveyor 1 DC Geared Motor]
    B --> C[Color Sensor TCS3200]
    C -- RED --> D1[Pneumatic Diverter 1 to Bin A]
    C -- GREEN --> D2[Pneumatic Diverter 2 to Bin B]
    C -- BLUE --> D3[Pneumatic Diverter 3 to Bin C]
    C -- UNKNOWN --> E[Reject Bin]
    D1 --> F1[Collection Bin A]
    D2 --> F2[Collection Bin B]
    D3 --> F3[Collection Bin C]
    B --> G[Encoder for Position Tracking]
    G --> H[Arduino Uno Microcontroller]
    H --> I[Relay Module 4 Channel]
    I --> J1[Conveyor Motor Driver]
    I --> J2[Solenoid Valve 1]
    I --> J3[Solenoid Valve 2]
    I --> J4[Solenoid Valve 3]

## 4.5 State Transition Diagram — Pick & Place Sequence (GRAFCET Style)

<!-- mermait -->
flowchart TD
    S0[State 0: Initial Cylinder Retracted Conveyor OFF] -->|Start PB| S1
    S1[State 1: Conveyor ON] -->|Sensor S1 Detects Object| S2
    S2[State 2: Conveyor Stops Timer 2s] -->|Timer Done| S3
    S3[State 3: Cylinder Extend Solenoid Y1 ON] -->|LS1 Extended| S4
    S4[State 4: Gripper Close Timer 1s] -->|Timer Done| S5
    S5[State 5: Cylinder Retract Solenoid Y2 ON] -->|LS2 Retracted| S6
    S6[State 6: Conveyor Resume] -->|Sensor S1 or Cycle End| S1
    S6 -->|Stop PB Pressed| S0
    S1 -->|Emergency Stop| S7
    S2 -->|Emergency Stop| S7
    S3 -->|Emergency Stop| S7
    S4 -->|Emergency Stop| S7
    S5 -->|Emergency Stop| S7
    S7[State 7: Fault All Outputs OFF] -->|Reset| S0
