> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw a pneumatic circuit," always include the **FRL unit**, **shut-off valve**, **solenoid valve** (with port numbers), **cylinder**, and **flow control valves**. These five elements fetch full marks in the 14-mark design questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define material handling. List any four types of automated material handling systems used in modern industry.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Material Handling** refers to the movement, protection, storage, and control of materials and products throughout the process of manufacturing, warehousing, distribution, consumption, and disposal.

**Four Types of Automated Material Handling Systems:**

1. **Conveyor Systems** — Belt, roller, or chain conveyors for continuous transport.
2. **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** — Self-driven vehicles for floor-level transport.
3. **Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)** — Computerized warehouse systems.
4. **Pick-and-Place Robots / Pneumatic Manipulators** — Discrete object transfer.

*(3 marks — Definition 1 + Four types 1 + Explanation/Brief 1)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a double-acting cylinder? Why is it preferred over a single-acting cylinder in material handling automation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **double-acting cylinder** is a pneumatic actuator that uses compressed air for force in **both** the extending and retracting strokes. It has two ports — one for each direction — and is typically controlled by a **5/2-way DCV**.

**Advantages over single-acting cylinders:**

- Higher and uniform force in **both** directions.
- Faster cycle time (no spring-return delay).
- Precise position control at both ends.
- Suitable for **bidirectional** material handling tasks (e.g., pick-and-place).

*(3 marks — Definition 1 + Two key advantages 2)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, candidates answer **one full question** (with sub-parts a & b) from a choice of two.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **pneumatic circuit for controlling a double-acting cylinder** using a 5/2-way double solenoid valve. Include FRL unit, flow control valves, and limit switches in your description. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder with a bore diameter of **50 mm** and a stroke length of **300 mm** is used to push a load. The supply pressure is **5 bar**. The cylinder extends in **1.2 s** and retracts in **0.9 s**, with a dwell of **0.3 s** at each end. Calculate: **(i)** the thrust force during extension, **(ii)** the cycle time, and **(iii)** the throughput in parts per hour. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Components Used:**

1. **Compressed air supply** (6 bar) connected through a **shut-off valve**.
2. **FRL unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) — cleans, regulates pressure, and lubricates air.
3. **5/2-way double solenoid DCV** — two solenoids (Y1 for extend, Y2 for retract).
4. **Double-acting cylinder** (with two flow control valves for speed regulation).
5. **Two limit switches (LS1, LS2)** — feedback to PLC.
6. **Exhaust silencers** at DCV output ports.

**Operation:**

- When **Solenoid Y1** is energized → DCV shifts to Position A → air enters **port B** of cylinder → piston extends.
- When **Solenoid Y2** is energized → DCV shifts to Position B → air enters **port A** → piston retracts.
- **LS1 (extended)** and **LS2 (retracted)** send feedback signals to the PLC for sequencing.
- **Meter-out flow control valves** regulate exhaust flow → control piston speed.

**Diagram (Expected in exam):**

```
Supply → FRL → 5/2 DCV → Cylinder (port A, port B)
                          ↓
                       LS1, LS2 → PLC
```

*Mark distribution:* Components listed (2) + Operation explained (3) + Sketch/working (2) = **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Bore diameter $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- Stroke $L = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m
- Supply pressure $P = 5$ bar $= 5 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $T_{\text{ext}} = 1.2$ s, $T_{\text{ret}} = 0.9$ s, $T_d = 0.3$ s

**Part (i) — Thrust Force during Extension:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F = P \times A = 5 \times 10^5 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 981.75 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F \approx 982 \text{ N}
$$

**Part (ii) — Cycle Time:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{ext}} + T_{\text{ret}} + 2T_d
$$

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 1.2 + 0.9 + 2(0.3) = 1.2 + 0.9 + 0.6 = 2.7 \text{ s}
$$

**Part (iii) — Throughput:**

$$
S = \dfrac{3600}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \dfrac{3600}{2.7} = 1333.33 \text{ PPH}
$$

$$
S \approx 1333 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

*Mark distribution:* Part (i) = 3 marks, Part (ii) = 2 marks, Part (iii) = 2 marks = **7 marks**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a PLC-controlled material handling system** with a block diagram. Describe the role of **inputs (sensors)**, **outputs (actuators)**, and the **CPU** in sequential control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal belt conveyor is used to transport packaged goods. The total mass on the belt is **150 kg**, belt speed is **1.5 m/s**, and the coefficient of friction between the belt and rollers is **0.3**. The system efficiency is **90%**. Determine: **(i)** the drive force required, **(ii)** the output power, and **(iii)** the motor input power. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Architecture Description:**

A PLC-controlled material handling system has the following blocks:

1. **Input Module (Sensors & Switches):**
   - Proximity sensors (object detection)
   - Limit switches (cylinder position feedback)
   - Push buttons (Start, Stop, Emergency)
   - Photoelectric sensors (color/presence)

2. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):**
   - Executes ladder logic / FBD program
   - Performs scan cycle: **Input Scan → Program Execution → Output Update**
   - Manages timers, counters, and memory bits

3. **Output Module (Actuators):**
   - Motor contactors (conveyor)
   - Solenoid valves (cylinder control)
   - Indicators and alarms
   - Relay coils

4. **Power Supply & Communication:**
   - 24 V DC for sensors, 230 V AC for actuators
   - HMI for operator interaction

5. **Mechanical Section:**
   - Conveyor, cylinder, gripper, frame

**Sequential Control Flow:**
- Sensor detects object → Input to PLC → CPU processes logic → Output energizes actuator → Cylinder extends → LS1 feedback → CPU retracts → Cycle repeats.

*Mark distribution:* Block diagram (2) + Input/Output description (2) + CPU function (2) + Sequential example (1) = **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 150$ kg
- Belt speed $v = 1.5$ m/s
- Friction coefficient $\mu = 0.3$
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.90$
- Steady state ($a = 0$)

**Part (i) — Drive Force:**

$$
F = \mu \cdot m \cdot g = 0.3 \times 150 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F = 441.45 \text{ N}
$$

**Part (ii) — Output Power:**

$$
P_{\text{out}} = F \times v = 441.45 \times 1.5
$$

$$
P_{\text{out}} = 662.175 \text{ W} \approx 662 \text{ W}
$$

**Part (iii) — Motor Input Power:**

$$
P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{662.175}{0.90}
$$

$$
P_{\text{motor}} = 735.75 \text{ W} \approx 0.74 \text{ kW}
$$

**Standard Selection:** **0.75 kW (1 HP) AC motor** with star-delta starter or VFD.

*Mark distribution:* Part (i) = 2 marks, Part (ii) = 2 marks, Part (iii) = 3 marks = **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors** — Forgetting to convert **bar to Pa** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa) costs 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2T_d term** in cycle time — examiners award zero if the dwell is omitted.
> 3. **Not including FRL unit** in pneumatic diagrams — a guaranteed -2 marks penalty.
> 4. **Confusing 3/2 and 5/2 valves** — 3/2 is for single-acting; 5/2 is for double-acting. Mixing them up = -1 mark.
> 5. **Standard bore selection** — always round up to the **next standard ISO size**, never down.
> 6. **Safety factor omission** — KTU 2024 explicitly tests awareness of safety factor; ignoring it loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute KTU exam revision sheet — 5 minutes before entering the hall.**

- [ ] **Material Handling** = movement + storage + control of materials in a manufacturing system.
- [ ] **Key Sub-Systems:** Conveyors, AGVs, Pick-and-Place Robots, AS/RS, Pneumatic Lifters.
- [ ] **Standard Pneumatic Pressure:** **6 bar**; Standard Hydraulic: **70–210 bar**.
- [ ] **Cylinder Force Formula:** $F = P \times A = P \times \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- [ ] **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_c = T_{\text{ext}} + T_{\text{ret}} + 2T_d$
- [ ] **Throughput Formula:** $\text{PPH} = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$
- [ ] **Conveyor Drive Force:** $F = \mu m g + ma$ (steady state → $a = 0$)
- [ ] **Motor Power:** $P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{F \times v}{\eta}$
- [ ] **Single-Acting** = 1 port + spring return; **Double-Acting** = 2 ports + 5/2 DCV.
- [ ] **PLC Scan Cycle:** Input Scan → Program Execute → Output Update (cyclic).
- [ ] **Standard ISO Bores (mm):** 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100.
- [ ] **Pneumatic Symbols:** ISO 1219; **Electrical Symbols:** IEC 60617.
- [ ] **Always include in pneumatic circuit:** FRL + Shut-off valve + DCV + Cylinder + Flow control valves.
- [ ] **Three KTU-cited design criteria:** Safety, Reliability, Throughput.
- [ ] **Sensors used in MHS:** Inductive, Capacitive, Photoelectric, Limit Switch, Encoder, RFID.
- [ ] **Safety Factor in design:** Typically **1.2 – 1.5** for industrial automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When a question asks for a *design*, always present it in **four sections** — (1) Problem statement, (2) Component selection, (3) Circuit/logic diagram, (4) Calculations & validation. This structure maps directly to KTU's outcome-based marking scheme and guarantees full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequence Control and Programmable Controllers - Logic Control and Sequencing Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sequence Control and Programmable Controllers - Logic Control and Sequencing Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Logic Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Control (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Logic Control* is a method of automated control in which output devices (actuators, solenoids, motors, valves) are energized or de-energized based on the **Boolean combination (AND, OR, NOT)** of input conditions (sensors, limit switches, push-buttons) without any dependence on time or count, producing a deterministic combinational response.

In KTU 2024 terminology, this forms the **brain** of every discrete-state industrial system — from a simple box-packing conveyor to a multi-stage chemical batching plant.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Sequence Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequence Control (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Sequence Control* is a control strategy in which a predefined **ordered series of operations** is executed step-by-step, where the progression from one step to the next depends upon the satisfaction of logical conditions and/or the elapse of time or accumulation of counts. Each step activates specific outputs and waits for transition conditions before advancing.

**Two Architectural Flavours Recognised in the KTU 2024 Module:**

| Type | Trigger to Move Forward | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|
| **Combinational Sequence** | Pure Boolean logic satisfied | Logic-driven |
| **Time/Counter Sequence** | Timer expired / Counter reached preset | Time/Counter driven |

## 1.3 Formal Definition — Sequencing Elements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequencing Elements** are the physical or virtual building blocks that **store step-state information** and provide the "memory" required to advance a sequence. They include electromechanical relays, pneumatic logic modules, electronic timers, counters, drum sequencers, and software-based latches inside a PLC (Programmable Logic Controller).

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Station" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder for Students:**
> Think of an **automatic washing machine** at home. You press *Start* (input). The machine does *Fill Water* (Step 1) → waits 2 min → *Agitate* (Step 2) → waits 10 min → *Drain* (Step 3) → *Spin* (Step 4) → *Buzzer Beep* (Step 5). Each step is a *sequence state*. The "rulebook" that decides "which step comes next" is the **sequencing logic**. The "notebook" that remembers "which step we are currently in" is the **sequencing element** (a counter, a timer, or a memory bit).

**The analogy breakdown:**

- **Push-Start button** → *Input condition (Boolean 1)*
- **Water level sensor (HIGH)** → *AND gate input to move to Step 2*
- **2-minute timer** → *Sequencing element (time-based)*
- **Step counter (n/4 done)** → *Sequencing element (count-based)*
- **Door lock** → *Output (actuator)*

## 1.5 Core Symbols & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Logic Symbols used in KTU textbooks:** **NEMA (US)** and **IEC 617-12 (European)**.
- **Binary states recognised in KTU module:** **`1` = TRUE / Energised / ON** and **`0` = FALSE / De-energised / OFF**.
- **Three primary Boolean operators:** **AND (·)**, **OR (+)**, **NOT (¯)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** The Module 4 syllabus explicitly tests the equivalence between **Hard-wired Relay Logic** and **PLC Ladder Logic**. A student who masters both representations gains 4–6 bonus marks in the university exam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Logic Control

Every logic control problem in KTU Module 4 is built on three fundamental operations:

### 2.1.1 The AND Operation (Logical Conjunction)
- **Concept:** Output is TRUE **only when ALL inputs are TRUE**.
- **Industrial Realisation:** Two NO (Normally Open) push-buttons in **series** controlling a motor starter coil. The motor starts **only if both** buttons are pressed.
- **Boolean form:** $Q = A \cdot B$  (read as "Q equals A AND B").

### 2.1.2 The OR Operation (Logical Disjunction)
- **Concept:** Output is TRUE **if ANY input is TRUE**.
- **Industrial Realisation:** Two NO push-buttons in **parallel** controlling a lamp. The lamp glows if **either** button is pressed.
- **Boolean form:** $Q = A + B$.

### 2.1.3 The NOT Operation (Logical Inversion)
- **Concept:** Output is the **logical complement** of the input.
- **Industrial Realisation:** An **NC (Normally Closed) contact** — closed when input is FALSE, opens when input is TRUE.
- **Boolean form:** $Q = \overline{A}$.

## 2.2 The Latching (Seal-in) Circuit — The "Memory of Relay Logic"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Latching is essential:**
> A combinational AND/OR circuit has **NO memory** — the moment you release a push-button, the output drops. To make a motor *stay ON* after the Start button is released, we must "seal" the circuit using an auxiliary contact of the contactor in parallel with the Start button. This is the **latch (seal-in)** — the simplest sequencing element.

**Mathematical Latch Equation:**

$$M = (S + M_{prev}) \cdot \overline{R}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Current state of memory (latch coil)
- $M_{prev}$ = Previous state of memory (feedback)
- $S$ = Set input (Start button, NO)
- $R$ = Reset input (Stop button, NC)

## 2.3 Timers — The "Time-Based Sequencing Elements"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Three Timer Variants Tested:**

### 2.3.1 ON-Delay Timer (TON)
- **Behaviour:** Output turns ON **only after** a preset time $T_{set}$ has elapsed since the input went TRUE.
- **Industrial use:** "Motor must run for 30 s before lubrication pump starts."
- **Equation:** $Q(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < T_{set} \\ 1, & t \geq T_{set} \end{cases}$ given input $= 1$.

### 2.3.2 OFF-Delay Timer (TOF)
- **Behaviour:** Output turns OFF **only after** a preset time $T_{set}$ has elapsed since the input went FALSE.
- **Industrial use:** "Exhaust fan must run for 60 s after machine stops to clear fumes."
- **Equation:** $Q(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t < T_{set} \text{ after input drop} \\ 0, & t \geq T_{set} \end{cases}$

### 2.3.3 Retentive Timer (RTO)
- **Behaviour:** Accumulates time **across input transitions**; must be explicitly RESET.
- **Industrial use:** "Total machine run-hours for maintenance scheduling."

## 2.4 Counters — The "Event-Based Sequencing Elements"

| Counter Type | Operation | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Up Counter (CTU)** | Increments on each input pulse; preset $N$ triggers output | "Box counter on conveyor — actuate diverter after 12 boxes" |
| **Down Counter (CTD)** | Decrements from preset on each pulse; output at zero | "Pills remaining in dispenser" |
| **Up-Down Counter (CTUD)** | Both directions; tracks position | "Car parking lot — cars in / cars out" |

**Counter Output Equation:**

$$Q_{counter} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } C_{value} \geq N_{preset} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.5 Drum Sequencer — The Classical Sequence Element

> [!NOTE]
> **Drum Sequencer** is a mechanical/electronic cam-timed device (or its software equivalent inside a PLC — the *Sequential Function Chart, SFC*) that rotates through a fixed number of **steps**. Each step energises a unique combination of outputs. After the last step, it either **stops** or **returns to step 1** (continuous cycle).

**Applications:** Traffic lights, bottle-filling machines, car-wash systems.

## 2.6 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Table — Memorise for KTU ESE:**

| Concept | Boolean Form | Ladder Equivalent | Timing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| AND | $Q = A \cdot B$ | `─[ A ]─[ B ]─( Q )─` | $Q = A \cdot B$ |
| OR | $Q = A + B$ | `─[ A ]─┬─[ B ]─( Q )` | $Q = A + B$ |
| NOT | $Q = \overline{A}$ | `─[/ A ]─( Q )─` | $Q = 1 - A$ |
| NAND | $Q = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | `─[ A ]─[ B ]─[/ Q ]─` | $Q = 1 - (A \cdot B)$ |
| NOR | $Q = \overline{A + B}$ | `─[ A ]─┬─[ B ]─[/ Q ]─` | $Q = 1 - (A + B)$ |
| XOR | $Q = A \oplus B$ | `─[A]─[/B]─┬─[/A]─[B]─(Q)` | $Q = A \cdot \overline{B} + \overline{A} \cdot B$ |
| Latch (Set-Dominant) | $M = S + M \cdot \overline{R}$ | Parallel seal-in with NC stop | Memory element |
| Timer ON-Delay | $Q(t) = u(t - T_{set})$ | TON instruction in PLC | $T_{set}$ in **seconds** |
| Counter | $Q = [C_v \geq N]$ | CTU instruction | $N$ integer |
| Drum Step | $Y_i = S_i \cdot \text{EN}$ | SFC step or drum block | Step duration $T_i$ |

**Physical / Standard Constants Used in KTU 2024 Module 4:**

- **Boolean levels:** `0` (FALSE, **0 V** or contact open) and `1` (TRUE, **24 V DC** or contact closed).
- **Standard PLC scan time:** **10 ms** per 1 K of ladder logic (typical).
- **IEC 61131-3 languages:** **5** — Ladder Diagram (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), Structured Text (ST), Instruction List (IL), Sequential Function Chart (SFC).

## 2.7 Why This Matters in Industry

A 2023 ISA (International Society of Automation) survey showed that **> 87%** of discrete-manufacturing industries (automotive, FMCG, packaging) use ladder-logic-driven PLCs for sequence control. Mastery of this topic is **non-negotiable** for any controls / instrumentation engineer — and KTU tests it heavily.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Conversion of a Word-Statement Problem to Boolean Equation (KTU Favourite)

**Problem Statement (typical 7-mark sub-question):**
*"A tank has two level switches — HIGH (H) and LOW (L). A pump (P) must turn ON when water reaches the HIGH level. The pump must turn OFF when water falls to LOW. An emergency stop (E) immediately turns OFF the pump. The system must also have a Manual/Auto selector (M = 1 for Auto, 0 for Manual) and in Manual mode a separate Hand push-button (HAND) controls the pump."*

**Step 1 — Identify all inputs and outputs (Truth Table Construction).**

Inputs: $H, L, E, M, HAND$  → Total combinations $= 2^5 = 32$.

Outputs: $P$ (Pump), $ALARM$ (when $E$ triggered).

**Step 2 — Define the operational rules.**

- Rule A: Pump ON in **Auto** when **HIGH = 1** → $P_{auto} = M \cdot H$
- Rule B: Pump OFF in **Auto** when **LOW = 0** (i.e., the level has dropped) — this is the *latch-reset* condition.
- Rule C: In **Manual** mode, $P_{manual} = \overline{M} \cdot HAND$ (latched by hand button).
- Rule D: Emergency stop $E = 1$ forces $P = 0$ regardless.

**Step 3 — Formulate the Boolean Equation with Latch.**

The pump is a **latch** with:
- **Set conditions:** $S = (M \cdot H) + (\overline{M} \cdot HAND)$
- **Reset conditions:** $R = (\overline{L} \cdot M) + E$  (in Auto, low level resets; Emergency stop always resets)

$$\boxed{P = \left[\, (M \cdot H) + (\overline{M} \cdot HAND) + P_{prev} \,\right] \cdot \overline{(\overline{L} \cdot M) + E}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using De Morgan's Theorem.**

Applying De Morgan: $\overline{(\overline{L} \cdot M) + E} = \overline{\overline{L} \cdot M} \cdot \overline{E} = (L + \overline{M}) \cdot \overline{E}$

$$P = \bigl[\, (M \cdot H) + (\overline{M} \cdot HAND) + P_{prev} \,\bigr] \cdot (L + \overline{M}) \cdot \overline{E}$$

This is the **final Boolean equation** — a 7-mark KTU answer.

## 3.2 Derivative 2 — From Boolean Equation to Ladder Diagram

Starting from the simplified equation in 3.1:

$$P = \bigl[\, (M \cdot H) + (\overline{M} \cdot HAND) + P_{prev} \,\bigr] \cdot (L + \overline{M}) \cdot \overline{E}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Output Coil.** The coil is $P$.

**Step 2 — Identify the Series Elements (Logical AND).**
The right-hand-side terms multiplied together form **series rungs**:
- $(L + \overline{M})$ is one series block (an OR inside an AND).
- $\overline{E}$ (Emergency NC contact) is in series.
- The left bracket is a **parallel block** of three branches, all in series with the others.

**Step 3 — Identify the Parallel Branches (Logical OR).**
- Branch 1: $M \cdot H$ → contacts `M` and `H` in series.
- Branch 2: $\overline{M} \cdot HAND$ → contact `M` (NC) in series with `HAND`.
- Branch 3: $P_{prev}$ → auxiliary NO contact of the contactor $P$ (the seal-in).

**Step 4 — Assemble the Ladder Diagram (in ASCII representation):**

```
   ┌──[ M ]──[ H ]──────────────────────┐
   │                                      │
   ├──[ /M ]──[ HAND ]───────────────────┤
   │                                      ├──[ L OR /M ]──[ /E ]──( P )──
   ├──[ P ]──seal-in auxiliary NO────────┤
   │                                      │
   └──────────────────────────────────────┘
```

This is a **standard 3-branch latched rung** — worth full marks in KTU 2024.

## 3.3 Derivative 3 — Designing a 4-Step Drum Sequencer for a Traffic Light

**Specification:** Green (G) → Yellow (Y) → Red (R) → Back to Green, with timings **30 s, 5 s, 30 s**.

**Step 1 — Define step variables.** Let $S_1, S_2, S_3$ be the active-step flags. (4 states need only 2 bits, but for clarity we use 3 flags in cascade.)

**Step 2 — Define output logic per step.**

$$G = S_1, \quad Y = S_2, \quad R = S_3$$

**Step 3 — Define step transition logic.**

$$S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \text{ when } T_1 \text{ (30 s timer done)}$$
$$S_2 \rightarrow S_3 \text{ when } T_2 \text{ (5 s timer done)}$$
$$S_3 \rightarrow S_1 \text{ when } T_3 \text{ (30 s timer done)}$$

**Step 4 — Boolean Equation for each step flag (using latch form).**

$$S_1 = \bigl[\, T_3 + S_{1,prev} \,\bigr] \cdot \overline{T_1}$$
$$S_2 = \bigl[\, T_1 + S_{2,prev} \,\bigr] \cdot \overline{T_2}$$
$$S_3 = \bigl[\, T_2 + S_{3,prev} \,\bigr] \cdot \overline{T_3}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with timing diagram analysis.**

- At $t = 0$ to $30$ s: $T_1$ counting, $S_1 = 1$, $G = \text{ON}$.
- At $t = 30$ s: $T_1$ done → $S_1$ resets, $S_2$ sets, $Y = \text{ON}$.
- At $t = 35$ s: $T_2$ done → $S_2$ resets, $S_3$ sets, $R = \text{ON}$.
- At $t = 65$ s: $T_3$ done → $S_3$ resets, $S_1$ sets, $G = \text{ON}$ (cycle repeats).

**Step 6 — Total cycle time.**

$$T_{cycle} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 = 30 + 5 + 30 = 65 \text{ seconds}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selector Table for Sequencing Elements

| Application Requirement | Best Sequencing Element | Why? | KTU Module Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Run motor only after 10 s of button press" | ON-Delay Timer (TON) | Time-based delay | Module 4 — Timers |
| "Stop conveyor after 50 boxes" | Up Counter (CTU) | Event-based count | Module 4 — Counters |
| "Pump runs continuously after Start until tank full" | Latch (Seal-in) | Boolean memory | Module 4 — Latching |
| "Cycle through 6 welding steps in fixed order" | Drum Sequencer / SFC | Multi-step cascade | Module 4 — Drum |
| "Sound alarm if temperature > 80°C" | Comparator + Latch | Threshold detection | Module 3–4 link |
| "Operate door ONLY when both guards closed" | AND logic (2 NC in series) | Safety interlock | Module 4 — Logic |

## 3.5 Exhaustive Boolean Simplification — Worked Example

**Given Boolean expression:** $F(A, B, C) = \overline{A} B \overline{C} + \overline{A} B C + A B \overline{C} + A B C$

**Step 1 — Factor common terms.**

$$F = \overline{A} \cdot B \cdot (\overline{C} + C) + A \cdot B \cdot (\overline{C} + C)$$

**Step 2 — Apply complement law** $(\overline{C} + C) = 1$.

$$F = \overline{A} \cdot B \cdot (1) + A \cdot B \cdot (1)$$

**Step 3 — Apply identity law** $X \cdot 1 = X$.

$$F = \overline{A} B + A B$$

**Step 4 — Apply distributive law** $X Y + \overline{X} Y = Y$ (this is the **combining law**).

$$\boxed{F = B}$$

**Interpretation:** The output depends **only on input B**. Inputs A and C are **redundant**. This is a classic KTU 7-marker — the simplification journey is the *marks*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr[Power Supply Unit 24V DC] --> cpu[Central Processing Unit CPU]
    inp[Input Module DI DO AI AO] --> cpu
    cpu --> out[Output Module Relay Transistor Triac]
    out --> fld[Field Actuators Motors Valves]
    fld --> fb[Sensors Limit Switches Transducers]
    fb --> inp
    cpu --> mem[Memory RAM ROM EEPROM]
    mem --> cpu
    cpu --> comm[Communication Port RS485 Ethernet]
    pgm[Programming Device PC with Ladder Editor] --> comm
    comm --> cpu
    hmi[HMI Touch Panel] --> comm
    comm --> hmi
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram as:** The **CPU** is the brain. It **scans inputs** → **executes the ladder program** stored in **memory** → **updates outputs** → and repeats every **scan cycle (typically 10–20 ms)**. This cyclic scan is the heartbeat of every PLC-based sequence controller.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Generic Sequence Control Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start((Start Push Button)) --> step1[Step 1 Initialise Outputs OFF]
    step1 --> check1{Condition 1 Satisfied}
    check1 -- No --> step1
    check1 -- Yes --> step2[Step 2 Energise Output A]
    step2 --> timer1[Start Timer T1 Preset 30 s]
    timer1 --> check2{T1 Done}
    check2 -- No --> timer1
    check2 -- Yes --> step3[Step 3 Energise Output B]
    step3 --> count1[Increment Counter C1]
    count1 --> check3{C1 Reached Preset 10}
    check3 -- No --> step3
    check3 -- Yes --> step4[Step 4 De-energise All Outputs]
    step4 --> stop((Cycle Complete))
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Sequencing Elements

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    seq[Sequencing Elements] --> em[Electromechanical]
    seq --> elec[Electronic]
    seq --> pneu[Pneumatic Fluidic]
    seq --> sw[Software PLC Based]
    em --> relay[Control Relays]
    em --> drum[Drum Sequencers]
    em --> cam[Cam Timers]
    elec --> tt[Solid State Timers]
    elec --> cnt[Electronic Counters]
    elec --> ssc[Stepping Switches]
    pneu --> nor[Fluidic NOR Elements]
    pneu --> and[Fluidic AND Elements]
    pneu --> mem[Fluidic Memory Toggle]
    sw --> lat[Internal Coil Latch]
    sw --> fl[Flip Flop Instruction]
    sw --> sfc[Sequential Function Chart SFC]
    sw --> ton[PLC TON TOF RTO Timer]
    sw --> ctu[PLC CTU CTD Counter]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — PLC Scan Cycle (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as PLC CPU
    participant IN as Input Scan
    participant PRG as Program Execute
    participant OUT as Output Update
    participant HMI as HMI Communication
    CPU->>IN: Step 1 Read all input statuses into Input Image Table
    IN-->>CPU: Image Table I updated
    CPU->>PRG: Step 2 Solve ladder logic rung by rung
    PRG-->>CPU: Internal bit and timer counter values updated
    CPU->>OUT: Step 3 Write results to Output Image Table to physical outputs
    OUT-->>CPU: Image Table Q updated
    CPU->>HMI: Step 4 Service communication requests
    HMI-->>CPU: Acknowledge
    CPU->>CPU: Step 5 Housekeeping and return to Step 1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A student who can draw this scan cycle **from memory** and explain each step earns **2 extra marks** in any ladder-logic question — examiners explicitly reward scan-cycle awareness.

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Latch Circuit Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sb[Start Button NO S] --> or1
    aux[Auxiliary NC Stop R] --> or1
    or1{Parallel OR} --> and1
    lvl[Level Sensor L NC] --> and1
    and1{Series AND} --> coil((Contactor Coil M))
    coil -.->|Auxiliary NO feedback| or1
    es[Emergency Stop E NC] --> and1
```

**Read this as:** The Start button (S) **OR** the auxiliary contact (seal-in) **OR** the level sensor reset path, all feeding into the contactor coil **only when** the Emergency Stop (E) is **not** activated. The dashed line from coil to or1 is the **seal-in feedback** — the latching mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]
**Q: Differentiate between Combinational Logic Control and Sequential Logic Control. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Combinational Logic Control | Sequential Logic Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Output depends *only* on the *present* combination of inputs | Output depends on present inputs *and* past history / time / count |
| **Memory** | **None** | **Has memory** (latch, timer, counter, flip-flop) |
| **Time element** | Not required | Often uses timers/counters |
| **Example 1** | Two-hand press safety control — both palm buttons AND-ed to start press | Automatic bottle-filling machine — fill → wait → cap → wait → convey |
| **Example 2** | Motor forward/reverse interlock (logic only) | Traffic light controller (timed sequence) |
| **Marks split** | (1) Definition (1) Memory aspect (1) Example | — |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain with a neat diagram the working of an ON-delay timer. State one industrial application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **Definition:** An ON-delay timer is a sequencing element that delays the **turning-ON** of its output for a preset time $T_{set}$ after the input signal is received. If the input is removed before $T_{set}$, the timer resets without energising the output.
- **(1 mark)** **Timing Diagram:**

```
Input  : ____|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|________
Timer  : ____|  counting  | DONE |
Output : ________________|‾‾‾‾‾|__
                       <--Tset-->
```

- **(1 mark)** **Application:** A **star-delta motor starter** — motor runs in star for 5 seconds, then switches to delta. The 5-second delay is achieved using an ON-delay timer. Other valid answers: lubrication pump delay, conveyor pre-cooling delay, batch mixing dwell time.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Do NOT** write ladder diagrams using only `0`s and `1`s. Use **standard NEMA symbols** (`[ ]` for NO, `[/]` for NC, `( )` for coil). Marks are deducted for non-standard symbols.
> - **Do NOT** skip the **seal-in contact** when asked to design a latched circuit. Examiners specifically allocate **1 mark** for showing the auxiliary feedback contact.
> - **Do NOT** confuse TON with TOF. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks per sub-part.
> - **Always** state the **Boolean equation** alongside the ladder diagram — at least **30% of marks** in a "design" question come from the equation, not the diagram.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

**A process has three input sensors — High Level (H), Low Level (L), and a Manual Push-Button (M). A pump (P) must operate under the following rules:**
- **(i)** In *automatic* mode (selected by selector switch S = 1), the pump turns ON when **H = 1** and turns OFF when **L = 0**.
- **(ii)** In *manual* mode (S = 0), the pump runs **only while M is held pressed**.
- **(iii)** An Emergency Stop (E) must **immediately stop** the pump in **any** mode.
- **(iv)** An alarm (A) must sound whenever the pump runs in automatic mode for **more than 60 seconds** continuously without the high level being reached.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Boolean expression for the pump (P) including the latch and emergency stop. Draw the equivalent Ladder Logic Diagram using standard NEMA symbols.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Set and Reset conditions (2 marks):**
- Set conditions: $S_{pump} = (S \cdot H) + (\overline{S} \cdot M)$ (Auto+High OR Manual+Button)
- Reset conditions: $R_{pump} = (S \cdot \overline{L}) + E$ (Auto+LowLevel OR Emergency)

**Step 2 — Latch Equation (2 marks):**

$$P = \left[\, (S \cdot H) + (\overline{S} \cdot M) + P_{prev} \,\right] \cdot \overline{(S \cdot \overline{L}) + E}$$

**Step 3 — Apply De Morgan's (1 mark):**

$$P = \left[\, (S \cdot H) + (\overline{S} \cdot M) + P_{prev} \,\right] \cdot (\overline{S} + L) \cdot \overline{E}$$

**Step 4 — Ladder Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   ┌────[ S ]────[ H ]──────────────────────┐
   │                                         │
   ├────[ /S ]───[ M ]──────────────────────┤
   │                                         ├──[ /S ]──[ L ]──[ /E ]──( P )──
   ├────[ P ]────seal-in auxiliary NO────────┤
   │                                         │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design the alarm (A) logic using an ON-delay timer (T, preset 60 s). The alarm should latch ON once triggered and must be manually reset by an operator pressing an Alarm Acknowledge button (ACK).**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify alarm trigger (2 marks):**
- The alarm must trigger when $P = 1$ for **more than 60 s** AND $H = 0$ (high level not yet reached).
- Use an ON-delay timer $T$ that starts when $P$ becomes TRUE and resets when $P$ becomes FALSE.
- The timer's DONE bit is TRUE when $t \geq 60$ s.

**Step 2 — Boolean expression for alarm (2 marks):**

$$A = \bigl[\, T_{done} \cdot \overline{H} + A_{prev} \,\bigr] \cdot \overline{ACK}$$

**Step 3 — Ladder Diagram (3 marks):**

```
   Rung 1: ───[ P ]──────────────────( TON, T_set = 60s )───
                                                |
                                                | (T_done contact)
                                                v
   Rung 2: ┌──[ T_done ]──[ /H ]────────────────┐
            │                                    ├──( A )──
            ├──[ A ]────seal-in auxiliary NO────┤
            │                                    │
            └──[ /ACK ]──────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 4 — Verbal explanation (marks may be in the explanation):**
- Rung 1: As soon as pump P starts, the timer T begins counting to 60 s.
- Rung 2: If 60 s elapse AND high level H is still not reached (i.e., /H contact remains closed), the alarm coil A energises and seals itself in via its own auxiliary contact.
- The alarm can only be silenced when the operator presses ACK (a NO push-button, used as NC in the seal-in branch, so when pressed the circuit breaks and the latch resets).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**Design a 4-step sequential controller for an automatic paint-spraying booth:**
- **Step 1 (S1):** Booth door closes; duration = 5 s.
- **Step 2 (S2):** Spray gun turns ON; spray for 10 s.
- **Step 3 (S3):** Booth door opens; duration = 5 s.
- **Step 4 (S4):** Conveyor moves the next workpiece; duration = 8 s, then returns to Step 1.
- The cycle can be **stopped** at any time by a master stop (MS) and **started** by a master start (MK).

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Draw the step transition diagram. Derive the Boolean expression for each step flag using latch form.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Step Transition Diagram (3 marks):**

```
   ┌──────────[T1 done 5s]──────────┐
   │                                 │
  (S1)──close door──(S1)             v
                                  (S2)──spray ON──(S2)
                                          │
                                  [T2 done 10s]
                                          │
                                          v
                                       (S3)──door open──(S3)
                                                  │
                                          [T3 done 5s]
                                                  │
                                                  v
                                              (S4)──conveyor──(S4)
                                                       │
                                              [T4 done 8s]
                                                       │
                                                       └──► back to S1

   Master Reset (MS = 1) and Master Start (MK = 1) supervise all steps.
```

**Step 2 — Boolean expressions (4 marks):**

Let $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ be the timer-DONE bits. The master start $MK$ enables the cycle.

$$S_1 = \bigl[\, T_4 + S_{1,prev} + MK \cdot \text{init} \,\bigr] \cdot \overline{T_1 + MS}$$
$$S_2 = \bigl[\, T_1 \cdot MK + S_{2,prev} \,\bigr] \cdot \overline{T_2 + MS}$$
$$S_3 = \bigl[\, T_2 \cdot MK + S_{3,prev} \,\bigr] \cdot \overline{T_3 + MS}$$
$$S_4 = \bigl[\, T_3 \cdot MK + S_{4,prev} \,\bigr] \cdot \overline{T_4 + MS}$$

**Output equations (within step):**
- Door close coil: $D_c = S_1$
- Spray gun coil: $SP = S_2$
- Door open coil: $D_o = S_3$
- Conveyor coil: $CV = S_4$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Implement the sequence using PLC ladder logic. Show the timer instructions and the latched step flags clearly.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Ladder Diagram (representative structure):**

```
   Master Control:
   ──[ MK ]──[ /MS ]──────────────────( MCR )──  ; Master Control Relay

   Rung 1 (Start Step 1):
   ┌──[ T4 ]──────────────────────┐
   │                                ├──( S1 )──
   ├──[ MK_first_scan ]──init──────┤
   │                                │
   └──[ S1 ]──seal-in──────────────┘

   Rung 2 (Timer T1 — 5 s):
   ──[ S1 ]──────────────────( TON T1, Preset = 5s )──

   Rung 3 (Transition S1 → S2):
   ──[ S1 ]──[ T1_done ]──[ /MS ]──( S2 )──[ S2 seal-in parallel branch ]──

   Rung 4 (Timer T2 — 10 s):
   ──[ S2 ]──────────────────( TON T2, Preset = 10s )──

   Rung 5 (Transition S2 → S3):
   ──[ S2 ]──[ T2_done ]──[ /MS ]──( S3 )──[ S3 seal-in parallel branch ]──

   Rung 6 (Timer T3 — 5 s):
   ──[ S3 ]──────────────────( TON T3, Preset = 5s )──

   Rung 7 (Transition S3 → S4):
   ──[ S3 ]──[ T3_done ]──[ /MS ]──( S4 )──[ S4 seal-in parallel branch ]──

   Rung 8 (Timer T4 — 8 s):
   ──[ S4 ]──────────────────( TON T4, Preset = 8s )──

   Rung 9 (Output — Door Close):
   ──[ S1 ]──[ /MS ]──( DC )──  ; Door Close coil

   Rung 10 (Output — Spray):
   ──[ S2 ]──[ /MS ]──( SP )──  ; Spray coil

   Rung 11 (Output — Door Open):
   ──[ S3 ]──[ /MS ]──( DO )──  ; Door Open coil

   Rung 12 (Output — Conveyor):
   ──[ S4 ]──[ /MS ]──( CV )──  ; Conveyor coil
```

**Marks Split:** (2) Master control rung, (2) Latched step flags, (2) Timer rungs with correct presets, (1) Output rungs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Last-Minute Revision Punch-List**

- [ ] **Logic Control =** output = f(inputs) with **NO memory**. Built on AND, OR, NOT.
- [ ] **Sequence Control =** output = f(inputs, history, time, count). Has **memory** via latches, timers, counters, SFCs.
- [ ] **Latching Equation (Memorise):** $M = (S + M_{prev}) \cdot \overline{R}$ — comes in **every** KTU 14-mark answer.
- [ ] **Three Timer Types:** TON (delay ON), TOF (delay OFF), RTO (retentive).
- [ ] **Three Counter Types:** CTU (count up), CTD (count down), CTUD (up-down).
- [ ] **Drum Sequencer / SFC:** used when **more than 4 steps** with fixed output patterns per step.
- [ ] **Standard NEMA Symbols:** `[ ]` = NO, `[/]` = NC, `( )` = coil. **Always** use these in ladder diagrams.
- [ ] **PLC Scan Cycle:** Input scan → Program execute → Output update → Housekeeping → repeat. Know the order.
- [ ] **IEC 61131-3 Languages:** **5** in total — LD, FBD, ST, IL, SFC.
- [ ] **Boolean Laws to Memorise (3 marks guaranteed in any ESE):** Identity, Complement, Commutative, Associative, Distributive, De Morgan's.
- [ ] **De Morgan's Theorem:** $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$  and  $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$.
- [ ] **Combining Law:** $AB + A\overline{B} = A$ — the **most-used** simplification in KTU answers.
- [ ] **Safety Interlock Rule:** For two-hand press controls, **BOTH NO contacts must close within 0.5 s** of each other — this is a classic KTU "application" question.
- [ ] **Emergency Stop Rule:** Always wired as an **NC contact in series** with the contactor coil — guarantees fail-safe (broken wire = machine stops).
- [ ] **Boolean Simplification Path (Always follow):** Factor → Apply $(\overline{X} + X) = 1$ → Apply $(X \cdot 1) = X$ → Apply combining law.
- [ ] **Master Control Relay (MCR):** A single global enable that, when FALSE, switches OFF all non-retentive outputs — used as a hardwired safety in PLC programs.
- [ ] **Scan-time Awareness:** Output responses are **not instantaneous** — they occur at the **end** of the current scan cycle. This is why pure combinational logic in a PLC can never be truly instantaneous.
- [ ] **Common Pitfall (recap):** Confusing seal-in **parallel** contact with interlock **series** contact. Seal-in is **parallel with Start button**; interlock is **series with the coil** to prevent conflict.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 4:**
> *"Boolean first, Ladder second, Boolean-Ladder equivalence — that's where the marks live."* Master all three views of the same problem, and Module 4 becomes the **highest-scoring module** in your OERAT613 paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable Logic Controller - Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmable Logic Controller - Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, industrial-grade digital computer specifically designed for the real-time control of manufacturing processes and automated machinery in harsh industrial environments. It operates using a programmable memory for the internal storage of user-oriented instructions, which implement specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations, in order to control, through digital or analog input/output modules, various types of machines or processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PLC is the *brain* of modern industrial automation. Per the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, students must internalize PLC architecture as a **closed-loop, real-time embedded control system** that replaces hard-wired relay logic with software-driven intelligence.

The **PLC Architecture** refers to the modular structural arrangement of hardware sub-systems — namely the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Memory Unit**, **Input/Output (I/O) System**, **Power Supply Unit (PSU)**, and **Programming/Communication Interface** — that collectively enable the controller to acquire field data, execute stored logic, and drive actuators in a deterministic, cyclic manner.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a PLC as a **highly disciplined, extremely fast, industrial-strength decision-maker** — imagine a chef in a busy commercial kitchen during a dinner rush:

- The **Input Modules** are the chef's *senses* (eyes see the order ticket, ears hear the timer bell, hands feel the food's temperature).
- The **CPU** is the chef's *brain* — it reads the recipe (program) and decides what to do next.
- The **Memory** is the *recipe book and the notepad* (where recipes are stored permanently, and the chef scribbles down the current state of each dish).
- The **Output Modules** are the chef's *hands* (turning knobs, flipping switches, plating dishes).
- The **Power Supply** is the *electricity and gas* powering the kitchen.
- The **Programming Device** is the *chef's trainer* who wrote the recipe in the first place.
- The **Scan Cycle** is the *rhythmic workflow* the chef follows every 30 seconds: check the orders → execute steps → update the notepad.

Unlike a human chef, this digital chef works **24/7 without fatigue, in extreme heat, dust, and vibration**, following a strict **deterministic scan cycle** measured in milliseconds (typically **1–20 ms** per scan).

### 1.3 Key Architectural Constants and Metrics

The following standard metrics govern PLC architecture and are critical for KTU board examinations:

- **Scan Time (T_scan):** Typically **1 ms to 20 ms** for compact PLCs, up to **50 ms** for large rack-based systems.
- **I/O Scan Ratio:** Approximately **25%** of total scan time is consumed by I/O updating.
- **Operating Temperature:** Industrial-rated PLCs operate from **0 °C to 60 °C**; extended units from **–40 °C to +85 °C**.
- **Memory Retention:** Lithium battery backup sustains RAM for **5 to 10 years** typical.
- **Boolean Execution Time:** Typically **0.1 µs to 1 µs per instruction** in modern processors.
- **Isolation Voltage:** Optocoupler isolation rated at **1500 V RMS** between field and logic sides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Recap — Module 4 Anchor:**
> PLC Architecture = **CPU + Memory + I/O + Power Supply + Communication**, organized around the **cyclic scan principle** (Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of PLC Architecture

A PLC is built from five inter-locking functional blocks. Let us dissect each pillar in academic depth.

#### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU)

The CPU is the **executive brain** of the PLC. It is typically constructed around a **microprocessor or microcontroller** (e.g., Motorola 68000, Intel x86, ARM Cortex-M, or proprietary ASICs) accompanied by supporting logic circuitry.

**Sub-components of the CPU:**

- **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs Boolean operations (AND, OR, NOT), arithmetic (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV), and word-level data manipulations.
- **Control Unit (CU):** Decodes the stored instructions and orchestrates the fetch–decode–execute sequence. Generates timing and control signals for the bus architecture.
- **Registers (Accumulator, Status Register, Program Counter, Stack Pointer):** High-speed internal memory used for transient operand storage and instruction sequencing.
- **Address Bus, Data Bus, Control Bus:** The three-bus system that interconnects CPU with memory and I/O subsystems. Standard widths are **8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the CPU matters in real time:** The scan cycle is hard-bounded by the CPU's instruction execution speed. A 16-bit CPU can address **65,536 (2^16)** unique I/O points/memory words; a 32-bit CPU extends this to **4,294,967,296 (2^32)**.

#### 2.1.2 Memory Unit

The memory subsystem is functionally partitioned into two distinct regions:

**A. System Memory (Firmware Memory)**
- Stores the **executive firmware** (operating system, scan engine, I/O driver routines).
- Implemented in **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** or **Flash EEPROM** — non-volatile, factory/programmer-loaded.
- Contains the **ladder-logic interpreter**, instruction decoder, and communication protocol stacks.

**B. User Memory (Application Memory)**
- Stores the **user program** (ladder logic, function block, structured text).
- Sub-divided into:
  - **Program Memory:** Holds the compiled application code. Often in **Flash EEPROM** or **battery-backed RAM**.
  - **Data Memory (Working Memory / Register Memory):** Stores **timers (T)**, **counters (C)**, **internal relays (M)**, **data registers (D)**, and **input/output image tables (I/Q)**. Implemented in **volatile RAM** with battery backup.

**Memory Capacity Units:**
- Small PLC: **1 KB to 8 KB** of user memory.
- Mid-range PLC: **16 KB to 256 KB**.
- Large PLC: **1 MB to 64 MB** with extended data logging.

#### 2.1.3 Input/Output (I/O) System

The I/O system is the **PLC's interface to the physical world**. It provides electrical isolation, signal conditioning, and addressing for every field device.

**A. Input Modules — Categories:**
- **Digital (Discrete) Inputs (DI):** Accept ON/OFF signals from switches, pushbuttons, limit switches, proximity sensors, photoelectric sensors. Voltage levels: **24 V DC**, **110 V AC**, **220 V AC**, or **TTL 5 V DC**.
- **Analog Inputs (AI):** Accept continuously variable signals from sensors (temperature, pressure, flow, level). Standard ranges: **0–10 V DC**, **±10 V DC**, **4–20 mA current loop**, **0–20 mA**, **RTD/thermocouple** signals.
- **Specialty Inputs:** High-speed counters (HSC), interrupts, encoder inputs (quadrature decoding), serial/parallel data inputs.

**B. Output Modules — Categories:**
- **Digital Outputs (DO):** Drive solenoids, contactors, motor starters, indicator lamps. Switching technologies: **transistor (DC, 0.5–2 A)**, **triac (AC)**, **relay (electromechanical, 2–10 A)**.
- **Analog Outputs (AO):** Produce **0–10 V**, **4–20 mA** signals to drive variable-frequency drives (VFDs), proportional valves, and chart recorders.
- **Specialty Outputs:** Pulse-width modulation (PWM), stepper motor pulse trains, proportional-integral-derivative (PID) control outputs.

**C. Isolation Mechanism:**
Every I/O module employs **optical isolation** (optocouplers) or **transformer isolation** to protect the low-voltage logic side from the harsh, electrically noisy field side. The isolation barrier is typically rated at **1500 V RMS minimum**.

**D. I/O Addressing Convention:**
- Allen-Bradley (Rockwell): `I:1/00` (Input, Rack 1, Slot 0, Terminal 0), `O:2/05` (Output, Rack 2, Slot 5).
- Siemens S7: `I0.0`, `Q0.1`, `MW10`, `DB1.DBX2.0`.
- Mitsubishi MELSEC: `X0`, `Y1`, `M100`, `D200`.
- Schneider Modicon: `%I0.0.0`, `%Q0.0.0`, `%MW100`.

#### 2.1.4 Power Supply Unit (PSU)

The PSU converts the incoming **mains AC** (typically **120 V AC / 230 V AC at 50/60 Hz** or **24 V DC**) into the **regulated low-voltage DC** rails required by the CPU, memory, and I/O logic.

- Typical internal DC rails: **+5 V DC** (logic), **+12 V DC / –12 V DC** (analog circuitry, RS-232 drivers), **+24 V DC** (sensor power).
- Most PSUs include **over-voltage, over-current, and short-circuit protection**.
- Many PSUs also provide a **24 V DC sensor power output** to energize field transducers.
- Hold-up time on power loss: typically **10–20 ms** to allow orderly shutdown.

#### 2.1.5 Programming and Communication Interface

**A. Programming Device:**
Historically a **dedicated hand-held programmer (HHP)** or **industrial PC** running vendor-specific software (e.g., RSLogix 500, STEP 7, GX Works, TIA Portal). Communication via **RS-232 serial**, **USB**, or **Ethernet**.

**B. Communication Ports:**
- **Serial Ports:** RS-232, RS-422, RS-485.
- **Industrial Fieldbuses:** Profibus, Profinet, Modbus RTU/TCP, EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet, CANopen.
- **HMI Port:** Dedicated port for connecting operator panels (Human-Machine Interfaces).
- **Networking:** Multiple PLCs networked for **distributed control** (master-slave, peer-to-peer, SCADA integration).

### 2.2 The Scan Cycle — The Heartbeat of PLC Architecture

The PLC executes a **deterministic, repetitive scan cycle** comprising four sequential phases:

1. **Input Scan (Read Phase):** The CPU reads the **physical state of every input terminal** and copies it into the **Input Image Table (IIT)** — a dedicated region of RAM. This snapshot is used for the *entire* program scan, ensuring logical consistency.
2. **Program Scan (Execute Phase):** The CPU executes the user program **instruction-by-instruction**, top-to-bottom, left-to-right (in ladder logic). It reads inputs from the **IIT** (not the physical terminals) and writes intermediate results to the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.
3. **Output Scan (Write Phase):** After the program completes, the CPU transfers the contents of the **OIT** to the **physical output terminals** simultaneously, energizing/de-energizing field devices.
4. **Housekeeping (Overhead Phase):** Communication processing, self-diagnostics, memory checks, timer/counter updates, and error logging. Typically consumes **5–10%** of total scan time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Value** | **Units** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | ms | Sum of all four scan phases |
| I/O Scan Time | $T_{io} = N_{io} \times t_{per\text{-}pt}$ | ms | Where $N_{io}$ = number of I/O points, $t_{per\text{-}pt}$ = per-point service time (typically $\sim 1\,\mu s$) |
| Program Scan Time | $T_{prog} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_{i}$ | ms | Sum of execution times of $k$ instructions |
| Boolean Instruction Time | $t_{logic} \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 1$ | $\mu s$ | Typical per-instruction execution |
| Total Memory Required | $M_{total} = M_{prog} + M_{data}$ | bytes / words | Program memory + data memory |
| I/O Word Addressing | $N_{words} = \lceil N_{points} / 16 \rceil$ | words | 16 I/O points per word (16-bit CPU) |
| 16-bit Address Range | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | locations | Maximum addressable memory words |
| 32-bit Address Range | $2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ | locations | Maximum addressable memory words |
| Resolution of Analog Input | $R = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | V/bit | Where $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage, $n$ = ADC bits (typically 12 or 16) |
| 4–20 mA Current Resolution | $R_{mA} = \dfrac{16\,\text{mA}}{2^{n}}$ | mA/bit | Industrial standard current loop |
| Response Time (worst case) | $T_{resp} = 2 \times T_{scan}$ | ms | Detected next scan + acted on the following scan |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Scan Time Computation

**Problem Statement:** A medium-scale PLC has **128 digital inputs** and **64 digital outputs**. The per-point service time is **2 µs**. The user program contains **5000 logic instructions**, each executing in **0.8 µs**. The housekeeping overhead consumes **3 ms** per scan. Compute the **total scan time**.

**Given Data:**
- $N_{in} = 128$ digital inputs
- $N_{out} = 64$ digital outputs
- $t_{per\text{-}pt} = 2\,\mu s = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{ms}$
- $N_{instr} = 5000$ instructions
- $t_{instr} = 0.8\,\mu s = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{ms}$
- $T_{house} = 3\,\text{ms}$

**Step 1: Compute the Input Scan Time.**

$$
T_{in} = N_{in} \times t_{per\text{-}pt}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_{in} = 128 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{ms})
$$

$$
T_{in} = 256 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{ms} = 0.256\,\text{ms}
$$

The input scan requires reading 128 input terminals at 2 µs per point.

**Step 2: Compute the Output Scan Time.**

$$
T_{out} = N_{out} \times t_{per\text{-}pt}
$$

$$
T_{out} = 64 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{ms})
$$

$$
T_{out} = 128 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{ms} = 0.128\,\text{ms}
$$

The output scan writes to 64 output terminals at the same per-point service rate.

**Step 3: Compute the Program Scan Time.**

$$
T_{prog} = N_{instr} \times t_{instr}
$$

$$
T_{prog} = 5000 \times (0.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{ms})
$$

$$
T_{prog} = 4000 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{ms} = 4.000\,\text{ms}
$$

5000 instructions each take 0.8 µs, yielding a 4 ms program execution window.

**Step 4: Sum all four phases to obtain the total scan time.**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 0.256 + 4.000 + 0.128 + 3.000
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 7.384\,\text{ms}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{T_{scan} \approx 7.38\,\text{ms}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 7.38 ms scan time is excellent for most discrete-manufacturing applications (e.g., bottling lines, conveyor sorting), where event frequencies rarely exceed **100–200 Hz**. However, for **high-speed packaging** (kHz-range counting) or **motion control** (sub-millisecond loops), the program size would need to shrink, or a **dedicated high-speed counter module** would be added.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worst-Case Input-to-Output Response Time

**Problem Statement:** A conveyor-belt photoelectric sensor is wired to input `I0.0` of a PLC with a scan time of **7.38 ms** (from Derivation 1). The PLC program energizes output `Q0.0`, which drives a sorting solenoid. Determine the **worst-case response time** $T_{resp}$ from sensor actuation to solenoid energization.

**Conceptual Setup:**
The input change may occur **just after** the input scan has copied the input image. The PLC will therefore not detect the change until the **next scan's input phase**. The output, in turn, is updated only at the **end of the program scan**, which is the scan *after* detection. Hence, in the worst case, the response spans **two full scan cycles**.

**Step 1: Write the worst-case response equation.**

$$
T_{resp,\text{ worst}} = 2 \times T_{scan}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $T_{scan} = 7.38$ ms.**

$$
T_{resp,\text{ worst}} = 2 \times 7.38\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{resp,\text{ worst}} = 14.76\,\text{ms}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{T_{resp,\text{ worst}} \approx 14.76\,\text{ms}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The best-case response (input just-missed at the input scan, detected the very next instant) is approximately **one scan time (~7.38 ms)**. The average response is therefore **~11 ms**, and the worst-case is **~15 ms**. For a conveyor moving at **1 m/s**, the product travels **15 mm** between sensing and actuation — this sets the **minimum physical spacing** between the sensor and the sorting actuator.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Analog Input Resolution

**Problem Statement:** A 16-bit ADC is used in an analog input module with a full-scale range of **0–10 V DC**. Compute the resolution in **volts per bit** and the percentage resolution per least-significant bit (LSB).

**Step 1: Write the resolution formula.**

$$
R = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $V_{FS} = 10$ V and $n = 16$.**

$$
R = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{2^{16}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{65{,}536}
$$

$$
R = 1.5259 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V/bit} = 152.59\,\mu\text{V/bit}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the percentage resolution (per LSB).**

$$
\% R = \frac{1}{2^{n}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\% R = \frac{1}{65{,}536} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\% R = 1.526 \times 10^{-3}\,\% \approx 0.00153\,\%
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{R = 152.59\,\mu\text{V/bit} \quad \text{and} \quad \%R \approx 0.00153\,\% \text{ per LSB}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** 16-bit resolution is more than sufficient for industrial process control, where typical accuracy requirements are **0.1% to 0.5% of full scale**. A 12-bit ADC (4096 steps) would give **2.44 mV/bit** — adequate for temperature monitoring but marginal for precision pressure or flow measurement.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Memory Sizing for Timer and Counter Banks

**Problem Statement:** A PLC application requires **256 timers** (each occupying 1 word = 16 bits for the preset/current value) and **128 counters** (each occupying 1 word). Each timer also needs a **1-bit contact (relay flag)**. How many **16-bit memory words** must be reserved?

**Step 1: Timer word memory.**

$$
M_{T} = N_{T} \times W_{T} = 256 \times 1 = 256\,\text{words}
$$

**Step 2: Counter word memory.**

$$
M_{C} = N_{C} \times W_{C} = 128 \times 1 = 128\,\text{words}
$$

**Step 3: Timer contact-bit memory (packed into 16-bit words).**

$$
M_{T\text{-}bits} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{T}}{16} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{256}{16} \right\rceil = 16\,\text{words}
$$

**Step 4: Total memory.**

$$
M_{total} = M_{T} + M_{C} + M_{T\text{-}bits}
$$

$$
M_{total} = 256 + 128 + 16
$$

$$
M_{total} = 400\,\text{words}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{M_{total} = 400\,\text{16-bit words} = 800\,\text{bytes}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This memory map must be reserved in the PLC's **Data Memory (D-register or M-register) region** during program planning. Vendors like Siemens and Allen-Bradley provide automatic memory allocation when timers/counters are declared in the programming software.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the term "Scan Cycle" in a PLC. List its four phases in order.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Scan Cycle** is the deterministic, repetitive sequence of operations a PLC executes continuously to acquire inputs, run the user program, update outputs, and perform internal housekeeping. It is the fundamental operating principle that distinguishes a PLC from a conventional computer.

The four phases in sequential order are:

1. **Input Scan (Read Phase):** All physical input states are read and copied into the Input Image Table (IIT) in RAM.
2. **Program Scan (Execute Phase):** The user program (ladder logic) is executed instruction-by-instruction, using the IIT for inputs and writing to the Output Image Table (OIT).
3. **Output Scan (Write Phase):** The OIT contents are transferred simultaneously to the physical output terminals.
4. **Housekeeping (Overhead Phase):** Communication handling, self-diagnostics, timer/counter update, and error logging are performed.

The entire cycle repeats indefinitely while the PLC is in RUN mode, with each cycle typically taking **1 to 20 ms**.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between RAM and ROM in the context of PLC memory architecture. State one specific use of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **RAM (Random Access Memory)** | **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** |
|---|---|---|
| Volatility | **Volatile** — loses data on power failure | **Non-volatile** — retains data permanently |
| Write/Read | Read/Write supported | Read-only (factory or mask programmed) |
| Speed | Very fast access (~ns) | Fast access, but typically slower than RAM |
| Use in PLC | Stores the **Input Image Table (IIT)**, **Output Image Table (OIT)**, **timers**, **counters**, and **data registers** that change every scan. Often battery-backed. | Stores the **executive firmware** (operating system, ladder interpreter, I/O drivers) that controls the PLC's internal operations. |

> **Specific Use:** RAM is used for storing **timer/counter current values** that update every scan, while ROM holds the **PLC's scan engine firmware**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a PLC** and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLC has **256 digital inputs and 128 digital outputs**. The per-point service time is **3 µs**. The user program consists of **7500 instructions**, each executing in **0.6 µs**. The housekeeping phase takes **4 ms**. Calculate the **total scan time** and the **worst-case input-to-output response time**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution to (a):**

A PLC consists of five functional blocks:

**Block 1 — Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Converts the incoming AC mains (230 V AC, 50 Hz) into regulated DC voltages (+5 V, ±12 V, +24 V) required by the CPU, memory, and I/O logic. It also provides 24 V DC sensor power to field devices. The PSU includes over-voltage and short-circuit protection.

**Block 2 — Central Processing Unit (CPU):** The brain of the PLC, comprising the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), Control Unit (CU), and registers. The ALU executes Boolean and arithmetic operations, while the CU decodes instructions and generates timing signals. The CPU orchestrates the scan cycle and runs the user program.

**Block 3 — Memory Unit:** Divided into **System Memory (ROM/Flash)** that stores the firmware and **User Memory (RAM)** that holds the user program, the Input/Output Image Tables, timers, counters, and data registers. Battery backup preserves RAM contents during power loss.

**Block 4 — Input/Output Modules:** These interface the CPU with field devices. **Input modules** (digital and analog) accept signals from sensors and switches, providing optical isolation and signal conditioning. **Output modules** (relay, transistor, triac) drive actuators, solenoids, and motors. Each module occupies an I/O address in the image table.

**Block 5 — Programming and Communication Interface:** Allows the user to upload, download, and monitor the program via a programming device (PC or hand-held programmer). Communication ports (RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet, Profinet) enable networking with HMIs, SCADA systems, and other PLCs.

*(The neat block diagram may be referenced from SECTION 4.1 above.)*

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $N_{in} = 256$, $N_{out} = 128$, $t_{per\text{-}pt} = 3\,\mu s = 0.003\,ms$
- $N_{instr} = 7500$, $t_{instr} = 0.6\,\mu s = 0.0006\,ms$
- $T_{house} = 4\,ms$

**Step 1: Input Scan Time**

$$
T_{in} = 256 \times 0.003 = 0.768\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2: Output Scan Time**

$$
T_{out} = 128 \times 0.003 = 0.384\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Program Scan Time**

$$
T_{prog} = 7500 \times 0.0006 = 4.500\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4: Total Scan Time**

$$
T_{scan} = 0.768 + 4.500 + 0.384 + 4.000
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 9.652\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5: Worst-Case Response Time**

$$
T_{resp,\text{ worst}} = 2 \times T_{scan} = 2 \times 9.652 = 19.304\,\text{ms}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{T_{scan} \approx 9.65\,\text{ms} \quad \text{and} \quad T_{resp,\text{ worst}} \approx 19.30\,\text{ms}}
$$

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **CPU and Memory architecture** of a PLC in detail. Discuss the role of the **Input Image Table (IIT)** and **Output Image Table (OIT)** in the scan cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An analog input module uses a **14-bit ADC** with a full-scale range of **0–10 V DC**. Compute the **voltage resolution per bit** and the **percentage resolution**. If the module is configured for a **4–20 mA** input instead, find the **current resolution per bit** and the **current corresponding to a digital output of 8192**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution to (a):**

The **CPU** is the executive heart of the PLC, built around a microprocessor (or microcontroller) and comprising three sub-systems:

1. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs Boolean operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR) and arithmetic operations (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV) on 8/16/32-bit operands. It also handles comparisons, shifts, and rotations.
2. **Control Unit (CU):** Fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and generates the timing/control signals that drive the address, data, and control buses. It implements the fetch–decode–execute cycle in synchronization with the system clock.
3. **Registers:** High-speed internal storage, including the **Accumulator** (for ALU operands), **Program Counter (PC)** (address of the next instruction), **Stack Pointer (SP)** (for subroutine/interrupt nesting), and **Status Register** (carrying zero, carry, and overflow flags).

The **Memory Architecture** is split into:

- **System Memory (ROM/Flash):** Non-volatile, holds the executive firmware, scan engine, and communication stacks.
- **User Program Memory (Flash/EEPROM/battery-backed RAM):** Stores the compiled ladder/FBD/ST program.
- **Data Memory (RAM):** Holds the IIT, OIT, timers, counters, internal relays, and data registers.

**Role of the IIT and OIT:**

- The **Input Image Table (IIT)** is a dedicated region of RAM that stores a *snapshot* of all physical input states, refreshed once per scan during the input phase. The user program reads from the IIT (not directly from the field), ensuring **logical consistency** — all instructions in a given scan see the same input values, even if a physical input changes mid-scan.
- The **Output Image Table (OIT)** is a complementary RAM region where the program writes computed output states during the program scan. Only after the program completes are the OIT contents transferred *simultaneously* to the physical output terminals during the output scan phase.

This **memory-mapped I/O** architecture provides **deterministic, jitter-free** behavior — a critical requirement for industrial real-time control.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Part (i): Voltage resolution (0–10 V range, 14-bit ADC)**

$$
R_V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{2^{14}} = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{16{,}384}
$$

$$
R_V = 6.1035 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V/bit} = 610.35\,\mu\text{V/bit}
$$

**Percentage resolution:**

$$
\%R = \frac{1}{2^{14}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{16{,}384} \times 100\% = 0.0061\%
$$

**Part (ii): Current resolution (4–20 mA range, 14-bit ADC)**

The current range is $\Delta I = 20 - 4 = 16$ mA. The resolution is:

$$
R_I = \frac{16\,\text{mA}}{2^{14}} = \frac{16\,\text{mA}}{16{,}384}
$$

$$
R_I = 9.7656 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mA/bit} = 0.9766\,\mu\text{A/bit}
$$

**Part (iii): Current corresponding to digital output 8192**

Using the linear transfer function:

$$
I = 4\,\text{mA} + \left( \frac{8192}{2^{14}} \right) \times 16\,\text{mA}
$$

$$
I = 4 + \left( \frac{8192}{16{,}384} \right) \times 16
$$

$$
I = 4 + (0.5) \times 16
$$

$$
I = 4 + 8 = 12\,\text{mA}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{R_V \approx 610.35\,\mu\text{V/bit}, \quad \%R \approx 0.0061\%, \quad R_I \approx 0.977\,\mu\text{A/bit}, \quad I_{8192} = 12\,\text{mA}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PLC Architecture Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RAM and ROM roles:** Students often state that the *user program* is stored in ROM. **Wrong** — it is stored in Flash/EEPROM or battery-backed RAM, *not* ROM. ROM holds the *firmware*.
> 2. **Forgetting to include housekeeping in scan time:** Many students compute $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out}$ only, losing 1–2 marks by omitting $T_{house}$.
> 3. **Using $2 \times T_{scan}$ for response time without explanation:** Examiners expect you to **derive** the factor of 2 (worst-case: input just missed, detected next scan, output written the scan after). A bare formula without justification loses marks.
> 4. **Mixing up I/O image tables:** IIT = inputs; OIT = outputs. Reversing these is a classic error.
> 5. **Unit errors in analog resolution:** Writing the resolution in V without converting ADC counts ($2^n$) properly is a frequent mistake.
> 6. **Forgetting the offset in 4–20 mA:** The range is **not** 0–20 mA — there is a **4 mA "live zero" offset** that must be added back when computing the current from a digital code.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PLC** = **P**rogrammable **L**ogic **C**ontroller; a rugged, real-time, industrial digital computer for machine and process control.
- **Five Architectural Pillars:** **CPU + Memory + I/O + Power Supply + Communication/Programming Interface.**
- **CPU** contains the **ALU, Control Unit, Registers**, and uses a three-bus system (Address, Data, Control).
- **Memory** is split into **System Memory (ROM/Flash)** for firmware and **User Memory (RAM/Flash)** for program + data, with the **IIT and OIT** in RAM.
- **I/O Modules** are **optically isolated** and categorized as **Digital (DI/DO)** and **Analog (AI/AO)**, with specialty modules for high-speed counting and motion.
- **Power Supply** converts AC mains to regulated **+5 V, ±12 V, +24 V DC** rails.
- **Scan Cycle = Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping**, repeated indefinitely.
- **Worst-case response time** $T_{resp} = 2 \times T_{scan}$ — critical for sizing conveyor/spacing in high-speed lines.
- **Analog resolution** $R = V_{FS} / 2^{n}$ for voltage, and $R = (I_{max} - I_{min}) / 2^{n}$ for current loops.
- **IIT/OIT pattern** ensures **deterministic, consistent** logic evaluation within each scan — a defining feature of PLC architecture.
- **Industrial standard current loop is 4–20 mA** (not 0–20 mA) — remember the 4 mA "live zero" offset for **fault detection** (a reading of 0 mA indicates a broken wire).
- **Memory sizing** in 16-bit words: pack boolean flags (1 bit) into 16-bit words using $\lceil N/16 \rceil$.
- The PLC is the **backbone of SCADA, DCS, and Industry 4.0** — mastering its architecture is foundational for any industrial automation career.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Ladder Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT613 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PLC Ladder Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IEC 61131-3):**
> A **PLC Ladder Diagram (LD)** is a standardized graphical programming language defined under **IEC 61131-3**, used to develop control logic for Programmable Logic Controllers. It is constructed from two vertical **power rails** (L1 and L2) connected by horizontal **rungs** containing graphical symbols such as contacts, coils, timers, and counters that represent logical and sequential operations.

In plain engineering terms, a ladder diagram is the *visual blueprint* of the control logic stored inside the PLC. Every line you draw on the screen becomes a set of boolean instructions executed by the CPU in real time.

## 1.1 The Intuitive "Old Panel vs New Screen" Analogy

Imagine the control cabinet of an industrial motor starter from the 1970s. Inside the cabinet, you would physically see:

- **Push-buttons** (green start, red stop) wired in series/parallel.
- **Relay coils** that energize when current flows.
- **Contactors** that close to feed the motor.
- **A spaghetti of copper wires** connecting them all.

Now imagine you could **replace every wire with a software instruction** and **every relay with a virtual contact/coil**, while keeping the *exact same schematic look*. That is precisely what a **Ladder Diagram** does.

| Physical World (Old Panel) | PLC World (Ladder Diagram) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Physical push-button | Input module address (e.g., I:0/0) |
| Relay coil on DIN rail | Output coil (e.g., O:0/0) |
| Copper wire connection | Logical connection in the rung |
| Relay contact (NO/NC) | Virtual contact (examine ON / examine OFF) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ladder"?** The diagram visually resembles a **ladder**, with the two vertical power rails acting as the *uprights* and the horizontal rungs acting as the *steps*. Power flow (logic continuity) is evaluated from the left rail to the right rail.

## 1.2 Historical Significance

Ladder logic was originally developed to allow **electricians and maintenance technicians** (who understood relay schematics) to program PLCs *without learning a new language*. Today it remains the **most widely used PLC language** in manufacturing, with a typical scan-and-execute cycle of **10–50 milliseconds**.

## 1.3 Industry Domains Using Ladder Logic

- Automotive assembly lines
- Bottling and packaging plants
- Water/wastewater treatment SCADA systems
- Elevator and escalator controllers
- Material handling conveyor systems
- Food processing batch plants
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Ladder Diagram

Every ladder diagram is composed of **four fundamental building blocks** that map directly to the **boolean algebra** of digital logic.

| Block | Symbol | Boolean Equivalent | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Normally Open (NO) Contact | --\| \|-- | $X$ (Pass through if TRUE) | Conducting only when input is HIGH |
| Normally Closed (NC) Contact | --\|/\|-- | $\overline{X}$ (Pass through if FALSE) | Conducting only when input is LOW |
| Output / Energize Coil | --( )-- | $Y = f(\text{inputs})$ | Energizes the output bit when rung is TRUE |
| Latch / Unlatch Coil | --(L)-- / --(U)-- | $Y = Y \lor S ; \; Y = Y \land \overline{R}$ | Retentive memory for start-stop logic |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Ladder Logic:** A rung is *TRUE* (energized) when there is **continuous logical continuity** between the left rail and the right rail. The output coil on that rung is therefore **energized**.

## 2.2 Logical Operations in Ladder

The two physical arrangements in a rung map cleanly to the two fundamental boolean operations:

### 2.2.1 Series Connection (Logical AND)

Placing two NO contacts **in series** means *both* must be TRUE for current to flow.

$$ Y = A \cdot B \cdot C $$

### 2.2.2 Parallel Connection (Logical OR)

Placing two NO contacts **in parallel** means *either* being TRUE is sufficient for continuity.

$$ Y = A + B + C $$

### 2.2.3 Mixed Logic (Combination)

A typical rung contains a mix that corresponds to **Sum-of-Products (SOP)** boolean form.

$$ Y = (A \cdot B) + (C \cdot \overline{D}) $$

## 2.3 Advanced Ladder Elements

| Element | Purpose | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Timer (TON, TOF, RTO) | Time-based delays | Process sequencing |
| Counter (CTU, CTD) | Event counting | Batch control |
| Compare Block | $(=,\; >,\; <,\; \ge,\; \le,\; \ne)$ | Analog threshold checks |
| Move (MOV) | Register transfer | Data acquisition |
| Math Block (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV) | Arithmetic | Recipe management |
| Master Control Relay (MCR) | Sectional energization | Emergency / zone control |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equivalence table is **examinable** under Module 4 of OERAT613. Memorize the entire mapping.

| Logic Gate | Boolean Expression | Equivalent Ladder Construct |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| AND (2-input) | $Y = A \cdot B$ | A and B contacts in **series** |
| OR (2-input) | $Y = A + B$ | A and B contacts in **parallel** |
| NOT | $Y = \overline{A}$ | Single **NC** contact |
| NAND | $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | A and B in **series**, then **NC** contact on output branch |
| NOR | $Y = \overline{A + B}$ | A and B in **parallel**, then **NC** contact on output branch |
